Liberals in the West see chance for revival; Leadership race

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-west-see-chance-revival-leadership-race/docview/3156275001/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+in+the+West+see+chance+for+revival%3B+Leadership+race&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The past several years have been lean times for Liberals west of Ontario, but insiders are hoping a spirited leadership race that happens to overweight western Liberal constituencies could help the party rebuild bridges in the region, after nearly a decade of frosty relations under Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's leadership.
"I'm already seeing tons of excitement for the upcoming leadership campaign," said Kent Hehr, a lifelong Alberta Liberal and ex-Calgary MP.
"Liberals around here have been shell-shocked for the last little while, but they're looking rejuvenated of late," Hehr said. "It's been nice to see." (Hehr resigned from Trudeau's cabinet in 2018 amid sexual harassment allegations.)
Vancouver-based Liberal strategist Mark Marissen noted the party's leadership vote rules, which give each electoral district 100 points, regardless of the number of members, tilt the system in favour of western ridings with smaller membership bases.
"The one thing that does happen is that you hear from everybody across the country ... every riding has the same weight in this leadership race," said Marissen.
Hehr estimates there are 1,000 Liberal members in his downtown Calgary riding. He expects this number to grow.
Core y Hogan, a Calgary-based strategist and former executive director of the Alberta Liberal Party, noted the leadership race's biggest names so far all have roots in Western Canada.
"Mark Carney, Chrystia Freeland and Christy Clark - all three of them grew up out West," Hogan said.
Carney, a former central banker turned Wall Street heavyweight, is set to launch his campaign in his hometown of Edmonton on Thursday. Clark, after organizing a planned leadership campaign, announced Tuesday she had changed her mind, saying her French was too weak. Freeland, whose resignation from cabinet in December preceded the prime minister's decision to quit, was raised in Peace River, Alta., although she now lives in Toronto. She is reportedly planning to enter the race in the coming days.
North Vancouver, B.C., MP Jonathan Wilkinson is also rumoured to be weighing a leadership run.
Hehr said the rules give an edge to better-organized campaigns with stronger ties to local riding associations.
"You have to have people on your team who can pick up the phone and ask, 'Hey, can you search your database for a guy I met at the gas station in Zama City, (Alta.)?'" Hehr said.
Ex-Vancouver Liberal MP and former federal cabinet minister Ujjal Dosanjh said much of the blame for the party's collapse in Western Canada leads to Trudeau.
"(Trudeau's) really severed all of his relationships with traditional Liberals before he came in," Dosanjh said. "He had new people that he recruited and put into those positions."
National Post rmohamed@postmedia.com

Document 197 of 500

Political will key to fixing addiction emergency

Author: Mckenney, Carine; Leiper, Jeff

Publication info: Mckenney, Carine; Leiper, Jeff. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/political-will-key-fixing-addiction-emergency/docview/3156274987/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Political+will+key+to+fixing+addiction+emergency&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Mckenney%2C+Carine%3BLeiper%2C+Jeff&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Centretown received good news last week when the Ontario government agreed to fund the Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hub at Somerset West Community Health Centre.
The program - which promises to provide treatment to those suffering addictions - takes the place of the consumption and treatment services that saved thousands of lives over the past several years, but which the provincial government has determined to close over opposition by communities and health care providers.
The forced shutdown of the Somerset West Community Health Centre consumption and treatment service will inevitably lead to more deaths, more paramedic calls to attend to people overdosing outside of a safer consumption centre with trained staff, and more crowding in Ottawa's emergency rooms that will ultimately deal with the rise in overdoses. Ottawa's central communities have been heavily affected by the perfect storm of a poisoned drug supply, lack of affordable housing and mental health supports.
In the absence of necessary supports to address opioid addictions issues, the community will have no choice but to rely to an increasing degree on the Alternate Neighbourhood Crisis Response (ANCHOR) program. We're fortunate that the City has funded this pilot program to divert police calls that should be dealt with by mental health professionals. But as the recent recommendations stemming from the inquest into the death of Abdirahman Abdi make clear, we need to ensure that while ANCHOR becomes a backstop to housing and health care gaps, it does not lose focus on its raison d'être - ensuring that the circumstances that led to Abdi's death are avoided in future.
ANCHOR is a mobile mental health team providing support to people in crisis due to mental health or addictions. It's an excellent replacement for police intervention, as it brings trained mental health professionals where they're needed, rather than treating health issues as a criminal matter.
The police-custody homicide of Abdi, an Ottawa resident with severe mental health issues, shone a light on the problems caused when police are called on to respond to mental health issues.
But while ANCHOR provides a much-needed alternative to residents who want to help their neighbours, tying up the phones and the response team when someone witnesses another person injecting drugs in their neighbourhood has the potential to divert resources and to distract from the program's core mandate. The City would be smart to provide better communications on when and why to call ANCHOR, providing clarity about this important new service.
And ANCHOR is providing relief to those already in crisis. What's lacking - and has been for a decade or more - is a commitment to provide long-term solutions for the crisis in our neighbourhoods.
The first step is affordable and supportive housing. Temporary shelters are over capacity and the wait time for social housing in Ottawa can be five years or more. Without new provincial funding, the City and its taxpayers are on the hook to be the sole source of new affordable housing in Ottawa.
This is unacceptable. The provincial and federal governments are responsible for housing and they have the ability to help solve our ongoing housing crisis by working with the city and our housing providers to build new affordable and supportive housing.
We need, desperately, to solve the housing emergency and opioid drug crisis. We need to build housing for people who have few or no resources, provide treatment for those suffering addictions, and to offer better support to residents with mental health illnesses.
Those are the straightforward solutions, well-recognized by experts worldwide. We're only lacking the political will. ANCHOR is a desperately needed service in a city that too often sees residents in mental health crisis harmed during interactions with police. To be fully successful, its mission needs to be clear - in both the minds of policy-makers and of residents. Catherine McKenney is a former Ottawa city councillor and current NDP provincial candidate. for Ottawa Centre. Jeff Leiper is the Councillor for Kitchissippi Ward.

Subject: Emergency medical care; Mental health; Addictions; Affordable housing; Medical personnel; Narcotics

Document 198 of 500

Premiers, PM hash it out over U.S. tariff threats; Mull response

Author: Taylor, Stephanie; Trépanier, Antoine; Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie; Trépanier, Antoine; Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-pm-hash-out-over-u-s-tariff-threats-mull/docview/3156274685/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers%2C+PM+hash+it+out+over+U.S.+tariff+threats%3B+Mull+response&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie%3BTr%C3%A9panier%2C+Antoine%3BNardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The chair of Canada's premiers had a message for Americans walking into Wednesday's meeting with the prime minister, called to discuss the country's plan should U.S. president-elect Donald Trump make good on his threat to slap a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian goods.
"Canada is not for sale," were the words emblazoned on the hat worn by Ontario Premier Doug Ford - a nod to Trump's frequent comments of Canada being a "51st state."
Canadian officials initially brushed the statement off as a joke. But with Trump's swearing-in only four days away and the threat of his incoming administration applying across-the-board tariffs on Canadian products becoming a worrying inevitably, it's clear leaders are increasingly losing their sense of humour.
"President Trump tries to divide us," Ford told reporters. "The country comes first and we will continue to fight these tariffs and we will use every tool at our disposal."
Agreeing on what exactly those tools might be topped the discussion Wednesday when the premiers sat down with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, the first faceto-face meeting the group has had since Trudeau announced his resignation on Jan. 6.
With talk of Canada preparing a package of retaliatory tariffs and other counter measures, western premiers say they want the idea floated by some, including Ford, of blocking energy exports declared off the table, same with applying any export tariffs.
"It would be the most divisive conversation that this nation would ever have," Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe warned.
On the weekend, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith made that case publicly after meeting with Trump at his Mar-a-Lago estate in Florida, which raised eyebrows among Canadian officials questioning how unified a front premiers were presenting.
Speaking before Wednesday's meeting, Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew emphasized the importance of maintaining unity, when asked about the idea of blocking energy exports.
"The role that I want to have is to urge unity and that we have a comprehensive approach."
Canada's energy sector is the queen in the game of chess, Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey said.
"We don't need to expose our queen too early. The opposition needs to know that our queen exists, but they don't need to know what we are going to do with the queen.
"Let's see what the tariffs are first and have a proportional, but a strong response to the United States," Furey added.
Speaking at the start of the meeting, Trudeau said there were two main themes on the agenda. First, opportunities to expand and deepen partnerships with the Americans, and then Canada's response to potential tariffs.
"It's an important moment for Canadians to see us coming together to work to deal with the most important relationship."
Participating on the federal side was Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, Foreign Affairs Mélanie Joly and Kristen Hillman, ambassador to the United States. Smith and B.C. Premier David Eby were joining virtually.
For weeks, federal and provincial leaders have been making the case to governors and everyday Americans, including through TV appearances, that slapping tariffs on Canadian goods would be bad for the U.S.
In an op-ed published Wednesday in The Hill, Quebec Premier François Legault argued "that tariffs of 25 per cent on exports from Canada and Mexico would reduce American GDP by at least 1 per cent over one year, not to mention inflation."
"Tariffs of 25 per cent would therefore only result in increasing the prices of beer cans, car parts, airplane parts and a host of other products for American consumers and businesses," he wrote.
He pointed out Quebec is a major supplier to the U.S. military aviation industry, such as engines, landing gear and flight simulators.
Legault also emphasized one of Quebec's greatest assets: electricity. The province exports its electricity to the U.S., including via a transmission line to New York state.
"This clean, dependable and cheap electricity is an extraordinary advantage for New Yorkers and their businesses. It lowers the cost of energy for Trump Tower, to give just one example."
Getting everyone to agree on the best approach is easier said than done, according to P.E.I. Premier Dennis King.
"It's hard to bring 13 premiers and the prime minister together and agree to the colour of Cheezies," he said. "But I do think at our root, we are about trying to do what's best for Canada, and I think we're going to try to find a very unique way to get to that today, or at least, I hope so."
As premiers assembled in Ottawa, federal Public Safety Minister David McGuinty and Immigration Minister Marc Miller told reporters the RCMP and Canada Border Services Agency were deploying additional drones, two leased Apache helicopters and surveillance towers at the Canada-U.S. border.
"These will help us to further enhance our border surveillance and our intelligence operations, again with our American partners, as further proof of our strong relationship with the United States," McGuinty told reporters.
Trump made his tariff threat after warning Canada and Mexico to take steps stop the flow of migrants into the U.S., and halt the flow of the deadly drug fentanyl, which has fuelled an overdose crisis across North America. Canadian officials say while they take Trump's concerns seriously, they reject the notion that Canada's border is where the problem lies.
National Post

Subject: Surveillance; Energy industry; Prime ministers; Energy exports & imports; Tariffs

Document 199 of 500

Bring back licence plate fees - and help end chronic homelessness

Author: Deachman, Bruce

Publication info: Deachman, Bruce. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bring-back-licence-plate-fees-help-end-chronic/docview/3156274611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bring+back+licence+plate+fees+-+and+help+end+chronic+homelessness&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Deachman%2C+Bruce&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Would you be willing to pay for your vehicle's licence-plate sticker again - a $120 annual expense for most car-and truck-owners that Premier Doug Ford's government did away with in 2022 - if it would end chronic homelessness in the province? In that context, it doesn't seem like such a huge sacrifice.
Because the $1.1 billion in yearly sticker fees that the premier forfeited, coincidentally, is the annual price tag that a recent report says would be needed above current spending to end Ontario's chronic homelessness over the next 10 years.
Otherwise, according to the report put out by the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, the ranks of province's unhoused population could explode from its current level of about 81,500 to more than 290,000 by 2035, an increase of 260 per cent.
"Sometimes you need to be hit over the head with a two-by-four to get your attention," says River Ward Coun. Riley Brockington, who serves on AMO's board of directors and executive council.
This report, he says, is that hard-hitting piece of lumber.
It's worth noting that the morethan-tripling of Ontario's homeless people assumes an economic downturn marked by higher unemployment and declining incomes and housing affordability. Yet, if figures trend at the current rates, chronic homelessness in the province is expected to double its toll by 2035 to 165,915 people.
The report, titled Municipalities Under Pressure: The Human and Financial Cost of Ontario's Homelessness Crisis, marks the first time that data from each of the province's 47 municipal services managers have been aggregated and analyzed. Some of its findings are alarming. The number of people experiencing "known" homelessness, for example - those known to the homelessness-serving system and largely including those in emergency shelters, hotels, or unsheltered locations such as in tents or outdoor spaces - has gone up by 25 per cent since 2022 alone.
The incidence of chronic homelessness, in which people experience prolonged or repeated periods of homelessness, has risen dramatically, too, tripling in less than a decade. For the first time, chronic homelessness now accounts for more than half the cases of known homelessness, rising from 22 per cent in 2016 to 51 per cent.
Children and youths, meanwhile, account for almost 25 per cent of those experiencing chronic homelessness.
"Child homelessness equals family homelessness," says Kaite Burkholder Harris, executive director of the Alliance to End Homelessness Ottawa. "And we know that that number is growing significantly in Ottawa, certainly, and all across the province."
At the same time, rural homelessness is growing at a faster rate than urban homelessness. After remaining largely unchanged from 2016 to 2021, the number of rural residents experiencing known homelessness rose from 1,858 in 2021 to 4,245 in 2024, an increase of more than 125 per cent.
The report outlines a pair of scenarios to help address homelessness. The first looks at what it would cost to reach "functional zero homelessness," meaning instances of homelessness are rare, brief and non-recurring.
That scenario calls for an additional $11 billion in spending over 10 years above the current figure the three levels of government put toward housing and homelessness programs - about $4.1 billion in 2024. The majority of that extra money would be put toward capital expenses, especially building subsidized housing.
The report's second scenario aims to address the challenge of encampments with a $2-billion investment that would stabilize housing and provide wraparound services for about 8,400 people, or just over 10 per cent of the known homeless population.
To be sure, these are large numbers, but not so enormous when you look at the province's overall budget of $214.5 billion and the federal government's roughly half-trillion. Then consider what savings might be found in health care, policing and incarceration costs if most people had homes.
Burkholder Harris wants both scenarios acted upon, but notes that getting people out of the cold is the most immediate need.
"People will die. People have died. They are going to die. It's essential, and I think people forget we can actually truly eliminate outdoor homelessness for the most part within two to four years."
But cities can't end homelessness on their own.
"This report," Brockington says, "underscores that to expect municipalities to address, finance and solve this crisis is completely unrealistic. It needs a provincial quarterback to acknowledge the depth and scope of the problem across the province."
That takes will, and the premier's announcement in December that the province will introduce legislation making it easier for municipalities and police to dismantle homeless encampments isn't encouraging. Eviction doesn't address the problem.
If Ford wants his political legacy to be little more than eliminating vehicle stickers and getting beer in convenience stores, he's all set. But if he wants to be remembered for actually lifting up people who are experiencing the greatest need, he should pay attention and act. bdeachman@postmedia.com

Document 200 of 500

Deachman: Bring back licence plate fees - and help end chronic homelessness

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/deachman-bring-back-licence-plate-fees-help-end/docview/3155941667/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Deachman%3A+Bring+back+licence+plate+fees+-+and+help+end+chronic+homelessness&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Would you be willing to pay for your vehicle's licence-plate sticker again - a $120 annual expense for most car- and truck-owners that Premier Doug Ford's government did away with in 2022 - if it would end chronic homelessness in the province? In that context, it doesn't seem like such a huge sacrifice.
Because the $1.1 billion in yearly sticker fees that the premier forfeited, coincidentally, is the annual price tag that a recent report says would be needed above current spending to end Ontario's chronic homelessness over the next 10 years.
Otherwise, according to the report put out by the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, the ranks of province's unhoused population could explode from its current level of about 81,500 to more than 290,000 by 2035, an increase of 260 per cent.
"Sometimes you need to be hit over the head with a two-by-four to get your attention," says River Ward Coun. Riley Brockington, who serves on AMO's board of directors and executive council.
This report, he says, is that hard-hitting piece of lumber.
It's worth noting that the more-than-tripling of Ontario's homeless rolls assumes an economic downturn marked by higher unemployment and declining incomes and housing affordability. Yet, if figures trend at the current rates, chronic homelessness in the province is expected to double its toll by 2035 to 165,915 people.
The report, titled Municipalities Under Pressure: The Human and Financial Cost of Ontario's Homelessness Crisis, marks the first time that data from each of the province's 47 municipal services managers has been aggregated and analyzed. Some of its findings are alarming. The number of people experiencing "known" homelessness, for example - those known to the homelessness-serving system and largely including those in emergency shelters, hotels, or unsheltered locations such as in tents or outdoor spaces - has gone up by 25 per cent since 2022 alone.
The incidence of chronic homelessness, in which people experience prolonged or repeated periods of homelessness, has risen dramatically, too, tripling in less than a decade. For the first time, chronic homelessness now accounts for more than half the cases of known homelessness, rising from 22 per cent in 2016 to 51 per cent.
Children and youths, meanwhile, account for almost 25 per cent of those experiencing chronic homelessness.
"Child homelessness equals family homelessness," says Kaite Burkholder Harris, executive director of the Alliance to End Homelessness Ottawa. "And we know that that number is growing significantly in Ottawa, certainly, and all across the province."
At the same time, rural homelessness is growing at a faster rate than urban homelessness. After remaining largely unchanged from 2016 to 2021, the number of rural residents experiencing known homelessness rose from 1,858 in 2021 to 4,245 in 2024, an increase of more than 125 per cent.
The report outlines a pair of scenarios to help address homelessness. The first looks at what it would cost to reach "functional zero homelessness," meaning that instances of homelessness are rare, brief and non-recurring.
That scenario calls for an additional $11 billion in spending over 10 years above the current figure the three levels of government put towards housing and homelessness programs - about $4.1 billion in 2024. The majority of that extra money would be put towards capital expenses, especially building subsidized housing.
The report's second scenario aims to address the challenge of encampments with a $2-billion investment that would stabilize housing and provide wraparound services for about 8,400 people, or just over 10 per cent of the known homeless population.
To be sure, these are large numbers, but not so enormous when you look at the province's overall budget of $214.5 billion and the feds' roughly half-trillion. Then consider what savings might be found in healthcare, policing and incarceration costs if most people had homes.
Burkholder Harris wants both scenarios acted upon, but notes that getting people out of the cold is the most immediate need. "People will die. People have died. They are going to die. It's essential, and I think people forget we can actually truly eliminate outdoor homelessness for the most part within two to four years."
But cities can't end homelessness on their own.
"This report," Brockington says, "underscores that to expect municipalities to address, finance and solve this crisis is completely unrealistic. It needs a provincial quarterback to acknowledge the depth and scope of the problem across the province."
That takes will, and the premier's announcement in December that the province will introduce legislation making it easier for municipalities and police to dismantle homeless encampments isn't encouraging. Eviction doesn't address the problem.
If Ford wants his political legacy to be little more than eliminating vehicle stickers and getting beer in convenience stores, he's all set. But, if he wants to be remembered for actually lifting up people who are experiencing the greatest need, he should pay attention and act.
bdeachman@postmedia.com

Ciriello: Homelessness - governments must stop working at cross purposes

Document 201 of 500

Alberta is now the real threat to Canadian unity

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-is-now-real-threat-canadian-unity/docview/3155876635/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+is+now+the+real+threat+to+Canadian+unity&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For years, it's been assumed that the biggest threat to Canadian unity comes from the Province of Quebec. It hosted a separatist terrorist movement in the 1960s and is home to the sovereigntist Parti Québécois. It held two referendums on separation, the last of which saw the country hang together by less than one per cent of the vote. Today, the party tops the polls and is threatening to hold another referendum if it takes power next year.
But the game has changed, thanks to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and U.S. president-elect Donald Trump. The biggest threat to the integrity of Canada no longer lies in the east, but in the west - namely, the Province of Alberta. A showdown is looming between Edmonton and Ottawa over Canada's upcoming tariff war with the United States - one that will start this week, when provincial premiers meet with the federal government to hash out a "Team Canada" response to Trump's threats.
The battle lines are clear.
Over the weekend, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly said that "everything is on the table" when it comes to countering Trump's tariffs, including withholding the four million barrels of oil Alberta ships south every day.
This caused a backlash from Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who spent part of the weekend meeting with Trump at his Mar-a-Lago resort.
"We won't stand for that and you should never, ever threaten something you cannot do," she said.
Smith reminded Joly that western Canadian crude flows to refineries in Ontario and Quebec through Enbridge's Line 5 pipeline, which runs through the U.S. Threats to cut off oil are thus "empty," she added, and would fuel a national unity crisis.
Smith is not wrong. If the federal government isn't careful, it could inflame the "Wexit" movement, which, for the first time, would be feasible, because it could find a willing partner in Washington. Trump thinks it would be great if Canada became America's 51st state. But what parts would he want? Downtown Toronto, with its core of NDP and Liberal voters? The Province of Quebec, which speaks a different language? Nope. The prize would be Alberta, with its oil and conservative mindset. And its border with the Northwest Territories. If Trump got Alberta, his next stop would be striking a deal with the N.W.T., then on to neighbouring Yukon, to increase the U.S.'s access to the Arctic and its undersea oil reserves.
And how would Albertans feel about joining the U.S.? In a December poll, 30 per cent said the province would benefit, the highest percentage in the country. Thirty-eight per cent of Albertans also thought the province would be better off as its own country, while only 35 per cent of Quebecers felt the same about their province. A separate poll, released Tuesday, found that when asked if Canada as a whole should join the U.S., 18 per cent of Albertans agree - far from a majority, but the highest percentage in the country.
For this, we can thank Trudeau and Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault. Over the past decade, they've been determined to put Alberta's oil industry out of business. Between their federal carbon tax, incoming emissions cap and general denigration of the resource as dirty and shameful, it's no wonder that the West feels disrespected.
It feels like a rerun of Pierre Trudeau's infamous National Energy Program, which caused thousands of Albertans to lose their livelihoods and spawned the bumper sticker, "Let the Eastern Bastards Freeze in the Dark."
But Trudeau père was also concerned about national unity and would roll over in his grave if his son's legacy was the breakup of Canada.
As Trudeau prepares to exit stage left, our leadership cannot allow that to happen. Canada needs to present a tough, united front that respects all its provinces. If we don't hang together, there is a real risk we will break apart. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Decades

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: years; assumed; biggest; threat; canadian; unity

Document 202 of 500

Smith delivers a warning to feds; Ban on alberta oil exports to u.s. would lead to 'national unity crisis,'premier says

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smith-delivers-warning-feds-ban-on-alberta-oil/docview/3155461527/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smith+delivers+a+warning+to+feds%3B+Ban+on+alberta+oil+exports+to+u.s.+would+lead+to+%27national+unity+crisis%2C%27premier+says&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith says that if the federal government imposes an export ban on Alberta oil going to the United States as a retaliatory measure against expected U.S. tariffs it would precipitate a "national unity crisis."
"Oil and gas is owned by the provinces, principally Alberta, and we won't stand for that," Smith said Monday. "I can't predict what Albertans would do."
She warned that Ottawa would have a "national unity crisis on (its) hands at the same time as having a crisis with our U.S. partners."
Smith made the remarks during a virtual press conference with reporters, which followed her meetings with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump at his West Palm Beach Mar-a-Lago resort and his golf club over the weekend.
Mélanie Joly, Canada's foreign affairs minister, was asked on CTV's Question Period talk show on Sunday if Canada would consider cutting off energy to the United States in the event of a tariff war, and responded, "Everything is on the table."
Canada is preparing a list of U.S. products against which surtaxes could be levied, should Trump bring in his promised 25-per-cent tariff on Canadian goods after he's inaugurated next week.
Canada made similar moves during negotiations in 2019 with the first Trump administration, adding more than $16 billion in surtaxes to a variety of American imports, including steel, aluminum, yogurt, toilet paper, dishwashers and various other items.
Joly's response echoes a pitch made by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who threatened in mid-December to cut off energy supply to the northeastern United States should the Trump tariffs become a reality. Smith has staunchly opposed such a measure, but said Monday morning that if Trump imposes tariffs, there will need to be a Canadian response.
"We have to wait and see what he does, and then we have to have a measured and mature response to it that hopefully does not damage the relationship or damage Canadian consumers and Canadian businesses too much," Smith said.
Smith, who said she attended Trump's Saturday soirée as a guest of celebrity investor and former Conservative party leadership candidate Kevin O'Leary, described her conversations with Trump as "constructive," but also seemed resigned that, come Jan. 20, Trump will be going ahead with tariffs, regardless of what Canadian politicians do.
She said that she is "not expecting" Alberta oil to be exempt from the Trump administration's tariffs, although she remains hopeful that the United States will want to purchase more oil from Canada for its refineries in the Midwest.
"I haven't seen any indication in any of the president's public commentary, or even in the comments that he had with me, that he's inclined to change his approach," Smith said.
The Alberta premier has been on an extensive heartsand-minds campaign in the United States, meeting with governors and appearing on conservative-leaning news programs. She has been making the case directly that U.S. tariffs would increase energy costs to American consumers. She has, however, faced criticism that by boosting Alberta she may be undermining the broader Canadian response to tariffs.
"I'm hoping that by talking about the importance of energy, that we'll be able to avoid tariffs on every Canadian product," said Smith.
Since Trump first made threats of tariffs on Canadian exports, Canadian provinces and the federal government have scrambled to respond.
In recent weeks, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has met with Trump at Mar-a-Lago, and a deputation of cabinet ministers has also travelled to Florida to meet with the incoming president's transition team. So far, there has been little indication that Trump has been persuaded to abandon his plan for tariffs, and it's all occurring against the backdrop of a domestic political crisis in Ottawa.
Last Monday, Trudeau announced that he would be resigning as prime minister, kicking off a race for the leadership of the Liberal party and proroguing Parliament until March 24. Smith has called for an immediate election to avoid what she called the "leadership vacuum" in Ottawa.
"We need somebody at the table who is able to engage with the (Trump) administration saying, 'You got a four year mandate. I got a four year mandate, let's deal.'And we don't have that right now," Smith said.
Canada's premiers and representatives from the federal government are meeting on Wednesday to discuss the tariff response. As well, a joint mission of provincial premiers will travel to Washington, D.C., on Feb. 12 to meet with Trump administration officials and talk trade. The Canadian economy, being orders of magnitude smaller than the United States', will be far more harmed by a prolonged trade war than the American economy will be, Smith said.
National Post

Document 203 of 500

Today's letters: Yes, Terry Fox deserves to be on Canada's currency

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-yes-terry-fox-deserves-be-on/docview/3155038044/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Yes%2C+Terry+Fox+deserves+to+be+on+Canada%27s+currency&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Terry Fox was a nation-builder
Re: Terry Fox doesn't deserve to be on Canada's $5 bill, Jan. 6.
Politicians alone do not define our nation. Terry Fox's tenacious struggle to financially support cancer research is recognized around the globe and has inspired countless nations to join this fight.
By the writer's own definition, Fox's selfless example of determination and sacrifice for the benefit of current and future generations has unquestionably "built up our nation, and contributed to the development of its identity and culture."
Symbolic photographs of Terry Fox labouring against adverse environment obstacles, while battling cancer to fulfil his dream, are therefore most worthy of inclusion on our currency.
Al Jones, Almonte
Why have royalty on our money?
I can't say I agree with Desmond Mills' opinion that non-statesmen be denied recognition on our paper bills. I believe it is uplifting to see inspiring personages honoured on Canadian paper money.
Noting the writer's contention that "our currency should be reserved for those who built up our nation and contributed to the development of its identity and culture," I wonder how he regards the appearance of royalty on Canadian currency? In my opinion, none of the royals appearing on the bills could be said to fit his criteria.
Paul Lindsay, Ottawa
Not thrilled with past statesmen
I almost choked on my coffee when I the assertion that "our collective past from before Confederation" is being abandoned. It isn't. The shame of programs to erase Indigenous people made it entirely appropriate to remove John A. Macdonald from the $10 bill.
Although it may have been more meaningful to put Louis Riel there, the addition of Viola Desmond highlights our hope for socio-cultural progress reinforced by our Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
I wasn't recognized as a "person" before 1929, so excuse me if I'm not thrilled with the "statesmen" of the past. The addition of "minor players" on our currency highlights that it is they and not so-called statesmen who build the character, culture, hope and opportunity in this country.
Evelyne Power, Gloucester
An 'ordinary kid' inspired us all
Terry Fox was an ordinary, unpretentious young man who wanted to do something for the fight against cancer, of which he knew the heartbreak. He never made it out of Ontario, but his "Canadian niceness" and humility united the entire nation and made all Canadians like him and feel unified.
Fox has been recognized with a statue in Ottawa (although we all know statues can be defaced or torn down), and there is an annual Terry Fox Run (and commemorative days can be illogically removed), but money is part of everybody's everyday life.
No one is going to rip up a banknote just to prove a point or because they don't like the person on the note. Reserving a banknote's face for a politician or a statesperson is fine, but there are actually examples of ordinary Canadians who did extraordinary things who have been honoured. Viola Desmond is one example.
Other countries have put non-politicians on their notes; author Jane Austen is on a Bank of England note. There was a trend in this country to put animals and birds on our money, but birds and animals have no nationality.
Terry Fox was an ordinary Canadian kid who lit up an entire nation, and inspired others to follow. Who can you really relate to: a stuffy and ambitious politician, or someone who may remind you of your own kid, particularly if you have a sick kid undergoing cancer treatment?
It's not the size of the dog in the fight, it's the size of the fight in the dog, and Terry Fox fought the good fight. That's something all Canadians can relate to.
Douglas Cornish, Ottawa
sdfsdsd
Demoting Laurier was quite appropriate
Maybe Sir Wilfrid Laurier was relegated to the $50 bill because he was one of a long line of past prime ministers who continued, and supported, the abhorrent treatment of Indigenous people in Canada.
Bill Reid, Ottawa
New military honours don't mean much
Re: Military cancelled combat badge over fears of risk-taking, documents show, Jan. 2.
The Combat Action Badge (CAB) was to be a Canadian version of the U.S. Army's Combat Infantryman Badge (CIB), which was first introduced in October 1943 to boost the morale and prestige of service in the infantry.
Given that military insignia has traditionally been used to not only encourage recruiting, build esprit de corps and recognize personal and unit achievement, it can be argued that it has always been used to promote "elitism."
While Airborne, Commando and Special Forces, perhaps the most elite units of the military, have always had their own distinctive insignia which set them apart from everyone else; every ship, army unit and air force squadron also have their own insignia, which in essence is a method to demonstrate exclusivity within the military.
The last 25 years have seen a proliferation of new insignia introduced within the four services of the Canadian military. And while the introduction of new insignia may have increased military effectiveness and a simplified and accelerated nomination process for individual honours may have improved recognizing military service, there appears to be little evidence that new badges and medals have contributed to the retention of service members, or encouraged recruiting.
Ed Storey Nepean
Let's just give them participation medals
Apparently military leaders were concerned that some soldiers might actually be brave enough to risk their lives in combat, and that soldiers who weren't on the ground fighting wouldn't get an equal chance to do so.
Rather than cancelling the honour, they could have considered issuing everyone big shiny participation medals to hang on ribbons around their necks, as has been done in some children's sports. That way, everyone would feel equally brave. A perfect Canadian solution.
Alma Estable, Ottawa
Follow Denley's advice on public transit
Re: Ottawa needs major rethink of public transit, Jan. 7.
Randall Denley states "start with how much the city can afford to pay and shape the transit system accordingly." An excellent principle that elected officials at all levels of government should adopt before approving any publicly funded initiative.
This principle rightfully amalgamates two fundamental elements for a good society: long-term sound financial health (funded by the taxpayers); and continued delivery of effective programs (to the taxpayers).
This principle ensures the best quality of life possible for our children and grandchildren, and their children not born yet.
André Corriveau, Stittsville
Diesel trains seem to be better choice
Great to see that the Trillium line is up and running, finally. Two things stood out in the coverage: some riders expressed concern that the trains were not electric; yet they said that the diesel trains were less noisy and more reliable.
I think this has been a major problem with the move to better public transit: not going for the best solutions to get people out of cars onto public transit. By tying our plans to only electric, our representatives locked themselves into a small space.
Isn't the idea of public transit to get the most people out of vehicles by a system that rewards that choice? The diesel trains seem to be far more reliable, far more passenger-friendly, and get 600 people per train off the road. That trumps choosing a future that is not as reliable.
David Keenan, Stittsville
Bring Ottawa back to world-class status
Re: To revive downtown Ottawa, the city must cooperate with the feds, Jan. 2.
Neil Saravanamuttoo puts in writing an issue that many Ottawa residents, as well as many Canadians in general, have been commenting on since the pandemic.
Ottawa is both a city and a national capital. It is a symbol of this country and a tourist destination, both for Canadians as well as for international visitors. It must be brought back to the world-class city it should be.
Stella Rastogi, Ottawa
Trudeau recognized mixed-race identity
Re:In praise of Justin Trudeau's leadership and kindness, Jan. 9.
During my public service tenure and Parliament's approval of the Canadian Multiculturalism Act, I was privileged to engage in the immediate follow-up within the federal government and Canadian society. There was a prime focus on advancing positive race relations.
One issue significant to me was the identity of "mixed race." I am a Canadian of mixed race identity: I inherited being East Indian/Hindu from my late father; and my Slovakian/Christian roots come from my deceased mother. Throughout a long career in a race-relations focused department, I never was fully recognized for my joint intercultural and interracial identity. I tended to be viewed or recognized as "one or the other."
When I retired, I arranged to meet with Justin Trudeau when he was responsible for multiculturalism and youth affairs in the Liberal party. He readily invited me to a positive and free-exchange meeting, where he firmly affirmed the significance of mixed race Canadians. He agreed that Statistics Canada must officially record this distinct identity.
Whatever the current status of Justin Trudeau, he will never be forgotten by me for this apolitical and positive recognition of Canada's diversity.
Roman Mukerjee, Ottawa
A new motto for the biased Citizen
Re: MacDougall: Trudeau's record enough to make you laugh, Jan. 7.
After reading much of the commentary in the Citizen, I am non-plussed by your "Fair Play and Daylight" page title. I have been wondering about a better descriptor.
Your subscribers had the choice of reading a critique of Ottawa's transit system (always an easy target), plus a not-so-objective column on our current prime minister's record, written by ex-prime minister Stephen Harper's director of communications.
I suggest your masthead be updated to read "Local rants and heavily biased opinions."
Ian Naish, Ottawa
Priggish nationalism won't save Canada
Re: Trump trying to distract from tariff costs with 51st state remarks, Trudeau says, Jan. 10.
That 19th-century "manifest destiny" doctrine that said Americans were destined to expand westward across North America has now been adjusted to include northward as well.
Of course, the U.S. can apply economic force on Canada. We are a mooching member of NATO and have been freeloading off the U.S. military for decades. Their security organizations tell us what threats are happening here. Our economy has become a mis-managed shambles. Our trade has not diversified and most of it is still with the U.S. Our exports depend on Americans' willingness to buy, or not.
That 49th parallel actually is just a "line on a map." In 1846, Britain and the U.S. signed the Oregon Treaty saying that it would be the main Pacific Northwest boundary. Canada did not exist back then.
Trump has a point. Canada needs a reason to exist other than priggish nationalism.
Dyan Cross, Ottawa
Premiers shouldn't attend inauguration
At least two of our premiers, Danielle Smith of Alberta and Andrew Furey of Newfoundland and Labrador, will attend Donald Trump's inauguration. They are doing so in the hope of generating business for their provinces and staving off Trump's 25-per-cent tariffs.
Have they no shame? In light of Trump's most recent remarks concerning the annexation of our country - going as far as posting an American flag over our sovereign territory - I feel it is an outrage that they should kowtow to this tyrant and go kiss his ring.
Diane Dumont, Orléans
And if Canada were the 51st state ...
Someone should tell Donald Trump that if Canada were the 51st state, Kamala Harris would be president-elect and he would be in prison.
Suzanne Turenne, Russell
Winter isn't actually this violent, folks
I know it's cold, just to be clear. But has anyone else noticed how we are transforming winter into an unnatural threat of violence?
As I grew up, my mother would tell me it was cold and I needed to dress warmly. That summed it up. Look at today's vocabulary: We have weather bombs. Bomb cyclones. Polar vortices. Arctic blasts.
In the past days alone, Accuweather has warned that our region faces "fangerous ice," "storm train," "massive winter storm," "the threat has increased to a high risk," "coldest January since 2011," and "Arctic air kicks into high gear."
But it's not Arctic air. More like Alberta air, if you trace it back. It's just a northwest wind, bringing winter as it always has.
As a side note, most forecasters add a windchill factor to make cold seem colder, but they skew it. Windchill applies only to bare skin, and I don't see people running around naked (thank the Lord). Yes, wind is cold - and I have stood at many windy bus stops to and from work - but not THAT cold.
Could be worse, I guess. The Brits this week issued an "amber alert" over weather, with light snow and temps that dipped below 0 C. None of them ownd winter tires and the videos of traffic are kind of fun.
Tom Spears, Ottawa

Document 204 of 500

Alberta premier visits Trump at Mar-a-Lago; 'Constructive'; Smith Recounts 'Friendly'Talk On Energy, Tariffs

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-premier-visits-trump-at-mar-lago/docview/3155018447/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+premier+visits+Trump+at+Mar-a-Lago%3B+%27Constructive%27%3B+Smith+Recounts+%27Friendly%27Talk+On+Energy%2C+Tariffs&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.3&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith visited U.S. president-elect Donald Trump at Mar-a-Lago on Saturday evening and again Sunday morning, as Smith continues her campaign to woo American politicians in the face of tariff threats from the incoming president.
"We had a friendly and constructive conversation during which I emphasized the mutual importance of the U.S.-Canadian energy relationship, and specifically, how hundreds of thousands of American jobs are supported by energy exports from Alberta," Smith wrote on X.
"On behalf of Albertans, I will continue to engage in constructive dialogue and diplomacy with the incoming administration and elected federal and state officials from both parties, and will do all I can to further Alberta's and Canada's interests."
Trump, who has escalated his rhetoric around Canada, has promised, on Day 1, to institute 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian exports to the United States and has mused repeatedly about annexing Canada by "economic force," referring to the 49th parallel as "artificial."
The tariff threat, and remarks about Canadian sovereignty, have alarmed Canadians across the political spectrum and prompted responses from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and provincial premiers. Photos posted to the Instagram account of Paul L. Palandijan, the CEO of O'Leary Ventures, show Smith posing with Kevin O'Leary, a Canadian businessman and former candidate to lead the Conservative Party of Canada, and Jordan Peterson, the Canadian psychologist, podcaster and sometime columnist for National Post.
In roughly a week, Trump will be sworn in as president of the United States, following his win in the November federal election.
His bullish talk on trade has coincided with a political crisis at the federal level in Canada: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who himself has been doing a circuit of U.S. media after attending the funeral of former president Jimmy Carter, announced his resignation last Monday, kicking off a race to elect a new leader of the Liberal party and leading to the perception that at a time of heightened tension with the United States, Canada has a rudderless government.
The premier of Alberta - the provincial economy being heavily dependent on oil-and-gas exports to the United States - has been on a hearts-and-minds tour of the United States in recent weeks. In December, Smith attended the Western Governors'Association, boosting Alberta's profile as a major exporter to the U.S. energy industry and explaining that tariffs on Alberta oil will make energy and gasoline more expensive for Americans. Smith took time away from a family vacation to visit Mar-a-Lago.
Smith has also appeared on Fox News regularly and is planning to attend Trump's inauguration ceremony on Jan. 20. Along with Ontario Premier Doug Ford - who has spoken regularly with U.S. media - Smith has become one of Canada's most prominent boosters as Trudeau's leadership faced internal Liberal party challenges.
It's not entirely clear whether Trump will follow through on his threats to enact steep tariffs against all Canadian goods, including energy. However, if he does, it's expected to cause major problems for the Alberta - and Canadian - economy. The Canadian Chamber of Commerce estimates that 25-per-cent tariffs would shrink Canada's GDP by about 2.6 per cent. In Alberta, the Trump tariffs could be expected to lead to 52,000 job losses, boosting unemployment to 7.8 per cent in the province and, potentially, push the province into a recession.
It all comes as the Canadian government contemplates retaliatory tariffs against the United States. Ford has also mused that Canada should shut off energy exports to the United States in a Hail Mary negotiating tactic. In addition to western oil and gas, Central Canada exports a considerable amount of hydroelectric power to the northeastern United States. Though Ford has since backed away from this position, Smith has staunchly opposed such measures.
"Under no circumstances will Alberta agree to cut off oil and gas exports," Smith told reporters last month. "We don't support tariffs. I don't support tariffs on Canadian goods, and I don't support tariffs on U.S. goods because all it does is make life more expensive for everyday Canadians and everyday Americans." In November, Trudeau also travelled to Mar-a-Lago to meet with the incoming president, a meeting which Trump described as "very productive." In late December, two senior Trudeau ministers, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly and Intergovernmental Affairs Minister Dominic LeBlanc, travelled to West Palm Beach, where Mar-a-Lago is located, to meet with members of the Trump transition team. The Trump transition team has yet to respond to National Post's request for comment about Smith's presence at Mar-a-Lago.
National Post

Document 205 of 500

Carney set to enter race this week; Former boc governor said to have backing of more than 30 mps in bid for liberal leadership

Author: Lévesque, Carine; Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine; Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carney-set-enter-race-this-week-former-boc/docview/3155017995/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carney+set+to+enter+race+this+week%3B+Former+boc+governor+said+to+have+backing+of+more+than+30+mps+in+bid+for+liberal+leadership&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine%3BTaylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney is preparing to announce this week that he will be in the running to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as Liberal leader.
A source said he is expected to launch his campaign toward the end of the week. The source said he has received the support of more than 30 MPs from different parts of the country, some of whom looked his way following Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc's decision not to enter the race.
Carney, 59, has been touted as one of the Liberal heavyweights who might seek to replace Trudeau in the top job.
He was tapped by the prime minister to chair a task force on economic growth last September, after being heavily courted to join the Liberal team. Carney was then rumoured to be joining Trudeau's cabinet as finance minister, replacing Chrystia Freeland. But Freeland resigned, sparking a political crisis, and Carney did not end up in cabinet.
The source said Carney would represent not only change, by bringing forward new ideas and perspectives to the Liberal party, but also a credible voice on the economy.
The source said that, as governor of the Bank of Canada, Carney steered Canada through the 2008 global financial crisis, protecting Canadians'homes, jobs and livelihoods in entire sectors, like the auto sector, under the Conservative Harper government. Later, as governor of the Bank of England, he dealt with the threat of populist politics with Brexit.
Credibility on the economic front is the kind of fight Carney wants to bring to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, added the source.
Carney would be the front-runner in a race that has so far only two declared candidates: Ottawa backbencher Chandra Arya and Quebec businessman and former MP Frank Baylis.
Another minister rumoured to have been considering a run for the Liberal leadership has decided against it, saying there isn't enough time for him to mount the kind of campaign he wants.
Labour Minister Steven MacKinnon said in a social media post Sunday that the leadership race needs diverse, experienced and pragmatic voices in both English and French.
And while he feels he could be that voice, the Gatineau MP says the length of the campaign for the leadership doesn't fit with his plans.
MacKinnon said he will focus on his ministerial responsibilities and will listen closely to help make an informed choice around who should replace Trudeau.
Transport and Internal Trade Minister Anita Anand also announced this weekend that she has declined to run and would also not seek reelection.
She said the joy of her work in politics delayed a planned return to academia, but now that Trudeau has decided to step aside she says it's time for her to do so as well.
"I sincerely thank Prime Minister Trudeau for welcoming me on the Liberal team as a member of Parliament and for entrusting me in key cabinet portfolios," she wrote in a statement on social media.
The announcement by MacKinnon and Anand follow Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, who on Friday said it is out of a "deep sense of duty" to her country that she is bowing out of the race. She said she was personally ready to become the Liberal party's first female leader, but that leaving her current responsibilities made running for the job impossible.
"Am I ready at the same time to step away from my responsibilities as minister of foreign affairs at a time where it is a crucial time in the Canada-U.S. relationship? The answer is no. And the reality is I can't do both," Joly told reporters.
"I was really torn about this decision because to be frank, I think that our chances were really, really high," she added.
Joly said she is foreign affairs minister at a "crucial" time in Canada's history and that many Canadians are feeling anxious days away from Trump's inauguration.
"My job is to make sure we are ready should president-elect Trump decide to go on with his tariffs," she said. "I decided to put my country first and I decided to do my job."
LeBlanc also announced last week that he would not be running for Trudeau's job because he wanted to focus on the threat of U.S. tariffs. The government is currently preparing an extensive list of retaliatory tariffs should Trump decide to move ahead with his threat when he assumes office on Jan. 20.
By then, most contenders will have announced that they are in the running.
The Liberal party released the rules of the race on Thursday with a new leader to be announced on March 9. Potential contenders have until Jan. 23 to declare their candidacy and will have to pay a $350,000 entry fee.
Eligible voters must become a Liberal party member by Jan. 27, be a Canadian citizen, permanent resident or have status under the Indian Act, and be at least 14 years old.
Former B.C. premier Christy Clark is also said to be preparing to announce a leadership bid, Sources said she has spent months organizing a campaign team that is ready to mobilize should she decide to enter the race. Last week, she held a meeting with 135 organizers from across the country.
People close to Freeland's leadership team have been more tight-lipped on her intentions, saying only that she was consulting with caucus members last week.
If she does go for it, Freeland can count on the help of Tom Allison, a longtime Ontario Liberal organizer who ran the winning campaigns of provincial Liberal leaders Bonnie Crombie, Steven Del Duca and Kathleen Wynne.
Poilievre has already been targeting Freeland, Carney and Clark as his top adversaries.
Other cabinet ministers who are still considering a leadership bid include Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne, Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson, and House leader Karina Gould.
A source close to Gould says she plans to make her decision public early this week.
National Post calevesque@postmedia.com staylor@postmedia.com

Document 206 of 500

Trillium line bright spot in a Tumultuous week; Successful launch was good news amid PM stepping down, Trump's threats to Canada

Author: Deachman, Bruce

Publication info: Deachman, Bruce. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trillium-line-bright-spot-tumultuous-week/docview/3154309741/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trillium+line+bright+spot+in+a+Tumultuous+week%3B+Successful+launch+was+good+news+amid+PM+stepping+down%2C+Trump%27s+threats+to+Canada&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Deachman%2C+Bruce&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was the best of times, it was the worst of times. The spring of hope, the winter of despair.
This tale of two cities, however, all took place in Ottawa, in a single day.
The best of times? The new north-south Trillium rail line, which had been closed for nearly five years to facilitate its expansion, relaunched on Jan. 6 with all the attendant bells and whistlestops and, to everyone's relief, no notable untoward incidents. (Admittedly, it also launched without many riders, but, to be charitable, it was a bitterly cold day that would forgive people for taking an "I'll wait a couple days to see if this actually works" posture.)
The worst of times? That celebration was tempered by the uncertainly created by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who announced he would resign, then prorogued Parliament until March 24. This immediately raised the question of how the city's efforts to squeeze some desperately needed money from the feds for transit might be affected.
Mayor Mark Sutcliffe, casting himself in the mould of optimistic and calming mayors, suggested that the parliamentary work stoppage shouldn't hamper his "Fairness for Ottawa" campaign, which aims to secure $36 million from higher levels of government to help balance OC Transpo's books, currently redder that its logo.
"I don't see this as an obstacle to the federal government following through on the discussions we've been having," Sutcliffe said. "If anything, it brings a bit of clarity to what was a very uncertain political picture in December ... I think it's business as usual."
His outward hopefulness reminds me of those signs at construction sites when a major street is torn up that say "Open for business as usual," yet you can't for the life of you navigate your way to any of the foundering retailers.
I wish I shared the mayor's optimism, but I don't see how his fairness campaign benefits from federal disarray. Surely it has moved down a rung or two in terms of priorities, with the Liberals scrambling to choose a replacement leader and ready themselves for an election. Federal money for Ottawa's transit system? I doubt that plays well outside the 613.
Meanwhile, Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said in December that the province would pitch in money for transit if the federal government did so, a classic case of passing the buck while not passing the bucks.
Trudeau's proroguing of Parliament may postpone the inevitable non-confidence vote and election, but his retirement announcement was a reminder that the next prime minister, after a brief interlude with Trudeau's successor, will likely be Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
For someone who seems to dislike Trudeau as much as he does, Poilievre's failure to make political hay by jumping on the Fairness for Ottawa bandwagon, or even commenting publicly, is telling.
First off, Fairness for Ottawa isn't the sort of catchy, grievance-baiting bumper-sticker slogan the Tory leader favours, like Axe the Tax, Stop the Crime or Build the Homes.
Boost the Buses? Freeze the Fares? Maintain the Trains? That's so not him.
Second, adding to Ottawa's coffers isn't the modus operandi of Poilievre, some of whose plans to Fix the Budget include largely gutting the CBC and greatly reducing the size of the federal public service. Those so-called efficiencies may be good for a country that Poilievre contends is broken, but they would take a bite out of Ottawa's economic health.
Then there's U.S. presidentelect Donald Trump's continued threat to annex Canada or tariff us into submission, waging economic warfare. "Many people in Canada LOVE being the 51st State," he claims.
Perhaps we're just entering a fantasyland here, but that's what I told myself heading into the 2016 U.S. presidential election, and look what happened. Would Ottawa even be a state capital in such a world? Would the public service be eliminated altogether? Who, then, would ride the trains at all, except to the airport to get away? Not to challenge Dickens too much, but can we still turn these tempestuous times into the spring of hope, and not the winter of despair? After a week like this, who knows? bdeachman@postmedia.com

Document 207 of 500

Stakeholders describe health system in distress; Administrators, patients warn committee about debt, lotteries to get family doctors

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stakeholders-describe-health-system-distress/docview/3154309492/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stakeholders+describe+health+system+in+distress%3B+Administrators%2C+patients+warn+committee+about+debt%2C+lotteries+to+get+family+doctors&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: From small hospitals taking on debt to stay afloat to patients entering lotteries to find a new doctor, members of Ontario's Standing Committee on Finance and Economic Affairs received a taste of the growing pressures on the province's health system.
Health is the largest category of spending in Ontario's $214.5-billion budget, but members of the committee, which travels the province in the fall and winter holding pre-budget consultations, heard that the province was not spending enough to ensure access and care for many people.
While the committee met inside Ottawa's downtown Sheraton Ottawa Hotel on Thursday, protesters affiliated with the Ottawa Health Coalition and CUPE hospital and long-term care staff rallied outside, calling for more hospital and health-care funding.
Among those making presentations to the committee was Cholly Boland, president and CEO of Winchester Hospital, south of Ottawa. He asked for some "long overdue stability" for small hospitals like Winchester's, which account for nearly half of the hospitals in Ontario. He asked that the province increase the budgets of small hospitals by 10 per cent.
"This would give us stability for the next several years. It will help us get out of debt so we can pay our bills and stop paying hundreds of thousands of dollars in interest charges so we can use all of our funding to operate our hospitals and care for patients."
Boland said Winchester Hospital was able to balance its budget for years by being careful with spending and taking steps such as closing 20 per cent of in-patient beds to reduce costs. "But there is a limit."
In 2021 "the well went dry, we couldn't squeeze any more out of the budget and we went into deficit," he said. It has continued to post annual deficits since, wiping out its cash reserves and forcing it to borrow money.
The hospital, whose annual budget is around $50 million, is now making debt payments on a line of credit worth around $10 million. Among other things, Boland said the hospital is unable to pay many vendors on time. "Instead of paying in 90 days, it takes us nine months. You get some suppliers who won't do business with you.
"Our financial situation is literally risking our ability to operate as a hospital to serve all our communities and be an effective partner with city hospitals."
Boland said he was not speaking officially for other hospitals, but knew many were in similar, or worse, positions. In fact, he said provincial officials told him other hospitals were in worse shape than Winchester.
Officials at the hospital, which has maintained among the highest patient satisfaction rates in the province, spend too much time thinking about balancing the budget and making payroll "when we are supposed to be thinking about patients," Boland added.
The committee also heard from Martine Whissel, director of the Eastern Ottawa family health team. She described the difficulty her team had hiring staff because their salaries had not increased in five years. There have been some operational funding increases during that time, she said.
Clinics like hers, she said, can significantly reduce pressures on hospitals, but funding needs to be increased. She said an investment of $500 million annually for five years would allow the expansion and modernization of health teams across the province to better provide people care.
She said it would align with the goals of Dr. Jane Philpott. The former federal health minister has been appointed by the province to chair a primary-care action team with a mandate to connect every person in Ontario to primary health care in the next five years.
For people like Ottawa resident Kathy Marshall and her family, that can't happen quickly enough. She and her husband, as well as two adult children and their families, all learned late last year that their longtime family doctor was closing her practice.
Marshall is 67 and her husband is 72 and takes medication that needs monitoring. She said she didn't blame her former doctor, but the news was a shock for all of them.
Marshall and her husband found another physician after being chosen through a lottery. Her children and grandchild are still without primary care, she said. "It was very upsetting trying to find a doctor and joining the throngs."
According to the Ontario College of Family Physicians, 2.5 million Ontario residents were without family physicians as of last July.
Ottawa Centre NDP MPP Joel Harden, who raised Marshall's story during the pre-budget hearings, said looking for a doctor in Ontario right now "is really like Lotto 649."
The committee heard from officials at Ottawa's Queensway Carleton Hospital pitching a new program to train anesthesiologist assistants to help keep operating rooms open.
Ottawa West Nepean NDP MPP Chandra Pasma noted that Queensway Carleton was struggling with overcrowding and inadequate bed capacity for its growing numbers of patients, many with complex needs. The hospital is looking for provincial funding to more than double the size of its emergency department, add 90 in-patient beds and build a new urgent care clinic.
In a statement, Ministry of Health spokesperson Ema Popovic said the province had increased the health-care budget by more than 31 per cent since 2018, investing $85 billion into the system this year alone, "greater than the total health-care budget of almost every province and territory combined."
That includes a four per cent increase to hospitals for the second year in a row, $44 million to tackle emergency department wait times and $330 million for pediatric care.
"Our government will continue to work with our hospital partners and the Ontario Hospital Association to ensure all of our hospitals have the tools they need to continue delivering the high-quality care patients deserve," Popovic said.
Ontario's per capita health-care spending was the lowest per capita among provinces in the 2022-2023 fiscal year, according to the Financial Accountability Office.

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Committees; Family physicians; Budgets; Funding; Patient satisfaction
