Document 234 of 500

Transit lifeline 'close,'mayor says; Sutcliffe hopeful feds will move ahead before election campaign derails funding

Author: Crawford, Blair

Publication info: Crawford, Blair. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/transit-lifeline-close-mayor-says-sutcliffe/docview/3149642395/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Transit+lifeline+%27close%2C%27mayor+says%3B+Sutcliffe+hopeful+feds+will+move+ahead+before+election+campaign+derails+funding&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Crawford%2C+Blair&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OC Transpo service cuts, another fare increase, pulling money from the City of Ottawa's rainy-day reserves - even a special transit levy on top of one agreed to in the 2025 budget - are all on the table if the city doesn't get the $36 million from "higher levels of government" it's counting on.
And with no mention of transit money for Ottawa in the Liberals' fall economic statement and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government seemingly on the verge of collapse, time is running out on Mayor Mark Sutcliffe's Fairness for Ottawa campaign.
"I think there's a willingness by the federal government to help us," Sutcliffe said last week, a few hours after NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said he would call for a non-confidence vote when Parliament returned in the new year.
"Things are moving in the right direction and we've travelled a long way down the road and we're very close to the finish line of being able to announce some help for Ottawa, but the political volatility right now on Parliament Hill is my main concern," Sutcliffe said.
"I hope we're able to move forward on something before that gets in the way, before we're into an election campaign or a leadership race or some other thing that would prevent the announcement from happening."
OC Transpo is facing a $120-million operating deficit in 2025. Fares are going up five per cent on Jan. 1 and an increase in the transit levy accounts for more than one-third of the 3.9-per-cent tax increase residents face next year. Even so, the city has added the $36-million placeholder in the transit budget for money it hopes to receive from the provincial and federal governments to balance its books.
Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy confirmed earlier in December that the province would pitch in money for transit if the federal government did so as well.
But, despite months of lobbying by the mayor, there has been no federal announcement.
Instead, the Liberals'fall economic statement committed money for more studies of a sixth bridge over the Ottawa River and a proposed tramway linking Aylmer and Gatineau to downtown Ottawa. Money to run OC Transpo? Nada.
Sutcliffe said he wasn't surprised by that, but remained hopeful.
"I've spoken to representatives from the federal government since the fall economic statement and they're continuing to send encouraging signals about the decision-making process on some help for Ottawa," he said. "We're getting closer."
And if the money isn't there? "If that doesn't come by February, say by the end of February, we'll have to look at other ways of dealing with that," Sutcliffe said.
"We'd have to find other solutions and all options are on the table. I'm not saying that any of these are desirable, but we'd have to look at increasing fares, a special transit levy, service reductions, other efficiencies ... We'd have to look at all of those things."
Neil Saravanamuttoo, director of CitySHAPES Ottawa, is dubious that help will come from Parliament Hill.
"The mayor seems optimistic that transit funding will be coming in early 2025. I have to admit I'm not quite so optimistic as he is," Saravanamuttoo said.
"The federal government has been crystal, crystal clear that they do not do transit operations. They do transit capital costs, but they see transit operations as the responsibility of the provinces.
"If you think about it, if the feds were to provide operational funding to Ottawa, they would have to do the same for every city in the country. I just can't see that happening. They're not prepared to set a precedent and open up the floodgates."
Saravanamuttoo also warned of another transit pitfall: OC Transpo's assumption that ridership will continue to increase and its projection of $200 million in fare revenue in 2025.
"When we look back over the past several years, fare revenue has been consistently lower than expected. That fare revenue may come in closer to $150 million. That would not be unusual at all. We're looking at this $36-million operational deficit, but there could be a fare deficit as well.
"At some point in the year, there will have to a reckoning. The city will just have to figure out a way to manage it."
A spokesperson for Jenna Sudds, the Minister of Families, Children and Social Development, a former Ottawa city councillor and the ranking MP for the National Capital Region, said the government's talks with Ottawa were continuing.
"Minister Sudds has always been committed to finding solutions that best serve the people of Ottawa - and will continue to do just that," her director of communications, Margaret Jacques, wrote in an email. "Minister Sudds remains in active conversations with the mayor to ensure Ottawa residents are served well. As operational costs for public transit fall under provincial jurisdiction, we encourage the Province of Ontario to work with the City of Ottawa to address ongoing concerns to provide stable and predictable support for the community."
But Sutcliffe says there are other ways for the feds to help. As an example, he cited that the provincial government is considering taking on the costs of maintaining Regional Road 174, something now paid for by the city.
"Cash is cash. When that's completed, that would allow us to move money for other areas of our budget," Sutcliffe said.
"Of course, we'd welcome federal help with transit, but there are lots of ways that the federal government can help us."
As a last resort, the city could use its reserve funds to keep OC Transpo whole, but that is not a sustainable solution.
"We have some (reserves), but those funds are an emergency basis only," Sutcliffe said. "When you use reserves for operational challenges, you can only use them once. That's not ideal."

Subject: Capital costs; Federal government; Fares; Funding

Document 235 of 500

Reader favourites: 14 of the most-read Ottawa Citizen stories of 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reader-favourites-14-most-read-ottawa-citizen/docview/3149059799/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reader+favourites%3A+14+of+the+most-read+Ottawa+Citizen+stories+of+2024&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Citizen published thousands of stories in 2024 - but some stories just catch lightning in a bottle, and are read by everyone in town. Themes that captured your attention this year included quirky news oddities, tragic breaking news, and Citizen scoops. Here's a look back at the year as curated by you, dear reader.
The Fall: My once-vibrant dad emerged broken from the hospital. Then he was gone.
Allan Payne is pictured at the long-term care home where he lived the last seven months of his life. FAMILY PHOTO
A longtime health reporter, Elizabeth Payne thought she understood Canada's failing health-care system. Then her dad fell. Sitting at her father's bedside, hoping he will rest after what has been a long day for a 90-year-old, or for anyone, really. But there is no rest here. He is agitated and wants to go home. Who could blame him? His bed is in a fluorescent-lit room whose walls are curtains that are seldom closed. He is hooked up to monitors that beep while he is secured to the bed after an earlier escape attempt.
Layoffs on the table for permanent government employees as part of spending review
Treasury Board President Anita Anand responds to a question during a news conference in Ottawa, Tuesday, June 18, 2024.
The federal government has been looking for ways to tighten its budget and curb the size of the public service, which has swelled in recent years. While the Liberal government has said it would do so through attrition and hiring freezes, cutting the jobs of permanent government employees wasn't on the table. But Canada's biggest public sector union, the Public Service Alliance of Canada (PSAC), says that no longer seems to be the case.
Deachman: Ottawa woman living in a canoe says it's the safest place for her
Frances DeSilva has been living in a canoe on the Rideau Canal since late May.
Frances DeSilva almost jumped down my throat when I asked her how she got her canoe, suggesting that if I were talking to someone else, someone of means, I would have first asked where they got it, not how. My question, she implied, was actually about how a homeless person could possibly be in possession of a canoe. Which, I confess, was pretty much true, although what I really wanted to know was why she was living in one, and whether there was a way to help her into more stable housing.
'It has been a difficult ten years not to say anything': Kevin Vickers shares his story of Parliament Hill shooting
"To be involved in taking another person's life is something that just sticks with you," Vickers says from his home in Trout Brook, New Brunswick
A decade removed from his confrontation with a jihadist gunman in the halls of Parliament, former Sergeant-at-Arms Kevin Vickers is ready to offer his full account of that day's deadly drama. Vickers, now 68, has never before given an interview about the events of Oct. 22, 2014, when Michael Zehaf-Bibeau stormed through the front doors of Centre Block after shooting Cpl. Nathan Cirillo in the back as he stood sentry at the National War Memorial.
Adam: Here's what the pay of an Ontario family doctor looks like
Dr. Salesh Budhoo, shown in his clinic, the Pearl Family Practice in Mississauga, provided a breakdown of family doctor costs and expenses to the Ottawa Citizen.
By any measure, Ontario is in the midst of a full-blown family doctor crisis as a growing number of physicians flee the practice, leaving millions of people without access to primary care. There are multiple reasons for the exodus, but doctors say the key ones include poor pay, the rising cost of business, burdensome paperwork and high workloads. But there is more to it than that. Family doctors say everything about the practice today is so stressful that the job is no longer as fulfilling as it once was. "Even though I really love it, I can see how if I was at the other end of the spectrum as a graduate, I may not choose it knowing what I know now," says Ottawa family doctor Michael Yachnin, a 40-year veteran.
The suicide of an Ottawa social justice advocate sparks calls for compassion, change
A photo posted by Erin O'Neil to Instagram, May 22, 2023
CONTENT WARNING: This article mentions topics related to suicide. If you or someone you know needs help, please look for the resources at the bottom of this article.
An Ottawa social justice activist who lived with mental health and physical problems died by suicide in July as she faced eviction from her Hintonburg apartment. A certified teacher, Erin O'Neil had a sizable following on X, where she was known as veganlovebunny, and on Instagram, where she was known as madness_unhoused.
What happened at the Barrhaven killings, according to the father's friend and local monk
A memorial at Palmadeo Park in Barrhaven dedicated to the six people, including four children, who were found dead at a home a in Barrhaven.
Lying in a hospital bed nursing physical wounds and unimaginable mental trauma, Dhanushka Wickramasinghe is trying to come to terms with the horrifying deaths of his young family and a good friend.
"He is still in the shock level," said Bhanthe Sumanarathana, a friend and resident monk at the Hilda Jayewardenaramaya Buddhist Monastery, who visited Wickramasinghe on Thursday. "I can feel it when he's talking. He has like a dizzy mind. He can't imagine the things that happened. It's like a dream to him."
Controversial new Canadian Army logo approved at highest levels, documents show
National Defence said the Canadian Army released on May 3 was developed by the department's internal graphic design team at no extra expense to taxpayers.
A new Canadian Army logo that sparked an intense online backlash was approved at the highest level with no objections raised about the image that has since been likened to a bear having sex with a moose.
The senior level military approval process for the new Canadian Army logo started in March 2024 with a plan to roll out the image in May online, according to documents obtained by the Ottawa Citizen.
Why Ottawa built an LRT station in the middle of nowhere
Bowesville station for the O Train near Bowesville and Earl Armstrong roads.
Bowesville Station rises from the scrub and fields of south Ottawa like a shining city of steel and glass set amid a Costco-sized parking lot.
It's the penultimate station on the Trillium Line. There isn't a house in sight and there's no development planned. Until further notice, it will remain mostly scrubland and cow pastures.
So when passengers are finally riding the diesel-powered trains on the long-delayed north-south route on the Trillium Line LRT, they might be forgiven for asking: "Why would anyone put a train station here?"
Richard Smith is an award-winning French teacher. Anonymous complaints ended his 23-year career
Richard Smith, a former teacher within the Ottawa Carleton School Board for 23 years, is one of dozens of teachers being "investigated" by the board, which led to him leaving the profession in 2022, he says. His last position was a French teacher at Sir Robert Borden High School.
Richard Smith taught core French for 23 years. He won awards and travelled the world teaching the accelerated integrated method, a methodology for teaching a second language known as AIM.
AIM is a particularly kinetic way of teaching. It involves gestures and motions for every part of speech from nouns to verb tenses. Smith follows a script of gestures. He smooths his hair to indicate a feminine noun and twirls an imaginary moustache for a masculine noun, for example.
Goalie goes the extra mile to serve as Senators' emergency backup
Emergency backup goalie Zach Dietz poses with his girlfriend, Charley Healey, in Senators colours on Saturday, Dec. 14, 2024. PHOTO SUPPLIED BY ZACK DIETZ
Who was that masked man? It's a bird! It's a plane! No, it was the Ottawa Senators' emergency backup goaltender (EBUG) trying to get to the Canadian Tire Centre to get dressed in case he was needed to face the Pittsburgh Penguins on Hockey Night in Canada.
The video of Zach Dietz, a 22-year-old goaltender with the University of Ottawa Gee-Gees, running with his equipment towards the rink on night of Saturday, Dec. 14 has gone viral on social media since it was posted and he'll have a great story to tell his kids down the road.
The mutiny at Buvette Daphnée
The restaurant Buvette Daphnée in Ottawa.
It was about two hours before the acclaimed ByWard Market restaurant Buvette Daphnée was to open its doors on a Friday night in late May. Servers were mopping the floor and setting up tables to conform with the night's reservations. Chefs were scrambling at their stations, trying to assemble the ingredients they needed for the upcoming service.
Unexpectedly, according to staff who were present, Dominique Dufour - the restaurant's celebrated chef and part owner - wanted a server to be fired. What made her decision all the more surprising was the allegation that it wasn't because of the server's performance, but rather because Dufour wanted to hire the server's boyfriend, a cook. Dufour had told staff that the restaurant's upper management had a rule that couples couldn't work together.
Jim Watson opens up about life after leaving office, public outrage over the LRT and Lansdowne 2.0
Jim Watson at Louis' Pizza in Ottawa.
During a decade-long tenure as Ottawa's mayor ending in late 2022, Jim Watson seemed to be everywhere. The joke he shared was that he'd show up to the opening of an envelope.
Now Watson's profile is considerably lower, in part because he's trying to adjust to life with colitis, the symptoms of which appeared during his final year in office. He's on his third different medication and says it seems to have stabilized his symptoms.
'By far the hottest, sweatiest show of my life': Here's what happened when Escapade was cancelled
After the festivities at Escapade were called off on Sunday, electronic-music fans flooded the ByWard Market. Many were hoping to get into 27 Club, where the official after party was scheduled to take place.
Sunday, June 23 was an uncharacteristically busy night in the nation's capital.
Thousands of people, glitter-clad and ready to rave - but with no festival to attend - flooded the streets of the ByWard Market, Centretown and surrounding neighbourhoods after one of Ottawa's major music festivals was cancelled.
There were 45,000 attendees expected to see the sold-out festival's final day, with the Chainsmokers and Armin Van Buuren among the biggest names set to perform.
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newsletters so we can keep you informed.

Document 236 of 500

Trudeau support hits rock bottom; New poll finds 69 per cent of Canadians think it's time for PM to step aside

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-support-hits-rock-bottom-new-poll-finds/docview/3148924659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+support+hits+rock+bottom%3B+New+poll+finds+69+per+cent+of+Canadians+think+it%27s+time+for+PM+to+step+aside&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All the while, nearly two dozen other Liberals have joined a growing list of those publicly demanding Trudeau's ouster, including New Brunswick MP Wayne Long, who said in an interview that Trudeau is "delusional."
But in terms of raw poll numbers, the Liberals are doing about the same as they were before the scandal broke on Dec. 16. Among poll respondents, 20 per cent said they intended to vote Liberal - about the same as the 21 per cent who said as much in a Dec. 3 Leger poll.
"The Liberals dropped a point; not a lot of movement," said Andrew Enns, an executive vice-president with Leger.
The stubbornness of the figures may reflect the fact that the Liberals have already hit their electoral floor - the hardcore base of supporters who will vote Liberal under almost any circumstances.
According to Enns, the "bottom" for the Liberals is "probably around 19, 20 per cent." Enns noted that was about as low as it got for then Liberal leader Michael Ignatieff, who infamously led the party to its worst defeat in a federal election in 2011.
It's also about the same as the historic bottom for the NDP. Although New Democrats typically benefit from hemorrhaging Liberal support, the party has hovered at around 20 per cent all throughout the Trudeau government's epic fall from grace.
In this latest poll, the NDP came in at 19 per cent.
As the Trudeau government enters the second week of a very public internal feud, a new Leger survey finds that the scandal hasn't done all that much to hurt its poll numbers - but that might be because Liberal support has already hit rock bottom.
The new Leger poll finds that a record 69 per cent of Canadians now think Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should resign. It also recorded new highs for government dissatisfaction: 72 per cent of respondents said they were dissatisfied with the Trudeau government.
Following the sudden resignation from cabinet of Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland last week, more than a third of the 153 Liberals in caucus are now resolved to pursue Trudeau's immediate resignation.
According to CBC, a conference call of the party's 50 Ontario MPs came to a "consensus" that their top priority needed to be Trudeau's removal.
But even if the Liberals can go no lower, they remain on course for a devastating defeat at the polls that would plunge them into the political wilderness for at least a decade. Every single electoral projection of the last six months have the Conservatives winning the next election in a landslide.
Enns noted that while the Liberals were able to win a majority government just four years after their 2011 drubbing, those circumstances aren't likely to repeat.
"For one, (Conservative Leader Pierre) Poilievre will likely win a larger majority than (Stephen) Harper, making the road back for the Liberals all that more difficult," Enns told the National Post.
Depending on how the NDP performs in a number of key Liberal-held ridings, it's possible that the next House of Commons could be one in which a Conservative supermajority faces the Bloc Québécois in opposition.
Enns called it "a likelihood" that the Official Opposition in the next Parliament could be Bloc.
The separatist party is now the clear favourite in Quebec, garnering 39 per cent of public support, against 29 per cent for the Conservatives and 20 per cent for the Liberals. That could be enough to yield the Bloc at least 40 of Quebec's 78 federal seats.
It would be the second time in which His Majesty's Loyal Opposition has been occupied by a separatist party that explicitly seeks Canada's dissolution.
The first time was in 1993, when the Bloc Québécois similarly capitalized on the collapse of an incumbent party.
In the chaos following the near-annihilation of the ruling Progressive Conservatives, the Bloc under Lucien Bouchard was able to form opposition against a Liberal majority with a caucus of 54 seats.
Dissident Liberals calling for Trudeau's ouster have made the argument that it's a simple numbers game: According to polls, the longer Trudeau is at the helm, the worse the defeat will be.
Trudeau isn't even all that popular among Liberal diehards. In the new Leger poll, even among the hardcore 20 per cent who still intended to vote Liberals, a third of them said Justin Trudeau should resign. Although elections are decided by swing voters, every party has a core of supporters that mark their ballots the same regardless of political trends.
The poll of 1,521 Canadians was conducted between Dec. 20 and 22 via an online panel. Leger weighted the results to ensure a representative sample of the Canadian population. A margin of error cannot be associated with a non-probability sample in an online survey.
National Post

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Today's letters: Christmas is for all Canadians, regardless of faith

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-christmas-is-all-canadians/docview/3148722247/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Christmas+is+for+all+Canadians%2C+regardless+of+faith&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Christmas can bring all Canadians together
I write to you from Alberta, as an Ottawan who now lives in that province.
Christmas is almost here. TV, flyer and internet ads abound. Businesses urge everyone to spend until it hurts. Credit card companies will bump your limit to crazy amounts without your asking. To many of us, it must seem the season is "all about the money." However, it shouldn't be.
Christmas is a Christian tradition celebrating the birth of Christ, whom Christians believe was the son of God. Other major religions of the world believe otherwise. Some of them have different prophets. All of them, though, belive in a supreme being or supreme force that overlies the relatively short lives of men and women on Earth.
Christmas in Canada should be more than an annual ritual of spending and profit-taking. If you're Christian, it's certainly a time to celebrate the birth of Jesus. It should, however, still be an uplifting time of year for all Canadians - regardless of our different faiths. It should be a time to recognize a collective spirit of togetherness, of belonging, of caring, of giving to others. In short, of being "Canadian."
All of us should recognize it's a time of year we should come together to celebrate our collective humanity, regardless of our individual beliefs. A time when we all reach out to help those less fortunate. A time to celebrate that we all share the common good fortune to live in a country that accepts others for who they are and not because of the colour of their skin, their language or their religion. A country that integrates and celebrates the best traits, the best traditions and the best principles of all our various religions and cultures from around the world.
It's a time of year for each and every Canadian to celebrate not only themselves but also every other Canadian in our wonderful, polyglot country no matter who they are, no matter what they believe and no matter where they live. It's a time to say to everyone else regardless of their religion or beliefs - very sincerely, and from our hearts - "Merry Christmas and may God bless you."
David Merritt, St. Albert, Alta.
Christmas is a time to focus on the positive
I've surpassed my complaint threshhold for 2024, so this week I'm trying to bring good feelings to a conscious level.
Even in this cranky world, there are in-person workers such as fitness class instructors, restaurant servers, health-care office staff, personal support workers, grocery clerks and many more who smile and make you feel better for interacting with them.
And there are strangers who make eye contact as well, and offer simple compliments. And volunteers at running races who cheer you on and say "great job!" And the smiling non-judgmental team at Pitch (Im)Perfect Singers who enable us non-singers to sing.
On a personal level, my heart was warmed recently by my neighbour who came over to help shovel the plough ridge as we tried to get the car out of the driveway; two separate friends who picked up the bill for my meals when we went out, without saying beforehand they were doing that; and Tom at my longtime garage on Iris Street who searched for and found unrusted car parts in New Orleans to keep my 2008 Yaris going for another year.
Thank you, too, to the two newspaper carriers who arrive on my doorstep in the dark of night, and always kindly put my newspapers in my mailbox instead of tossing them on the wet sidewalk.
They are all part of what poet George Eliot calls "the choir invisible, whose music is the gladness of the world."
Louise Rachlis, Ottawa
Any kind deed adds happiness
Over the past few months, I've been giving out colourful little cards with words such as "mindfulness," "smile," "thank you" and "inspired life" on them, and when they're opened there's a quotation printed inside.
I've given them to family, friends, cashiers, servers at restaurants and random people in places such as grocery stores. Almost always people seem surprised, then delighted, and also very thankful after receiving them.
I'm writing to share this somewhat surprising and definitely wonderful experience simply as a reminder of how tiny actions can brighten the day for others and even ourselves.
I believe any kind deed definitely adds beauty and happiness to the world and also creates a ripple effect. Therefore, let's remember to try to do what good we can when we can because it matters and we may never even know the full impact of what we may consider a little thing.
Renuka Ria Maraj, Ottawa
Will airport train link run when needed?
Re: 'Excited' Amilcar shows off Trillium Line LRT, Dec. 16.
There has been absolutely no mention of train frequency, especially for the
airport link on the new Trillium Line.
A lot of us take package vacations to the south in winter. Most have very early flight times, 6 a.m. or shortly thereafter, which require being at the airport three hours earlier. And, of course, we have to get from home (no cars) to the nearest LRT station, anywhere from Blair station to Tunney's Pasture.
How (in)convenient will this LRT link be for us?
Roger Webber-Taylor, Ottawa
If only the public were this excited about the Trillium Line
I see that the general manager of transit services for the City of Ottawa is praising the functionality and reliability of the Trillium Line a full three weeks before it goes into (limited) service.
Wouldn't it be nice if it was the travelling public who were excited?
George Smith, Ottawa
Meanwhile, buses are being cancelled
It is interesting to note that on the very day OC Transpo entertained members of the press on the Trillium line 2, OC Transpo cancelled 10 bus trips on the substitute line 2 bus service. This level of cancellation is quite common.
The new line passed its testing many weeks ago, yet members of the paying, travelling public continue to face last-minute bus cancellations in cold weather. (Information on bus cancellations comes from the OC Transpo app.)
Colin J. Churcher, Ottawa
National Arts Centre fails its audience
Ever since the pandemic, the National Arts Centre's music department has hit upon a way to save some money. Under the hollow slogan "We've gone digital!" it has done away with providing patrons a printed program. A year ago, it relented and resorted to giving attendees a one-page handout listing the artists and the titles of the evening's offering, a dumbing-down practice if there ever was one.
Comprehensive program notes are still produced but they are accessible only via a smartphone, or you can print them out, typically comprising 15 to 20 pages in a poorly designed layout. If you are a subscriber you receive an email the day before the concert with the link. If you bought a one-off ticket you'll learn about this only via the QR code on the one-pager.
How many patrons actually access these program notes is unknown. I have repeatedly challenged NAC management to conduct a survey of their subscribers (by mail) to assess their feelings about this new policy. I know they've received complaints about it from several quarters. At their annual meeting last February, it was the first and hotly debated topic of discussion. To no avail.
On a recent Saturday, this failure to serve its patrons appropriately was compounded because the performance was a recital of songs by Olivier Messiaen (in French) and John Zorn (in Finnish). The program notes included all the texts and translations but that was of no use because Southam Hall was kept dark. Instead, the stage was bathed in warm colours, apparently to create a "mood."
The NAC seems to be unaware that appreciation of vocal performances are greatly enhanced when one can follow the words. Any other music organization I know of understands this and acts accordingly. The NAC should wise up, bring back printed programs and keep the lights on during vocal performances.
Erwin Dreessen, Ottawa
(Erwin Dreessen's music reviews appear on substack at https://erwindreessen.substack.com/
Citizen is missing the story on the Byward Market
Re: What it's like in the ByWard Market, series.
I was saddened that once again to see The Citizen has focused on the same old subjects as in the past. Homelessness is a serious issue, but it is all over the city. "The Laff," a beer parlour, is an institution that everyone knows. Policeman Sebastien LeMay is a stalwart in our community who continues to make a huge contribution but the new police sub-station is in the Rideau Centre, not in the ByWard Market.
There are now many positive projects going forward in the ByWard Market as it moves toward serious recovery. The Citizen shows no evidence of moving around the Market and talking to different people; or touching base with the ByWard Market District Authority; or investigating the variety of interesting small retail stores, new cafés and patisseries, the seven independent art galleries plus the Ottawa School of Art and so on; all of which enrich life in this beleaguered district.
Everyone around here, including many new residents, is now working to bring the market back to good health. It should be noted that the population numbers in the area are growing. That is the real story of ByWard life.
It is long past time that the Citizen recognized what this "national site of historic importance" represents in the life of the city and what is under way to ensure it returns to good health and flourishes.
Sarah Jennings, Ottawa
Coal for Doug Ford, kudos for compassion
I was appalled when Premier Doug Ford tabled legislation to prohibit sleeping rough in encampments in public spaces. The biblical Sermon on the Mount may tell us that the poor are blessed because theirs is the kingdom of heaven, but in the kingdom of Ford, the homeless are to be punished and imprisoned.
I was greatly heartened by two articles in the Citizen Dec. 14 telling us about Ottawa Police Sgt. Sebastien Lemay and Ottawa Inner City block leader Carlos Daniel Diaz, who help the ByWard Market homeless with compassion, respect and grace.
They put us all to shame. May their tribes increase.
Peter Dykstra, Kanata
Public servant commutes: Stop complaining
Re: How new remote-work rules have caused commute woes for public servants, Dec. 12.
Where do I start? The federal worker featured in the Citizen who made the lifestyle decision to buy a home in Plaisance knew full well at the time that the job was in Ottawa, and that at some point there would be a need to drive to Ottawa for work.
The worker has three simple choices: 1) Move closer to the job; 2) Find a job closer to home; 3) Simply suck it up about commuting times and be thankful to have a golden "job for life" in the federal public service.
I'm tired of hearing federal workers complain about how difficult their commuting lives are. In fact, I'm tired of hearing them complain about a variety of things that the rank and file worker population endures.
Frank Scott, Ottawa
You have a choice about your job
I am retired. During my career I, too, had to drive two or more hours a day to a job I loved. Public servants saying that a commute had a very negative impact on their productivity to me is nonsense. They are lucky to have a job in this economy. I and many others made a choice to stay at a job we loved in spite of gas costs.
Public servants have a choice to find another job that pays incredible benefits and salary. I doubt that many of them will.
Sid Cratzbarg, Ottawa
Even in the city, it's a long commute
Re: Letter, Public servants can make choices, Dec. 14.
The letter writer implies that everyone should live closer to work, ideally within the city.
I live near Pinecrest Road, well within the city boundaries, and it still takes more than an hour to get to work downtown on public transit (I don't drive) thanks to: 1) my bus stop is 3/4-of-a-kilometre walk from my house; 2) LRT construction detours and bottlenecks abound; 3) I have to transfer at Tunney's Pasture to a train.
The letter-writer needs to understand that not everyone's situation is the same as his. I invite him and/or the Citizen to tag along with me on my way to work and enjoy the experience.
Carolyn Holmes, Ottawa
One family's plan to help the downtrodden
This Christmas, we are going to invite to dinner someone who has been downtrodden, beaten and ignored.
Yes, we sent an invitation to our prime minister, Justin Trudeau.
Pat McAlpine, Kanata

Young people's climate change lawsuit appealed to Supreme Court

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/young-peoples-climate-change-lawsuit-appealed/docview/3148721075/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Young+people%27s+climate+change+lawsuit+appealed+to+Supreme+Court&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has appealed a landmark environmental decision that said seven young plaintiffs could sue the province for failing to act on climate change.
The Ontario government last week filed an application for leave to appeal in the Supreme Court of Canada, setting the stage for what could be an important test of the Charter's application to environmental policy.
Fraser Thomson, climate director of Ecojustice, an environmental law charity, said the appeal had the potential to trigger "a generation-defining hearing" in Canada's highest court.
"It's one that could lead to recognizing that governments have constitutional obligations to address one of the most pressing emergencies we have ever faced," he said.
One of the seven young plaintiffs is Alexandra Neufeldt, 28, of Ottawa, who joined the legal bid to win provincial action on climate change. The lawsuit alleges the Ontario government's decision to claw back emissions targets in 2018 violated the Charter's guarantee of the rights to equality, life and security of the person.
"Our case has beaten the odds at every turn, and I believe it will continue to do so," said Neufeldt, a freelance fashion designer. "My hope is that our fight helps reshape Canadian law and holds the Ontario government - and all governments - accountable for fueling the climate crisis."
Neufeldt said the case held enormous consequences. "We don't have anything in the Charter about a right to a safe climate, and this could potentially change that," Neufeldt said in an interview.
In October, the Court of Appeal for Ontario said the group's lawsuit could proceed in a decision hailed by environmental activists.
The decision overturned a lower-court ruling that dismissed the lawsuit as a "positive rights case" - a case that established a novel legal right. The appeal court said a new hearing must determine whether the government's actions on greenhouse gas emissions complied with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
The three-justice panel said "it is indisputable that, as a result of climate change, the appellants and Ontarians in general are experiencing an increased risk of death and an increased risk to the security of the person."
The lawsuit was triggered by the Progressive Conservative government's decision in 2018 to cut back greenhouse gas emissions targets in Ontario. Premier Doug Ford's government reduced the province's emission goals from 37 to 30 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030.
The province did not contest the existence of climate change, its risks to human health or the need for all countries to take action to mitigate its adverse effects.
Instead, lawyers for the Ontario government argued that its actions would have little or no effect on climate change given the scope of the problem and that the environmental impacts described by the young plaintiffs were not the result of the province's greenhouse gas policies.
Backed by lawyers from Ecojustice and Stockwoods LLP, the lawsuit contends the impact of climate change offends the Charter's equality provision because it will have a disproportionate impact on youth, particularly Indigenous youth, given the toll that wildfires and drought take on traditional hunting and fishing grounds.
Nader Hasan, a member of the Stockwoods legal firm and lead lawyer in the case, said that, if the Supreme Court grants leave, it will mark the first time the court considers whether a government's climate change policy can violate Charter rights.
"The stakes have never been higher," he said, "and the seven youth at the helm of this case are committed to safeguarding our rights to a safe, healthy, livable future."
Neufeldt says the case is important because the potentially catastrophic impact of climate change will be borne by those who are young today or as yet unborn.
"I joined this case because the climate crisis is impacting the way young people like me can dream about our futures - about building homes, businesses, and communities," she said.
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newslettersso we can keep you informed.

Jewish group challenges decision to keep alleged Nazis' names private

Document 239 of 500

Mayor Mark Sutcliffe hopes for a $36M lifeline for OC Transpo. What if the money doesn't come?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mayor-mark-sutcliffe-hopes-36m-lifeline-oc/docview/3147833848/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor+Mark+Sutcliffe+hopes+for+a+%2436M+lifeline+for+OC+Transpo.+What+if+the+money+doesn%27t+come%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OC Transpo service cuts, another fare increase, pulling money from the City of Ottawa's rainy-day reserves, even a special transit levy on top of one agreed to in the 2025 budget are all on the table if the city doesn't get the $36 million from "higher levels of government" it's counting on.
And, with no mention of transit money for Ottawa in the Liberals' fall economic statement and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government seemingly on the verge of collapse, time is running out on Mayor Mark Sutcliffe's "Fairness for Ottawa" campaign.
"I think there's a willingness by the federal government to help us," Sutcliffe said Friday, a few hours after NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said he would call for a non-confidence vote when Parliament returned in the new year.
"Things are moving in the right direction and we've travelled a long way down the road and we're very close to the finish line of being able to announce some help for Ottawa, but the political volatility right now on Parliament Hill is my main concern," Sutcliffe said.
"I hope we're able to move forward on something before that gets in the way, before we're into an election campaign or a leadership race or some other thing that would prevent the announcement from happening."
OC Transpo is facing a $120-million operating deficit in 2025. Fares are going up five per cent on Jan. 1 and an increase in the transit levy accounts for more than one-third of the 3.9-per-cent tax increase residents face next year. Even so, the city has added the $36-million placeholder in the transit budget for money it hopes to receive from the provincial and federal governments to balance its books.
Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy confirmed earlier in December that the province would pitch in money for transit if the federal government did so as well. But, despite months of lobbying by the mayor, there has been no federal announcement.
Instead, the Liberals' fall economic statement committed money for more studies of a sixth bridge over the Ottawa River and a proposed tramway linking Aylmer and Gatineau to downtown Ottawa. Money to run OC Transpo? Nada.
Sutcliffe said he wasn't surprised by that, but remained hopeful.
"I've spoken to representatives from the federal government since the fall economic statement and they're continuing to send encouraging signals about the decision-making process on some help for Ottawa," he said. "We're getting closer."
And if the money isn't there?
"If that doesn't come, by February, say by the end of February, we'll have to look at other ways of dealing with that," Sutcliffe said.
"We'd have to find other solutions and all options are on the table. I'm not saying that any of these are desirable, but we'd have to look at increasing fares, a special transit levy, service reductions, other efficiencies ... We'd have to look at all of those things."
Neil Saravanamuttoo, director of CitySHAPES Ottawa, is dubious that help will come from Parliament Hill.
"The mayor seems optimistic that transit funding will be coming in early 2025. I have to admit I'm not quite so optimistic as he is," Saravanamuttoo said.
"The federal government has been crystal, crystal clear that they do not do transit operations. They do transit capital costs, but they see transit operations as the responsibility of the provinces.
"If you think about it, if the feds were to provide operational funding to Ottawa, they would have to do the same for every city in the country. I just can't see that happening. They're not prepared to set a precedent and open up the floodgates."
Saravanamuttoo also warned of another transit pitfall: OC Transpo's assumption that ridership will continue to increase and its projection of $200 million in fare revenue in 2025.
"When we look back over the past several years, fare revenue has been consistently lower than expected. That fare revenue may come in closer to $150 million. That would not be unusual at all. We're looking at this $36-million operational deficit, but there could be a fare deficit as well.
"At some point in the year, there will have to a reckoning. The city will just have to figure out a way to manage it."
A spokesperson for Jenna Sudds, the Minister of Families, Children and Social Development, a former Ottawa city councillor and the ranking MP for the National Capital Region, said the government's talks with Ottawa were continuing.
"Minister Sudds has always been committed to finding solutions that best serve the people of Ottawa - and will continue to do just that," her director of communications, Margaret Jacques, wrote in an email. "Minister Sudds remains in active conversations with the mayor to ensure Ottawa residents are served well. As operational costs for public transit fall under provincial jurisdiction, we encourage the Province of Ontario to work with the City of Ottawa to address ongoing concerns to provide stable and predictable support for the community."
A file photo of Mayor Mark Sutcliffe kicking off his Fairness for Ottawa campaign at a news conference in August.
But Sutcliffe says there are other ways for the feds to help. As an example, he cited that the provincial government is considering taking on the costs of maintaining Regional Road 174, something now paid for by the city.
"Cash is cash. When that's completed, that would allow us to move money for other areas of our budget," Sutcliffe said. "Of course, we'd welcome federal help with transit, but there are lots of ways that the federal government can help us."
As a last resort, the city could use its reserve funds to keep OC Transpo whole, but that is not a sustainable solution.
"We have some (reserves), but those funds are an emergency basis only," Sutcliffe said. "When you use reserves for operational challenges, you can only use them once. That's not ideal."
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newslettersso we can keep you informed.

Jewish group challenges decision to keep alleged Nazis' names private
David McGuinty named minister of public safety

Subject: Capital costs; Federal government; Fares

Business indexing term: Subject: Capital costs

Company / organization: Name: OC Transpo; NAICS: 485112

Identifier / keyword: transpo; service; another; increase; pull ing; money

Title: Mayor Mark Sutcliffe hopes for a $36M lifeline for OC Transpo. What if the money doesn't come?

Publication title: The Ottawa Citizen (Online); Ottawa

Publication year: 2024

Document 240 of 500

Shuffle poised to be short-lived; Trudeau adds 8 new ministers to cabinet as ouster looms large

Author: Trépanier, Antoine; Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Trépanier, Antoine; Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/shuffle-poised-be-short-lived-trudeau-adds-8-new/docview/3147804792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Shuffle+poised+to+be+short-lived%3B+Trudeau+adds+8+new+ministers+to+cabinet+as+ouster+looms+large&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Tr%C3%A9panier%2C+Antoine%3BL%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced 12 changes to his cabinet Friday morning, bringing in eight new ministers in one of his biggest cabinet shuffles since he was elected nine years ago. But they may only be in place for a few weeks, as the NDP is now promising to topple the government once Parliament returns in January.
Minutes before the shuffle, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said his caucus would put forward a "clear motion of non-confidence" at the earliest opportunity when MPs are back in Ottawa.
With all opposition parties now committed to bringing down the government, that means Canadians could be headed to the polls earlier than the next scheduled vote in October.
Trudeau had been considering changes to his cabinet for some time, but the blockbuster resignation of his deputy prime minister and finance minister Chrystia Freeland on Monday forced him to act quickly. The position of minister responsible for Canada-U.S. relations, which according to a Liberal source was offered to Freeland and caused her to resign, did not appear on the list of new cabinet appointees.
New Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc will no longer be minister of public safety and democratic institutions. Longtime Ottawa MP David McGuinty will become the new minister of public safety.
McGuinty, whose brother is former Ontario premier Dalton Mc-Guinty, was chair of the national security and intelligence committee of parliamentarians. The committee worked extensively on foreign interference.
Treasury Board president Anita Anand and Veterans Affairs Minister Ginette Petitpas Taylor also held several portfolios for weeks before Friday's shuffle.
Anand will remain minister of transport, a portfolio she assumed in September when Pablo Rodriguez resigned, and will also be responsible for internal trade.
Petitpas Taylor will replace her at Treasury Board.
Trudeau also named former Ontario Liberal leadership candidate Nathaniel Erskine-Smith to cabinet as minister of housing. The Toronto MP had recently said he would be leaving federal politics and has been an outspoken member of Parliament, sometimes voting against his own party. He will replace Nova Scotia MP Sean Fraser in the role.
This year alone, nine cabinet ministers have resigned, announced they would not run for re-election or been fired. The new cabinet will not include a single Alberta representative. Meanwhile, Quebec will now have 11 ministers, including the prime minister. Manitoba MP Terry Duguid, who will serve as Liberal national campaign co-chair for the next election, becomes minister of sport and minister responsible for Prairie Economic Development Canada.
After National Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau recently announced she would not run again, Trudeau decided to appoint Sherbrooke MP Élisabeth Brière to replace her.
Another Quebecer, Rachel Bendayan, becomes minister of official languages and associate minister of public safety.
Steven MacKinnon will become minister of employment, workforce development and labour, and Gary Anandasangaree becomes minister of Crown-Indigenous relations and northern affairs and minister responsible for the Canadian Northern Economic Development Agency.
Other new faces who were sworn in Friday morning include Ruby Sahota as minister of democratic institutions and minister responsible for the Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario; Darren Fisher as minister of veterans affairs and associate minister of national defence; and Newfoundland MP Joanne Thompson, who becomes minister of seniors.
There are a number of notable absences in the list of new appointees, including MPs who served as parliamentary secretaries for years. One of them, Toronto-area MP Rob Oliphant, has been parliamentary secretary for the minister of foreign affairs since 2019 and was seen as a prospective cabinet minister.
But on Friday, Oliphant called on Trudeau to step down as leader. He wrote in a letter that he came to that conclusion after consulting with his riding association.
Speaking to reporters, the newly sworn ministers reiterated their faith in Trudeau and called on their Liberal peers to stand united.
"I'm simply going to say we're all here today because we absolutely support the prime minister," said Petitpas Taylor.
Anand, who was visibly shaken after the news of Freeland's departure, said she had been thinking a lot about the week's event and had spoken with Trudeau a number of times.
"I believe this is a moment where we need to stand united," she said, mentioning the threat of tariffs from the incoming U.S. administration.
Duguid echoed their message of unity. "It's important that we keep Canadians'concerns about affordability, about growing our economy front and centre," he said.
Erskine-Smith, known for his outspokenness, said out loud what everyone else was thinking.
"I understand there's going to be a short runway. I'm not blind to that," he said.
But he said he would make the most of it as housing minister in the little time that appears to be left.
"If I can make a small difference, if I can make a big difference, I want it to be the biggest difference that I can. That's why I joined politics in the first place."
National Post

Document 241 of 500

In with the BOLD; Charlie Angus - politician, activist and musician - uses the past to challenge the future

Author: Portman, Jamie

Publication info: Portman, Jamie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/with-bold-charlie-angus-politician-activist/docview/3147804599/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+with+the+BOLD%3B+Charlie+Angus+-+politician%2C+activist+and+musician+-+uses+the+past+to+challenge+the+future&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.12&amp;au=Portman%2C+Jamie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Dangerous Memory: Coming of Age in the Decade of Greed Charlie Angus Anansi Her name was Shannon and she had just arrived across the border from Michigan with her three children. They were in desperate need of help when they arrived in Toronto. They had no place to stay, no knowledge of the city, no entry papers. They had little chance of qualifying as landed immigrants, and would likely face deportation if they approached social services.
So Shannon turned to Angelus House, a refuge for the homeless that had been established in Toronto's South Riverdale area by an idealistic young punk rocker named Charlie Angus and his girlfriend Brit, who would later become his wife. The year was 1988 and in their own humble way they sought to ease an urban housing shortage that was already surfacing more than three decades before it would explode into a national crisis.
Shannon's story is one of many that Angus tells in his new memoir, a chronicle of hardship and hope titled Dangerous Memory. She is also one of the reasons for the emergence of a Juno Award-winning song called Boomtown. Angus and fellow musician Andrew Cash wrote it in protest against a mounting tide of homelessness in Toronto.
"It was a song about a family unable to afford to live in that city," Angus says now. "Songs were the way I articulated things back then. At that time, in the late 1980s, this notion of Toronto as a boomtown and world-class city was getting heavy promotion, but what I was seeing at the grassroots level was a city that was becoming more and more unlivable - but back then I never imagined the extreme nature of where it is today."
Angus is 62 now, and soon to retire as MP for the northern Ontario seat he has held for the NDP for the last two decades. He knows he's leaving at a time of anxiety and uncertainty within the country, but as he looks back on his own youth, he remembers that even though things looked grim 40 years ago, it was still possible to perform good works.
His book takes a caustic view of globalization, corporate greed and the economic theories of Milton Friedman, seeing these as key reasons for today's social and economic disparities - particularly an urban housing crisis further exacerbated by gentrification, flip-flopping real-estate ventures and governments defaulting on their obligations. But Angus has also delivered both a valuable social document and an engaging memory piece, one anchored to a fervent belief that solutions to serious social issues need not fall prey to knee-jerk, left-right divisiveness.
When Shannon and her kids entered Canada, she was convinced that they could make it in their adopted country. As Angus feared, she would fail and finally retreat across the border. Her plight was agonizingly familiar during the years that he and Brit ran a haven for the destitute modelled on the Catholic Worker House movement that had taken root in America during the Depression.
"We weren't a flophouse," he tells Postmedia. "We were a family and we believed that the only way to give people a place where they could come off the street was to provide a community that was a 'family.'We couldn't take everyone in, and it was very hard deciding who could come and who couldn't. And by far the most heartbreaking stories were the mothers with young children who were falling through the cracks at a dramatic rate because there simply weren't the services that they needed available."
In later years, Angus would sometimes be denied communion by his church and vilified by the right wing Catholic media for his activism and, in particular, support of same-sex marriage. He describes his current relationship with his faith as "complex" but he's forthright in saying that he's the product of an era when the church of his upbringing was driven by a strong belief in social justice.
Those principles were ingrained in the youngster who quit school in his teens and, with his buddy Cash, founded the punk rock group L'etranger. The book's third chapter - A Punk Kid on the Edge - is an entertaining account of performing in scruffy bars across Canada, often in unsafe situations.
Things are more peaceful for Angus's current group, Grievous Angels, which recently released a new album.
"But back then, we were extremely naive ... especially going into blue-collar towns in the early 1980s where the jobs were disappearing and the bars were pretty tough. We needed protection so we normally travelled with two or three big guys. They were the muscle for a band that was preaching non-violence ..."
Yes - non-violence. There was one night when a bunch of neo-Nazi goons disrupted a concert and spat in the young Charlie's face after he spoke out against them.
At the time, he felt an immediate need to push back; now decades older, Angus is reflective on what brought him to this point.
"I grew up at a time when a rear window in the Catholic church was teaching its young to be activists and speak out for social justice. I came out of this experience. But then there was also punk rock which was heavily political ... and inspired me to be more political. So yeah, we were seeing things happening in our youth culture like having bottles thrown on us and being spat on by Nazi punks. So we had to be a voice and speak up. There was always faith of a sort involved."
Angus was 22 when he - a struggling musician who washed dishes on the side - and Brit managed to scrape together $85,000 and purchase the dwelling that would become Angelus House during the 1980s. He wryly notes that to understand the nature of Toronto's affordable housing crisis, you need only consider that this property would now have a price tag of about $1.8 million.
"We were seeing it in its earliest manifestations back then ... What a change from the notion that we should build a lot of houses to create investments and security and build a modern middle class. That was what was dismantled from the 1980s on."
There were moments when he and Brit were terrified by someone they had taken in. There were occasional thefts - Angus still mourns the loss of a treasured bass guitar. But he looks back on those times with affection.
"South Riverdale was this magical community. I loved the neighbourhood. It was very working class with a real mix of incomes, but there was not the social divide that exists now. It's shocking to see how it's been transformed for the worse - places for the super wealthy while others are homeless on the street."
So why this book? He worries about today's young generation "who may be facing serious existential dread in a time of crisis." He looks at his own earlier years and the efforts to make the impossible prove possible. "I'm saying that we have been here before. I'm hoping that this might serve as an inspiration for people to say - yeah, things are serious but maybe we need to try new things, to take crazy chances ... to be bold."
To be bold. Just as he and his friends were all those years ago.

Lump of coal for Ford, kudos for compassion

Author: Dykstra, Peter

Publication info: Dykstra, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lump-coal-ford-kudos-compassion/docview/3147804557/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lump+of+coal+for+Ford%2C+kudos+for+compassion&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=Dykstra%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I was appalled when Premier Doug Ford tabled legislation to prohibit sleeping rough in encampments in public spaces.
The biblical Sermon on the Mount may tell us that the poor are blessed because theirs is the kingdom of heaven, but in the kingdom of Ford, the homeless are to be punished and imprisoned.
I was greatly heartened by two articles in the Citizen Dec. 14 telling us about Ottawa Police Sgt. Sebastien Lemay and Ottawa Inner City block leader Carlos Daniel Diaz, who help the ByWard Market homeless with compassion, respect and grace.
They put us all to shame. May their tribes increase.

Document 243 of 500

Penalty reduced for comment about shooting fellow officer; Toronto Police; Quip did not constitute threat, commission says

Author: Lambie, Chris

Publication info: Lambie, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/penalty-reduced-comment-about-shooting-fellow/docview/3147565936/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Penalty+reduced+for+comment+about+shooting+fellow+officer%3B+Toronto+Police%3B+Quip+did+not+constitute+threat%2C+commission+says&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.4&amp;au=Lambie%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Talking to his partner about shooting a fellow Toronto police officer in the head did not constitute a threat or justify reducing the officer's rank for six months, the Ontario Civilian Police Commission has ruled.
After Const. Kehui Li pleaded guilty in June 2023 to one count of discreditable conduct for making the comment after a tense domestic call, Supt. Shane Branton reduced his rank from third to fourth class constable for six months at a hearing of the Toronto police tribunal. The demotion would have cost Li about $5,470, according to the Toronto Police Service pay scale.
"The hearing officer erred in principle by erroneously assigning heightened weight to the seriousness of the misconduct by deciding it was tantamount to a criminal offence," said a recent decision from the three-member Ontario Civilian Police Commission.
Li appealed Branton's sixmonth demotion, arguing docking him three days'pay would be a more appropriate penalty.
The commission allowed his appeal. It decided that forfeiting seven days of pay, or about $1,680, "addresses the seriousness of (Li's) conduct and provides a deterrent effect, while also giving weight to progressive discipline and (his) ability to reform."
But the constable still has to take the anger management program Branton ordered him to complete.
Li was working the night shift at 11 Division on Feb. 6, 2022, when he got a call to take photographs of a crime victim.
After another officer he'd had a disagreement with left the scene, Li told his partner: "I was so mad that I was going to shoot him in the head."
Li "made the comment in a monotone voice, but it shocked (his partner) who asked if it was a joke and said she did not find the comment funny," according to an agreed statement of facts in the case.
After reflecting, Li decided he needed to work things out with the officer he'd had a conflict with that night, so he called him and apologized, saying he'd been caught up in the moment. They planned to "sit down to talk things out," but that never happened.
There's nothing in the agreed statement of facts to indicate Li or his partner ever told the other officer about the shooting comment. Li made it when he and his partner were alone together in their police vehicle.
Li argued successfully that Branton made a "fundamental error" by equating his shooting comment to a criminal threat.
At the penalty hearing last year, Branton said Li's "conduct itself was on the serious end of the spectrum because even though it did not result in a criminal charge, in some situations it could have."
The superintendent also said for "a fully uniformed officer equipped with a firearm, to threaten to shoot another officer in the head is unconscionable."
Li had argued his "utterance was not a threat" as it was "made in the past tense" in private, after the officer he was complaining about had left the scene.
But Branton found last year that the comment was a threat.
"The public has an expectation that officers will behave professionally at all times," the superintendent said at the time. "PC Li's response of threatening to shoot a co-worker over a disagreement is completely unacceptable and unimaginable. With the increase in gun violence and the scrutiny of the police it is concerning that after all the training PC Li had very recently received, he chose to escalate a situation to threatening a person with a firearm."
The commission agreed with Li that Branton "erred in principle" by considering Li's statement to his partner a criminal threat The Toronto Police Service argued that Li's "words were meant to intimidate and instill a sense of fear."
Branton's conclusion that Li's conduct "met all of the elements of the criminal offence of uttering threats erroneously heightened the seriousness of the threat," said the appeal decision.
"The commission finds the hearing officer's analysis of the seriousness of the misconduct, and its negative impact on the public's trust in policing, cannot be divorced from his repeated emphasis that the utterance in question was a 'death threat'that he found met the elements of a criminal offence."
National Post

Subject: Threats; Firearms

Document 244 of 500

A rising chorus for Trudeau to go; Liberal MPs increasingly souring on pm, but leblanc says cabinet unwavering in support

Author: Laura Dhillon Kane

Publication info: Laura Dhillon Kane. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rising-chorus-trudeau-go-liberal-mps-increasingly/docview/3147565226/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+rising+chorus+for+Trudeau+to+go%3B+Liberal+MPs+increasingly+souring+on+pm%2C+but+leblanc+says+cabinet+unwavering+in+support&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Laura+Dhillon+Kane&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's political crisis is deepening, with more members of his Liberal party publicly calling for him to step aside and give a new leader a chance before an election in 2025.
Chad Collins, an Ontario member of Parliament, said that around 50 elected Liberals - perhaps more - are part of a growing group that wants the prime minister's resignation. Other Liberals in the anti-Trudeau camp have given similar numbers. That would be about a third of the 153-person Liberal contingent in the House of Commons.
Monday's resignation of Chrystia Freeland, Trudeau's finance minister and his longtime deputy, was a massive shock that has irreparably damaged the prime minister, Collins said in an interview.
Freeland said she quit after being told she would be moved to a different role in the cabinet. Trudeau delivered that news on Friday, she said - just three days before she was due for a major speech that would update the country on its fiscal and economic situation.
"I don't know who's giving him advice. I can guess. It's not good advice," Collins said. "But the buck stops with him with the decisions he makes, and we're now seeing the fallout that's come with what many would consider a very poor decision.
"In terms of who the successor is, I don't know at this point whether or not we could do much worse," he said.
Still, Trudeau's new finance minister insisted on Thursday that Trudeau has the full support of his cabinet to stay on as prime minister.
Dominic L eBlanc also sidestepped a question about whether he would like to lead the Liberal party.
"If the prime minister has the full support of his cabinet, then why would we contemplate what happens when he decides to leave?" he said.
Trudeau is expected to shuffle his cabinet on Friday to replace five ministers who said they won't re-offer in the next election.
Earlier Thursday, Justice Minister Arif Virani avoided offering a clear defence of Trudeau when asked whether he thought he should resign.
"Decisions will be taken by the parties that are involved," he said, steering the discussion back to his announcement about preventing wrongful convictions. "I have absolute confidence in the prime minister in terms of what he has asked me to do. That is serve as a minister of justice who defends people's rights."
In a social media post, Toronto MP and foreign affairs parliamentary secretary Rob Oliphant said he called for an "urgent meeting" of his riding association executive Thursday before taking a public stand on the leadership question.
"What's best for Canada comes first and our party comes second," he wrote.
Trudeau has been under pressure to leave for months. In June, the Liberals lost a special election in a Toronto riding they had held for decades. In September, they lost a seat in Montreal in similar fashion. Soon after, about two dozen Liberal lawmakers signed a letter asking him to go.
But Freeland's resignation - coming at a time when Canada's economy faces the threat of tariffs from the incoming Trump administration - has turned bubbling discontent into a full-scale crisis for Trudeau. The party also lost yet another byelection the same day she quit.
The prime minister has cancelled all of his usual yearend television interviews and said almost nothing publicly since her departure, other than some brief comments at two Liberal events.
Polls suggest the Conservative party, led by Pierre Poilievre, is on course to win a majority government in the next election.
"This isn't just about one man - it's about saving our party from a historic defeat," Wayne Long, another Liberal MP from New Brunswick, wrote in an open letter on Wednesday.
More Liberals will step away from politics if Trudeau tries to stick around, Collins warned.
"I think the risk he runs is that he'll have a skeleton crew of experienced elected representatives."
Jenica Atwin, a Liberal who serves as parliamentary secretary to a cabinet minister, told a newspaper in her home province of New Brunswick that Trudeau should leave and she won't run for re-election if he stays.
Bloomberg, with a file from The Canadian Press

Subject: Prime ministers

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: A rising chorus for Trudeau to go; Liberal MPs increasingly souring on pm, but leblanc says cabinet unwavering in support

Publication title: The Ottawa Citizen; Ottawa, Ont.

First page: NP.1

Publication year: 2024

Section: Canada

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Ottawa, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada
