Document 245 of 500

Today's letters: Richmond Road businesses in Ottawa deserve help

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-richmond-road-businesses-ottawa/docview/3147309001/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Richmond+Road+businesses+in+Ottawa+deserve+help&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Give these businesses a rent rebate
Re: LRT construction guts Richmond Road businesses, Dec. 4.
The planned three-year "reconfiguration" of Richmond Road, on top of years of LRT noise, dirt and confusion, is disgraceful. For the businesses at 911 Richmond Rd., it is a death knell - one caused by a lack of accountability on the part of the deadline-free companies designing and implementing the construction of the LRT, and the city's disgracefully sluggish timeframe on water and sewer main replacement.
Presumably the roadwork has been costed, except for the price that the businesses at 911 Richmond Road are paying. If they are given no compensation, the likelihood is that all of them will be gone within six months, if not sooner. The plan to transform "the area into a vibrant community hub" will in fact rest on the graves of the businesses whose already vibrant community hub has been destroyed.
Sympathy is not enough. One option is to provide these businesses with a rent rebate, back-dated to the time of the recent "reconfigurations" which have almost killed their operations. This would be a start in showing that the city has a sense of accountability and responsibility for the damage it is causing.
Ian Smillie, Ottawa
Fix Richmond Road signage, at least
The story about Richmond Road LRT construction comes as no surprise. For years I have hounded my councillor in Bay ward about the virtually useless signage for construction affecting cycle paths. The few times I have received emails from project managers, their responses are arrogant, spout city boilerplate and don't address what I actually asked them about.
Years ago, in one of Edmonton's busiest restaurant/shopping areas (Whyte Avenue at 82nd Avenue, the city did major construction through the heart of the business district. It was identical to the Richmond Road underground construction described in the Ottawa Citizen. The huge difference was that someone in Edmonton decided to only work on one lane of the street at a time.
Yes, there was disruption but it was simple to access both sides of the street, walking from a side street. The signage to access what and where was clear and could be found everywhere.
If the city managed its projects with an eye towards causing the least amount of pain to taxpayers instead of in such a city staff/contractor-centric manner, everyone would be better off.
Ian Stewart, Ottawa
HST cut should cover all kinds of diapers
Re: Ready for a GST holiday? The low-down on tax breaks, rebates and other government giveaways, Nov. 27.
Having survived into my senior years, I can attest that my bladder is failing and that adult diapers will soon be a welcome addition to my shopping list. So the recent announcement concerning a selective HST holiday from Dec. 14, 2024, to Feb. 15, 2025 was a little perplexing.
On reading the details, I noticed that adult diapers would not receive a HST holiday this festive season; however, wine and beer would be HST-exempt. This had me pondering if my cognitive decline had also accelerated.
In what world does one offer beer and wine at a reduced price while forcing seniors like me, on a fixed income, to pay taxes for disposing of it? In truth, I had long wondered why diapers for any age group were subject to HST in the first place. If feminine hygiene products such as sanitary napkins and tampons are HST-exempt, and provided free of charge to some employees, how is it possible that the HST hasn't been removed from all diapers?
I also suspect that the HST holiday will be a regressive tax rebate scheme with wealthier individuals benefitting far more than the working class, as the well-to-do wine and dine and stock up on non-perishables, and a bit more wine.
On reflection, perhaps it is not my cognitive decline that I should be most concerned about.
Robert St-Laurent, Ottawa
Patience, drivers - 'Yule' still get there
Recently on foot, in the car and during a recent bike ride, I experienced this annual, regrettable rite of the road: Yuletide driving.
It involves the usual failure to come to a full stop on right turns; cell phoning behind the wheel; lack of signalling au general; pointless speeding and even closer tailgating.
I am a cautious driver. If I am taking my time on a left turn at rush hour, it is because there is a visibility problem. Do not toot your horn at me after 3.5 microseconds; I will actually take more of your pointlessly precious time for the insult.
We're all in this together.
Thomas Brawn, Orléans
Bridge closure: When liability undercuts livability
Re: Rideau River bridge at Carleton University closed for winter - unexpectedly, Dec. 9.
Thank you for your article exposing the misguided barriers we face every winter throughout this city. But the barriers are just a symptom, not the disease.
Every winter after I cross the Pretoria bridge, I meet a chain barring access to the stairway to the Rideau Canal, often with a threat of prosecution. The many footsteps in the snow of this forbidden stairway make it clear that the chain merely adds to the danger.
Just around a blind corner on a downhill section of a bike-and-hike trail, the city deemed it safe to mount a chain between two trees, just about neck height. When this chain was still there in June, I called my councillor, and learned that someone had slipped on a frozen creek at the base of that hill, and successfully sued the city.
That is the disease. The liability of slip-and-fall accidents is crippling everyone. Ask teachers, service clubs, or anyone who organizes public events, and you will hear, "We can't do that anymore ... liability." Lawmakers at the highest level must be directed to change the laws, and make us responsible for our own safety.
Brian Schacht, Manotick
Public servants can make travel choices
Re: How new remote-work rules have caused commute woes for public servants, Dec. 12.
As an Ottawa Citizen subscriber, I appreciate local "human interest" stories. I appreciate them less when they state the obvious. The Citizen tagged along on a public servant's morning commute only to discover that living in a village 70 kilometres away from downtown Ottawa can result in a long drive to work.
In life, we make choices and we face the consequences. I often think it would be lovely to live in an idyllic village. Instead, I live in Vanier. I made a choice to buy my first home there because I knew that even in the worst traffic, I could get to work within 20 minutes. The Citizen is welcome to tag along anytime.
Christopher Adam, Ottawa
Federal bilingualism policy is out of date
Re: Federal departments still failing on bilingualism requirements: language watchdog, Dec. 5.
So a review of 10 departments showed that the feds are still not doing enough to comply with section 91 of the Official Languages Act. This shouldn't continue to be a surprise; it's been going on for more than 50 years.
I can't imagine the gobs of money spent on French-language requirements and it continues unabated.
Has Official Languages Commissioner Raymond Théberge not heard of AI and the miracle of technology that can translate anything immediately? The world is changing and the feds should change with it and focus efforts on employing the right person for the job, not hire because of official bilingualism.
I was recently in Georgia and had an Uber driver who was Spanish-speaking and couldn't speak English very well. We were able to converse easily via an app on his iPhone that provided translation by simply talking into it. At the click of a button.
Maybe now that AI can provide those services, we should put this money to better use and more importantly hire people who can do the job and have the necessary skills for it without bilingualism being one of the prerequisites.
Marlena Floyd, Ottawa
Liberals have been ineffective on Canada Post
Re: Canada Post strike threatens donations to Ottawa charities, Dec. 10.
The Canada Post strike and the inability of the federal government to legislate employees back to work has shown Canadians how ineffective the Liberals have become. They are afraid to anger the NDP who continue to prop them up as Canadians grow more and more angry. No wonder president-elect Donald Trump jokes that Canada should become the 51st state; he knows nothing will be done to deal with his tariffs under the current government.
The statement that Canada Post is not an essential service is ridiculous when you look at the impacts on all of the rural and remote communities across Canada; on small businesses; on anyone requiring a credit card replacement or government document; and on those who still rely on receiving provincial assistance via mail.
Instead, the Liberals try to buy votes with a two-month reduction in GST at Christmas. Clearly, they have lost their way - like my Christmas gifts sent just before the strike to my niece and nephews.
I hope that Canadians realize that the Liberal government does not serve Canadians' best interests and it is time for a change.
Shelley Baran, Ottawa
Is this the end of the Christmas card?
Since moving east 37 years ago, we have been sending Christmas cards back home. The cards coming back to us became part of our annual Christmas decorations. As a result of this year's postal strike, I fear this wonderful tradition is gone forever. Perhaps it was only a matter of time as family and friends have passed away and as Christmas greetings began arriving by computer.
The cruellest irony is that we could have bought our Christmas cards HST-free this year, as the government generously directed itself to helping us celebrate the holiday season.
Stuart Swanson, Kanata
Postal strikers have been hurting the wrong people
Who managed to time this strike for the weeks leading up to Christmas?
When industrial trade unions were organized, with strikes as their main artillery, the strikes put pressure on the employer by cutting off income. Public service unions need to consider their tactics when it is not the employer who is inconvenienced, but the public.
Murray Citron, Ottawa
Canadian neighbours, it's time for the 'nuclear option'
With the impending economic doom that incoming U.S. president Donald Trump seems committed to putting us through, I think that our friends to the North should consider the nuclear option.
Cut off all oil exports to the United States.
For every day that Trump's tariffs are in effect, there should be zero oil flowing south of the border. It is embarrassing as an American to see Trump insulting our greatest allies. If he wants to start a trade war, let him deal with a real trade war.
With roughly 40 per cent of all U.S. oil imports coming from Canada, it would not take very long for an oil embargo to have a lasting effect.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau needs to have this arrow in his quiver.
Tim Johnson, Greenfield, Wis.
Teach Trump about heroine Laura Secord
After Donald Trump embarrassed himself by posting an image of himself towering over Canada (the image appears to show a mountain in Switzerland), he decided to go further with a text for "51st state Governor of Canada" Justin Trudeau.
The best way to react to a needy, obnoxious bully is to ignore him. The second-best way is to stand up and fight. Here's an alternative, the symbolism of which he won't even understand as he seems to have no concept of history, especially that of our proud nation: Send him, all tied up in red-and-white ribbon for Christmas, a gift box of Laura Secord chocolates.
Peter Haley, Ottawa
How about if we take over the U.S. instead?
Donald Trump's annexation offer may well have been a joke. Instead of sitting there looking stunned, Justin Trudeau should have made a counter-offer: Canada does a reverse takeover. At a later stage, Canadians would be invited to vote in a referendum on whether we wanted the U.S. or not.
Jack Stilborn, Kanata
What next, Premier? Official dog fights?
Re: Penned dog hunting is back in Ontario, and it's dangerous for animals and people, Sept. 3.
A thought-provoking Citizen opinion article back in September discussed the sad practice of penned dog hunting (that is, people paying to let their dogs track rabbits, foxes, coyotes, etc. trapped in an enclosure). Despite the Mike Harris government previously deciding to phase out penned dog hunting; all other provinces apparently having banned it; various animal welfare societies vehemently opposing it; and reports of possible health concerns because of it, Premier Doug Ford's government seems to be plowing ahead with plans to expand this disturbing "sport."
Why? This expansion of penned dog hunting tarnishes Ontario's image and could negatively impact Ontario tourism. Who knows what will be next if Ford wins re-election: government sanctioned fox hunting, dog fighting ...?
Judith Gorman, Renfrew County
Skyrocketing sports salaries: Let's get priorities right
Re: Juan Soto's $765M deal with the Mets is the largest in MLB history, Dec. 8.
Doesn't this strike you as ridiculous? The baseball player Juan Soto gets more for a home run than a prime minister may get for running a country. The salary of one player may exceed a city's annual budget for dealing with homeless or other social issues.
As a caring country, we could alleviate many shortcomings by redirecting massive expenditures that give us fleeting pleasure and no more. We can do better.
Michael Wiggin, Ottawa

Document 246 of 500

'Feminist' PM has a problem; 'She deserved better': Trudeau's reputation takes hit as caucus colleagues defend Freeland

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/feminist-pm-has-problem-she-deserved-better/docview/3146645351/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Feminist%27+PM+has+a+problem%3B+%27She+deserved+better%27%3A+Trudeau%27s+reputation+takes+hit+as+caucus+colleagues+defend+Freeland&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.6&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Chryst-ia Freeland fired as finance minister, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's reputation as a self-described feminist is taking a hit.
Trudeau has been largely out of public view grappling with the fallout from Freeland's bombshell decision to resign from his cabinet the same day she was set to present the Liberals'fall fiscal update on Monday, saying she was doing so after the prime minister told her last Friday that he no longer wanted her in the role.
In a scathing resignation letter posted to the social media platform X, the Toronto MP, who had been a loyal supporter of Trudeau's, said they had for weeks found themselves disagreeing over how best to manage the country's finances and brace for U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threat of tariffs, criticizing his decision to pursue "costly political gimmicks."
While Trudeau found himself at odds with Freeland's predecessor, Bill Morneau, over COVID-19 pandemic spending, Freeland's departure is being viewed by some former colleagues-turned-critics as another knock against his reputation as a feminist prime minister.
Former environment and infrastructure minister Catherine McKenna posted to the platform Bluesky that she wasn't sure "the (prime minister) gets to call himself a feminist," saying "the test isn't what you say, it's what you do."
Upon learning of their colleague's decision, Treasury Board President and Transport Minister Anita Anand told reporters it was hitting her hard, while Indigenous Services Minister Patty Hajdu wished Freeland the best over her decision, saying, "I respect her for it."
Other Liberal MPs have come to her defence after Freeland's time serving as the face of some of the government's most important files.
"She deserved better," said Liberal MP Sean Casey, who represents Charlottetown.
"Chrystia Freeland is genuinely well-liked, and I think on a personal level, we all feel badly," said Marcus Powlowski, Liberal MP for Thunder Bay-Rainy River.
"I think she, too, is somebody who's dedicated a lot of time on behalf of Canadians and doing the right things for Canadians. I think she's a very capable person. I think all of us feel badly."
Adam van Koeverden, a Liberal MP from Ontario, said he was among a number of MPs who have questions about what happened.
Two Liberal MPs who attended Monday's emergency caucus meeting confirmed that Freeland received a standing ovation from the room. Inside the House of Commons Tuesday, Conservative MPs accused Trudeau of being a "fake feminist prime minister," which is a common attack they use against him, given the departures of other women from high-profile cabinet roles.
Jody Wilson-Raybould, who in 2015 became the first Indigenous person to serve as justice minister and attorney general, resigned in 2019, after she was demoted as she and Trudeau found themselves at odds over a deferred prosecution agreement for engineering giant SNC-Lavalin, now AtkinsRéalis.
Jane Philpott, former health minister, resigned in support of Wilson-Raybould.
Both took to X after Freeland's news, with Wilson-Raybould delivering a warning that "when the general is losing his most loyal soldiers on the eve of a (tariff) war, the country desperately needs a new general."
Philpott posted a message of encouragement to her former colleague, saying "hold your head high, my friend."
Celina Caesar-Chavannes, who stepped down as a Liberal MP, also during Trudeau's first majority term, told CBC that Freeland's resignation feels like "history's repeating itself" from 2019, saying "look at the body count of women."
Women and G ender Equality Minister Marci Ien rejected any notion Freeland's exit challenges Trudeau's reputation as a feminist.
She told National Post she "absolutely" still considers him to be, saying "I'm here." The minister added she wouldn't call Trudeau's commitments as a feminist political leader into question "at all" over what happened with Freeland.
Other Liberal MPs were not willing to defend the prime minister as vocally.
Sophie Chatel, who represents a Quebec riding, when asked whether she considers Trudeau to still be a feminist prime minister, said: "I'm not answering those questions yet. I'm in reflection as well."
Mean while, Annie Koutrakis, Liberal MP for the Quebec riding of Vimy, said, "I think you'll have to ask him that."
Before informing Freeland he no longer wanted her to serve as finance minister, a role she has held since 2020, Trudeau delivered a speech to the Equal Voice Foundation last week, where he touted himself as a proud feminist and ally to women.
He told the foundation, which advocates for more women to be involved in politics, that women's progress was under attack, pointing to Trump's victory over U.S. Vice-President Kamala Harris.
The foundation released a statement after Freeland's news, saying she "is one of a growing list of powerful women who have resigned from politics in unusual circumstances, often facing scrutiny and expectations distinct from their male counterparts," saying it was a situation not unique to one political party or level of government.
Since taking office, Trudeau has assembled a cabinet with an equal number of women and men, marking a first in Canadian history. Freeland's resignation has not changed that.
National Post staylor@postmedia.com

Subject: Feminism; Women; Politics; Prime ministers; Reputations

'It's become uncomfortable for me': Peterson leaving Canada for the U.S.; Online harms act

Author: Greenberg, Courtney

Publication info: Greenberg, Courtney. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/become-uncomfortable-me-peterson-leaving-canada-u/docview/3145907368/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+become+uncomfortable+for+me%27%3A+Peterson+leaving+Canada+for+the+U.S.%3B+Online+harms+act&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Greenberg%2C+Courtney&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jordan Peterson says he's leaving Canada for the United States and that it had become "uncomfortable" for him in his Toronto neighbourhood.
Peterson made the announcement at the beginning of a nearly two-hour sit-down interview with his daughter Mikhaila Peterson, posted on her YouTube account on Dec. 6.
"The government in Canada at the federal level is incompetent beyond belief and it's become uncomfortable for me in my neighbourhood in Toronto," said Peterson.
Peterson, a psychologist and former University of Toronto professor in the department of psychology, was investigated by the College of Psychologists of Ontario after receiving complaints about his online conduct in 2022.
His appeal was rejected by an Ontario court and he was ordered to enter a remedial coaching program.
"(With) the new legislation that the Liberals are attempting to push through, Bill C63, I'd just be living in a totalitarian hell hole if that legislation passes, and it could well pass," he said.
Bill C-63, also called the Online Harms Act, "contains a variety of measures to address a range of harmful content online as well as hate speech," according to the federal government.
"It's a bill that purports to protect children from online harm, and that's the beginning and the end of the bill, and the middle is literally the creation of a new extrajudicial system that isn't bound by the rules of legal investigation or guilt, which has an unlimited range of expansion and all the powers of a court," Peterson said.
Postmedia News

Location: United States--US; Canada

What next, Premier? Official dog fights?

Author: Gorman, Judith

Publication info: Gorman, Judith. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-next-premier-official-dog-fights/docview/3144454499/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+next%2C+Premier%3F+Official+dog+fights%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=Gorman%2C+Judith&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: Penned dog hunting is back in Ontario, and it's dangerous for animals and people, Sept. 3. A thought-provoking Citizen opinion article back in September discussed the sad practice of penned dog hunting (that is, people paying to let their dogs track rabbits, foxes, coyotes, etc. trapped in an enclosure).
Despite the Mike Harris government previously deciding to phase out penned dog hunting; all other provinces apparently having banned it; animal welfare societies vehemently opposing it; and reports of possible health concerns because of it, Premier Doug Ford's government seems to be plowing ahead with plans to expand this disturbing "sport."
Why? This expansion of penned dog hunting tarnishes Ontario's image and could negatively impact Ontario tourism. Who knows what will be next if Ford wins re-election: government sanctioned fox hunting, dog fighting?

Document 249 of 500

'Cancer patients don't have time'; Better access to drugs needs to be provided, advocates, doctors warn

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cancer-patients-dont-have-time-better-access/docview/3143466836/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Cancer+patients+don%27t+have+time%27%3B+Better+access+to+drugs+needs+to+be+provided%2C+advocates%2C+doctors+warn&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Long waits for cancer drug approvals in Canada mean some patients die before they can try treatments that might have helped them, say oncologists and patient groups who are calling for better access to oncology drugs.
"Cancer patients don't have time to wait," said Barry D. Stein, one of the authors of the Call to Action recently published in the medical journal Current Oncology. He is a colorectal cancer patient and president of the Colorectal Cancer Association of Canada.
Canada has the second-longest waits among OECD countries for access to new cancer drugs - an average of one-and-a-half years to two years after they have been approved by Health Canada. That means some patients die before a new treatment becomes available.
It also means there is a growing disparity between patients who have private insurance and can get access to costly new treatments earlier and those who have to wait for them to become available through provincially funded plans or hope for compassionate access to drugs that could help them live longer or improve their quality of life.
For Ottawa oncologist Dr. Sandeep Sehdev, that growing gap between those who can quickly access new treatments and those who can't, is disturbing.
He worked in the U.S. briefly, until the disparities between people's access to health care in the U.S. system began to get to him.
The medical oncologist at The Ottawa Hospital cancer centre said he is now seeing similar disparities in Canada when it comes to access to new cancer drugs. It is a daily occurrence for Sehdev to tell a patient there is a treatment available, approved by Health Canada, that could improve their health, but it is not yet available. Those with enough money or with private insurance plans that will cover the cost can often get the drug.
"It happens every day," Sehdev said. "I don't like disparities. Canadians don't like unfairness. I can live with having or not having a medicine if I don't think there is value. But for the rich to get it and insured people to get it and others not to is frustrating."
Sehdev is among oncologists and patient advocates who recently published Call to Action, pressing for a more streamlined system that gives Canadian cancer patients quicker access to new oncology drugs in keeping with other countries in Europe and around the world.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford, meanwhile, has talked about the need to speed up access to new publicly funded drugs. The issue is expected to come up at meetings of Canada's premiers and territorial leaders later this week. Ford is the current chair of the Council of the Federation.
Against that backdrop, some oncologists in Canada and the U.S. are part of a movement that argues some cancer medicines with marginal benefits are being overused on patients with end-stage disease. Under the name Common Sense Oncology, they are pushing for treatments that meaningfully improve survival and quality of life to be prioritized.
Access to drugs is not the only burden being borne by cancer patients in Canada. This week, the Canadian Cancer Society issued a report that estimates that cancer is costing patients an average of $33,000 in out-of-pocket costs. Some forego parts of their treatment for financial reasons.
Meanwhile, there are concerns about access to cardiovascular drugs in Canada as well. A study published in the Canadian Journal of Cardiology and co-led by Dr. Morgane Laverdure and Dr. Derek So, both of the division of cardiology at the University of Ottawa Heart Institute, found that access to cardiovascular medications in Canada is not consistent among provinces and territories. The study said there is "substantial redundancy" with multiple tiers of agencies in drug approval processes across provinces and territories.
Authors of the study also found that close to one-quarter of all formulary decisions they studied (based on which treatments would be funded) were discordant with Canadian treatment guidelines and there are no protocols in place in any province to permit timely updates of formularies to account for novel evidence in cardiac drugs or to change pricing. They also found that newer medications are disproportionately less reimbursed.
"The current system leads to significant inequities, with Canadians residing in different provinces having varied access to different evidence-based medications and treatment," said So. While solutions are being explored, he said the situation may compromise the health of Canadians, especially those relying on publicly funded drug plans.
Oncologists and patient advocates also say the delays in approvals are compromising the health of cancer patients.
"We are all aligned among patient groups across the country that the time to access new drugs is far, far too long, compared to other countries. There has to be a better framework. Other countries are doing it," said Stein, who heads the Colorectal Cancer Association of Canada.
The paper published in Current Oncology found that not only does Canada lag behind other countries but public drug reimbursement timelines are continuing to increase "detrimentally impacting cancer patients."
These disparities between Canada and other countries, the authors wrote, are partly attributable to "complex, overlapping sequential multi-step regulatory and funding processes involving federal, provincial, and private authorities and different provincial and private payers."
Health Canada is the regulatory body that decides whether a drug is safe. After that, there are a series of other regulatory hurdles and negotiations before patients can get access to a drug through provincially funded plans. One of the final steps involves negotiations to agree on a price, but even after a price is agreed on, there can be lengthy delays before provinces begin paying for the drug.
Sehdev said Health Canada approval lags about six months behind many other countries, but it has joined Project Orbis, along with the U.S. FDA, Singapore, Australia, Brazil, Switzerland, the U.K., and Israel, which aims to give patients faster access to promising cancer treatments around the globe. The rest of the process can add a year or more.
Timely access to treatment is now a leading barrier in managing cancer treatment.
Sehdev would like to see Canada follow what some other countries are doing and provide access to drugs even before negotiations to agree on a price are complete.
He said he agrees with the premise of Common Sense Oncology that money should not be spent on drugs that have marginal value, and he noted that most patients want drugs that meaningfully improve their quality of life.
But the authors of the paper say their findings about delayed access to cancer drugs "confirms clinicians'observations that patients who would likely have benefited from new treatments are dying without having the opportunity to try them."
Close to 250,000 Canadians will be diagnosed with cancer this year and 88,000 Canadians will die. It is the leading cause of death in Canada.

Subject: Drugs; Health care access; Provinces; Patients; Quality of life; Canadians; Oncology; Colorectal cancer; Cancer therapies; Cardiology

Document 250 of 500

Trump's tempest in a tease post; Ministers react to new taunts about 'governor'trudeau, 'great state of Canada'

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trumps-tempest-tease-post-ministers-react-new/docview/3143064212/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%27s+tempest+in+a+tease+post%3B+Ministers+react+to+new+taunts+about+%27governor%27trudeau%2C+%27great+state+of+Canada%27&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal cabinet ministers spent Tuesday morning downplaying the latest dig by U.S. president-elect Donald Trump, who called Prime Minister Justin Trudeau a "Governor" of "the Great State of Canada."
Trump did so in a post overnight on his Truth Social account, saying it was a pleasure to have dined "with Governor Justin Trudeau of the Great State of Canada" and that he looked forward to seeing "the Governor again soon" to continue their "in depth talks on Tariffs and Trade."
"The results of which will be truly spectacular for all," he wrote.
Trump's comments come after sources told Fox News that during his recent dinner with Trudeau at his Mara-Lago estate, the president-elect talked about Canada becoming the 51st U.S. state - a comment that Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc, who also attended the dinner, later said was nothing more than a joke.
Trump later posted a seemingly AI-generated image of himself standing on a mountain ridge with a Canadian flag planted in it, with the caption "Oh Canada!" As they filed into their weekly cabinet meeting Tuesday, ministers chalked Trump's latest words up to being more of the same.
Health Minister Mark Holland told reporters that while he was unaware of the "context" of Trump's comments, he believes it was done in the spirit of "levity" during challenging global times.
"We have to all not react to every word because we don't know the context, we can't react to everything, we need to focus on being mature as a country."
"It sounds like we're living in an episode of South Park," quipped Immigration Minister Marc Miller.
He added: "I don't think we should necessarily look on Truth Social for public policy," and said "all options" were on the table when it comes to responding to Trump's threat of tariffs.
Avoiding the 25 per cent tariffs the president-elect has promised to slap on Canadian imports over his concerns about illegal border crossings and the flow of fentanyl into the U.S., has been top of mind for Trudeau, as his government prepares to deliver its fall economic statement on Monday, which is expected to contain millions more in new spending to increase border security.
Finance Minster Chrystia Freeland wouldn't directly address reporters'questions on Tuesday about whether she will blow past a projected deficit of $40.1 billion when she unveils the government's updated spending plan.
LeBlanc has said that when it comes to border security, the government is prepared to provide the RCMP - which is responsible for policing between official ports of entry - with additional equipment, ranging from helicopters to drones.
Federal New Democrats have also called for at least 1,000 officers to be hired for Canada Border Services Agency.
RCMP Commissioner Mike Duheme told reporters last week the national force submitted its request for how many members it believes are needed to operate these new technologies and enhance its visibility along the Canada-U.S. border and that any final announcements would be coming from the federal government.
Trudeau met last week with federal opposition leaders to discuss his dinner with Trump and cautioned them against making public comments that could play into the president-elect's criticisms of Canada. He later took specific aim at Opposition Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's comments in the House of Commons that Trudeau had "lost control of the borders."
Speaking to the Halifax Chamber of Commerce on Monday, Trudeau raised the spectre that Canada would be prepared to impose retaliatory tariffs as it did when Trump slapped his own on Canadian steel and aluminum during the renegotiation of the North American Free Trade Agreement in 2018.
Canada will "respond to unfair tariffs in a number of ways, and we're still looking at the right ways to respond, but our responses to the unfair steel and aluminum tariffs were what ended up lifting those tariffs last time," Trudeau said.
International Trade Minister Mary Ng told reporters on Tuesday Canada has to take Trump's threat seriously and tariffs are concerning for the country's economy.
"We're doing that work right now, and we will have more to say in due course," she said, when asked about what action it would be prepared to take.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford and other premiers are preparing to discuss Canada's plan to avoid descending into a trade war with the U.S., when they meet on Wednesday, which follows a recent call they had with Trudeau.
National Post staylor@postmedia.com

Subject: Aluminum; Presidents; Governors; Budgets; Border security; Tariffs

Cancer patients are dying awaiting new treatments, advocates and doctors warn

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cancer-patients-are-dying-awaiting-new-treatments/docview/3143064112/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 252 of 500

Councillor nominated as PC candidate in Carleton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/councillor-nominated-as-pc-candidate-carleton/docview/3143063134/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Councillor+nominated+as+PC+candidate+in+Carleton&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Osgoode ward Coun. George Darouze has been nominated as the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario's candidate for Carleton in the next provincial election.
"I am extremely honoured to announce that I am officially the PC Party of Ontario candidate for the riding of Carleton, with a landslide victory winning 96% of the vote," Darouze wrote Dec. 8 on Facebook.
Premier Doug Ford commented on the nomination in a Dec. 8 release from the party.
"George Darouze is a champion for the people of Carleton and a seasoned leader," Ford said. "George's track record of advocating for better infrastructure, supporting local businesses, and delivering on community priorities makes him the ideal candidate to represent Carleton. I'm excited to have him on our team as we continue to build a stronger Ontario."
Darouze has represented Osgoode for three terms, since 2014, hanging on to the rural ward in the 2022 election by a mere 238 votes.
In the PC release, the party said Darouze "brings years of proven leadership and a strong commitment to his community. As a current Ottawa City Councillor, he has consistently worked to enhance rural and suburban life, improve infrastructure, and deliver results for Carleton residents. From his work in community safety to advocating for better public services, George's dedication to his constituents is unwavering."
The announcement came amid speculation that Ford could call an early spring election.
Goldie Ghamari represents the Carleton riding, but Ford kicked her out of the Progressive Conservative caucus in late June after what he called repeated and serious lapses in judgment.
Shortly before that, Ghamari had taken a virtual meeting with Tommy Robinson, the former leader of the far-right English Defence League. After Ghamari posted on social media about the meeting, the National Council of Canadian Muslims called Robinson an Islamophobe and urged Ford to remove Ghamari from his caucus.
Ghamari still holds a seat in the legislature as an independent.

Document 254 of 500

Ford leads the way on transit; Opinion; Ontario premier working to get Toronto moving

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-leads-way-on-transit-opinion-ontario-premier/docview/3141983037/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+leads+the+way+on+transit%3B+Opinion%3B+Ontario+premier+working+to+get+Toronto+moving&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has had a successful run since being elected in 2018, but perhaps more than anything else, he deserves credit for expanding the subway system in Canada's largest city.
The Ontario Line will introduce nearly 16 kilometres of track to the fastest-growing city in North America, but it wasn't easy. Initially conceived as the Downtown Relief Line, it has been mired in politics and bureaucracy for decades - until Doug Ford came along and made it happen.
Without Ford's initiative, the Ontario Line would still be aspirational rather than under construction, although that seems lost on Toronto's chattering liberal class, for whom swallowing cyanide might be preferable to bestowing even a soupçon of credit upon a Conservative.
The Ontario Line is no doubt a legacy project for a premier who will be remembered for building the most significant infrastructure project the city has seen in decades.
Ford is presently being chided from all angles for his stance on bicycle lanes in Toronto, even though it's one of the most congested cities in the world. It doesn't take an urban planner to figure out that augmenting mass transit, not expanding underused bike lanes (which are used by a mere 1.2 per cent of the residents, according to census data), is the solution.
Nearly two decades after the Ontario Liberals introduced the Places to Grow Act, in part to curtail congestion, the tribulations of navigating Toronto's factory farm-like conditions have exposed kinks in the legislation's armour.
Bloor Street is down to a single lane in either direction along the hyper-dense expanse west of Spadina Avenue. Further to the north, Eglinton Avenue has been saddled with a bike lane that virtually no one uses but has somehow managed to make traffic around the notoriously congested Allen Road intersection even worse than before.
Ford's initiatives have nevertheless done precious little to stem the barrage of criticism he receives on a quotidian basis. And the Toronto Star, which dropped the mitts against the Ford family long ago, is unsurprisingly front and centre.
It recently ran a column accusing Ford of trying to hoodwink voters with trivialities like permitting grocers and convenience stores to sell alcohol. It's a shrill rebuke that reeks of the same patronizing paternalism that's mugged the province's hard-working taxpayers of the same consumer liberty that neighbouring Quebec has enjoyed from what seems like time immemorial.
The Star's petty, low-rent reprimand ignores the fact that Ford is merely adhering to arguably the most salient principle of free-market capitalism: competition between vendors results in lower consumer prices.
The Star must have forgotten that its columnists have also advocated for the exact thing Ford codified into law, having noted in the past that the Beer Store is a foreign-owned "cartel-cum-monopoly" and that its stores feel like "Stalinist relics ... designed by Communist apparatchiks for the beer-swilling lumpenproletariat."
The Star might need a primer on its own founding principles and mission statement, which says that it believes in "individual and civil liberties," and works to "focus public attention on injustices of all kinds." Those "injustices" apparently don't include corporations colluding to price-gouge increasingly immiserated consumers.
The reality is that such free market policies augment razor-thin profit margins for entrepreneurs, many of whom pour their life savings into ventures like neighbourhood convenience stores, and in case the chattering liberal class in Toronto hasn't noticed, everything is more expensive.
That includes small businesses'overheads, but alcohol sales will help - and considering that you'd be hard-pressed to find a pint anywhere in Toronto for under $9, Ford's push to increase competition and choice is great news for consumers and businesses alike.
The portion of Highway 401 running through Toronto is North America's busiest and the bane of the city's drivers. Weekday or weekend, day or night, drivers can expect a slow commute.
Ford has proposed building a tunnel below the highway to augment traffic flow. It's an ambitious project that comes with considerable risk - just look at Boston's "Big Dig," which suffered years of delays and billions in cost overruns. Yet Ford's army of media critics have only admonished his idea instead of offering their own solutions to the city's traffic woes.
Ford is a lightning rod for Canadian media, most of which has a liberal bias that perhaps explains why they're more interested in tearing him down than proffering policies of their own. They offer criticism without any real solutions of their own, which reeks of partisanship and does little to further public discourse.
Urban planners and leftwing commentators are fond of gaslighting Canadians by pointing to bike-lane usage in cities such as Beijing, Paris and Amsterdam, where temperatures rarely dip below freezing, and using the "If you build it, they will come" argument.
But the fact remains that Canada does not have the bike culture of a country like China, and no matter how bad traffic gets, the prospect of riding a bicycle through the snow when it's -20 C outside will remain unappealing to most Canadians.
Unfortunately, Ford's critics seem completely devoid of solutions of their own, repeating tired clichés about how bike lanes are the answer to all our problems and building new roads will only invite more traffic.
Toronto fancies itself Canada's answer to Manhattan - and in a way it is, if exorbitant rent and gridlock factor into the equation - but if that dream ever becomes reality, Ford's push to modernize transit and ease congestion will have played an outsized role.

Document 255 of 500

Trump's fentanyl ultimatum; Canada's 'super labs'under the microscope amid tariff threat

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trumps-fentanyl-ultimatum-canadas-super-labsunder/docview/3140676909/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%27s+fentanyl+ultimatum%3B+Canada%27s+%27super+labs%27under+the+microscope+amid+tariff+threat&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The growth of illegal Canadian fentanyl production came into focus over the weekend after U.S. president-elect Donald Trump reportedly gave Canadian government leaders a clear impression that the runaway drug problem is his top priority, even in Canada-U.S. relations.
Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc, who, along with the prime minister, attended Friday's dinner at Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate in Florida, told CBC that it was a topic of discussion. "We talked about the challenge that fentanyl represents in Canada," he said. Reporting elsewhere about the dinner discussion said Trump made it clear that the fentanyl epidemic is his top priority before he takes office on Jan. 20, including drugs being smuggled from Canada.
Trump's focus on fentanyl smuggling comes at a time when socalled super labs scattered across Canada are turbocharging production of the deadly synthetic opioid. A recent RCMP raid of an Interior British Columbia facility, for example, recovered the equivalent of 95 million doses of fentanyl.
Similarly large-scale operations have recently been uncovered in Alberta and Ontario. One such facility, busted by police last August, was located just 50 kilometres from the Canada-U.S. border crossing at Niagara Falls.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said last week that she and her fellow premiers have urged Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to act quickly on the fentanyl problem.
Trump threatened last week to slap a 25-per-cent tariff on all Canadian and Mexican goods if the two respective governments didn't take measures to reduce the flow of illegal drugs and migrants into the U.S.
"This Tariff will remain in effect until such time as Drugs, in particular Fentanyl, and all Illegal Aliens stop this Invasion of our Country!" Trump said in a social media post announcing the action.
Canada arguably faces as big a challenge in cracking down on the illegal fentanyl factories. These highly sophisticated facilities are hard to police as they largely source ingredients that are not themselves illegal.
A briefing note prepared by Global Affairs Canada earlier this year said that this spike in production has likely made Canada a net exporter of fentanyl.
"Seizures of Canadasourced fentanyl in places like the U.S. and Australia suggest that domestic production is likely exceeding domestic demand, and that Canada is now a source (and transit) country for fentanyl to some markets," read the note. U.S. Customs agents seized some 19.5 kilograms of fentanyl at northern border crossings between October 2023 and September 2024, up from just over a kilo one year earlier and 6.4 kilos in the 2021-22 fiscal year.
While still just a small fraction of the total amount of fentanyl entering the U.S., this nevertheless exceeds the volume of product intercepted by Canadian agents on the other side of the border.
The Canadian Border Service Agency seized 946 grams of fentanyl in the 2023 fiscal year and 4.4 kilos in the first two quarters of the 2024 fiscal year, according to its website.
Smith recently said her Alberta government is considering setting up a special "fentanyl apprehension team" within the provincially controlled Alberta Sheriffs Branch.
"Let's stop the fentanyl first," Smith said in a Tuesday interview.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford, currently the chair of the Council of the Federation, echoed this sentiment in a news release urging the federal government to take a "more proactive approach" in curbing fentanyl smuggling.
"As an example, Health Canada needs to be able to share where fentanyl and other drugs seized by police forces are manufactured and synthesized for enhanced tracking and tracing, like they do in the U.S.," Ford said.
National Post rmohamed@postmedia.com

Subject: Drugs; Smuggling; Prime ministers; Fentanyl; Tariffs

Document 256 of 500

How Ottawa-area businesses may be hurt by Trump's tariffs

Author: Warren, Ken

Publication info: Warren, Ken. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-ottawa-area-businesses-may-be-hurt-trumps/docview/3138976717/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Ottawa-area+businesses+may+be+hurt+by+Trump%27s+tariffs&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-12-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Warren%2C+Ken&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The vow by U.S. president-elect Donald Trump to smack a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian imports into the United States represents "an existential threat to Canada's economic security," according to David Ross, CEO of Ottawa's Ross Video.
"I don't think Canadians really understand what's happening. We're under attack by our closest neighbour. It's like a pandemic without the pandemic," Ross said.
He is hardly a lone voice in the economic wilderness. Just like business leaders and politicians across Canada and Mexico, which Trump has also included in his across-the-board tariff promise, Ross's primary goal is protecting jobs.
Countless economic experts suggest the tariff plan makes no sense because it would ultimately significantly raise prices in the U.S., and believe Trump could be dangling the tariff scare as a way to negotiate major concessions from Canada and Mexico in other areas. The incoming U.S. president has repeatedly said that a flood of illegal immigration and illicit drugs across his country's northern and southern borders must be stopped. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc paid an emergency visit to Trump at his Mar-a-Lago estate in Florida on Nov. 29, where tariffs were a key point of discussion. Afterwards, on Truth Social, Trump said the meeting was "very productive."
WHAT 'S THE POTENTIAL IMPACTON THE COMPANY? Just the same, Canadian companies must try to prepare for economic chaos.
Ross Video, a live production technology company, employs 500 in Ottawa and another 350 in Iroquois, 70 minutes southeast of the national capital.
All told, the booming business has 1,400 employees worldwide, including 20 research and development outposts in the U.S.
If you've watched TV or attended a major-league sports event, chances are Ross equipment has been involved since it's used by professional franchises and in state-of-the-art stadiums throughout North America.
The cable-suspended camera systems that provide eyes from the sky into what's happening on the field or on the ice are Ross products. Many of the world's business giants, including Google and Amazon, use the materials produced in Ottawa and its backyard. So, too, do major U.S. broadcast networks, where Trump, ironically, has used Canadian-made products to help issue his protectionist messages.
"That's $200 million (in revenues) that's in jeopardy," said Ross, who was in emergency meetings with the company's senior officials on Nov. 27, two days after Trump's trade war tirade landed with a thud north and south of the Canada-U.S. border.
Big picture: Ross would like the company to grow to 2,000 employees and to earn $1 billion in annual revenues by 2030, but the tariff threat is looming like a black cloud in advance of Trump's official return to the White House. Inauguration day is Jan. 20.
"There are a few companies in the U.S. that do similar work, but not many," Ross said. "Most are in Europe and Japan that are not subject to the 25 per cent tariff. We're at a 25 per cent disadvantage."
In terms of standard trade operating practices between Canada and the U.S., Ross remembers a business conversation with his father, John Ross, who founded the company out of his Montreal basement in 1974.
"He was talking before NAFTA (the 1994 trade agreement between Canada, the U.S. and Mexico) and he said, 'Always add two per cent costs for over the border.'" A major tariff would also affect smaller businesses with U.S. customers.
That includes Newman Tools, a Stittsville-based industrial and specialty tools distributor founded in 1955.
"With more than 50 per cent of our business in the U.S., it would significantly impact us," chief operating officer Ivy Terry said.
COULD COMPANIES MOVE OPERATIONS TO THE U.S.? That's not a legitimate possibility for Ross.
Again, protecting jobs here is a priority, and logistically it makes no sense.
"We're only two months away," Ross said. "If (Trump) says there would be an executive order as president on Day 1, there's no time to prepare. Building manufacturing operations takes years. You can't just do it in a month or two. We're talking about thousands and thousands of different assemblies."
The challenge for a smaller-scale operation like Newman Tools would be alerting U.S. clients about the sticker shock of having 25 per cent tacked on to the original purchase price once a product crosses the border.
"It's going to be a conversation I might have to have on every phone call," Terry said. "It might result in people trying to find the product locally."
WHAT ARE BUSINESS ORGANIZATIONS SAYING? Seventy-five per cent of Canadian exports are to the U.S. According to the U.S. Census Bureau, Canada exported $435 billion of goods to our southern neighbour between January and September this year.
Naturally, reaction to Trump's comments from the Ontario Chamber of Commerce was swift and strong.
"Addressing this threat is not just an economic challenge, it must be a national priority," OCC president and CEO Daniel Tisch said.
The full-court press is on from business leaders demanding that Canadian political leaders fight back against the lofty tariffs being threatened.
"With $3.6 billion in goods and services crossing the border daily - $1 billion to and from Ontario alone - the tariff would have devastating consequences for both countries," Tisch said.
He added that Ontario was a "critical hub" for manufacturing, agriculture, technology and innovation, pointing out that U.S. consumers relied on products manufactured in the province.
Products and technology like those produced by Ross Video.
Businesses and jobs on both sides of the border are at stake.
"It would disrupt supply chains, drive up manufacturing costs, reduce exports, erode investor confidence and fuel job losses," Tisch said.
Canadian Chamber of Commerce and University of Calgary economics professor Trevor Tombe produced a study saying that a mandatory 25 per cent tariff would cause a recession, reducing the Canadian economy by 2.6 per cent, or approximately $2,000 per person.
It works both ways, though. Americans would immediately take a hit at the gas pump because 20 per cent of all oil used in that country comes from Canada. The tariffs would be tacked on the price at the gas pump. Patrick DeHaan, who heads petroleum analysis at GasBuddy, told CBS News that prices could rise 30-70 cents per gallon within two days of a 25 per cent tariff being imposed.
A Wall Street Journal report also suggested that the price of a new car would jump by an average of $3,000. The U.S. imports four million cars annually from Mexico and Canada.
Eventually, U.S. grocery prices could also rise.
"Prices will creep up after the supply chain dries up and (stores) have to sell new stuff for 25 per cent more," Ross said. "I don't think Americans understand tariffs."
WHAT ABOUT EXCEPTIONS? There is some belief that Trump could relax the 25 per cent tariff on particular industries, including oil, as a negotiating ploy while asking Canada to improve border security.
That, of course, would do nothing to help Ross Video or Newman Tools.
"Maybe they would say they would slap a 25 per cent tariff on everything in Canada, except oil," Ross said. "My view is that Canada should be saying there should be a 100 export tariff on everything from the U.S."
WHAT ARE GOVERNMENTS DOING TO FIGHT BACK? Trudeau and LeBlanc emerged from the Mar-a-Lago meeting smiling, believing that some progress had been made from the faceto-face session.
Trudeau and provincial premiers had held an emergency meeting Nov. 27. Despite their political differences on many topics, the aim was to reach consensus on a "Team Canada" approach: speaking as one unified voice against Trump's tariff pledge.
"I don't want to minimize for a moment the gravity of the challenge we now face," Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland said. "Now is not the moment to squabble amongst ourselves."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford is among those loudly pushing for increased border security to slow down illegal border crossings and the movement of guns and drugs into the U.S. That's a message that would appeal to Trump and his base.
For what it's worth, outgoing U.S.
President Joe Biden is not a fan of the tariff plan.
"It's a counterproductive thing to do," Biden said on Nov. 28. "We're surrounded by the Pacific Ocean, Atlantic Ocean and two allies: Mexico and Canada. The last thing we need to do is screw up those relationships." kwarren@postmedia.com X: Citizenkwarren

Subject: International trade; Presidents; Prices; Manufacturing; Prime ministers; Pandemics; Border security; Tariffs

Document 257 of 500

Today's letters: Get the campers out of Ottawa parks

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-get-campers-out-ottawa-parks/docview/3135036993/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Get+the+campers+out+of+Ottawa+parks&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Lowertown residents have a right to their parks
Over the past few years, there has been a steady flow of campers setting up tents in our Lowertown parks. At times, it can take weeks to have the City of Ottawa relocate these campers despite the fact that there are long established bylaws against camping in City of Ottawa parks, stringing up clothes lines to trees and the use of drugs and alcohol. Lowertown taxpayers have a right to use their parks for the purpose for which they were established.
The Macdonald Gardens Park is a case in point. The park, once a crown jewel of Ottawa with a significant history, was designated as a heritage park in 2017. This Lowertown neighbourhood park is an important green space for local residents. We enjoy seeing children play soccer, families having a picnic and individuals sunning themselves or reading books on benches.
A volunteer community group, the "Friends of Macdonald Gardens Park," has organized biannual cleanup of the park for more than two decades. Several members have also contributed financially, or through their labour, to plant flowers and shrubs. However, we now find ourselves picking up garbage left by campers, feeling threatened by those who have mental health challenges, and finding partially unused packages of Narcan, used needles, broken crack pipes, beer cans or wine bottles.
We understand that for various reasons many people do not have adequate housing and the city has to find a way to address the housing crisis. However, allowing them then to move to city-owned park land is not a solution. The city should establish more beds in shelters, and more supportive housing and recovery services which are spread out to other parts of the city. Maybe it should consider underutilized city land for these purposes.
Our city councillor, Stéphanie Plante, supports efforts to "share the load" but is only one voice and one vote on city council. All three levels of government have made a mess of the housing situation and their fiscal mismanagement has weakened their ability to respond to the crisis. Problems with the justice system, drug and alcohol treatment services, as well as availability of mental health treatment, have contributed to an increase in campers.
We call on our elected representatives from all three levels of government to get together and pool their resources in a coordinated plan to address this problem. We want, and have a right to, our parks.
Shawn Marmer, Ottawa
Ottawa's 311 system gets the job done
Re: Need to report a problem to the City of Ottawa? Good luck with that, Nov. 28.
The city's 311 reporting system worked efficiently for me on two occasions.
The top of a city street sign was lying on the grass near my home. I notified 311 and it was replaced within a day, along with a repainting of the rusted post.
At another time, I notified 311 that the city-owned mature tree in front of my home appeared to be in failing condition. Within a day, the city painted a large red X on it and within a few days it was cut down, leaving a very short stump. I was informed that the stump will remain for about one year to allow some drying time.
After about one year, the city removed the stump and resurfaced the area with fresh top soil and grass seed.
Yes, 311 worked for me.
Irving Slone, Ottawa
City's funding gap must be fixed, and soon
Charles Akben-Marchand makes the crucial point that the citizens of Ottawa are living through an ongoing crisis from decades of austerity at City Hall.
The mayor, in his 2025 budget, planned to hike the price of OC Tranaspo for seniors by 120 per cent. Council, responding to reaction from citizens to this callous proposal, is proposing to reduce the increase just a bit.
I recognize that the current council is stuck with the fundamental errors made by an earlier mayor, councils and administrations. But its current approach is fiddling on the margins. What is needed is a strategic approach that addresses the problems head-on.
There is a chronic funding gap. It should be dealt with now, not later. It just gets bigger and will eventually fall on citizens who did not create it. The mayors of other large Ontario municipalities, in their budgets, recognize a chronic problem and are budgetting for significantly larger increases than Ottawa is. Citizens should realize that chronically inadequate funding affects them directly in many ways - for example, through roads with potholes that destroy tires and damage suspensions. We ratepayers need to recognize that the city must balance its financial books, and agree that sooner is better than later.
John Hollins, Gloucester
Make a plan for saving public art from the library
Re: Deachman, Ottawa Public Library art must not be left orphaned, Nov. 25.
One would think our city's custodians of literary works would attribute some value to their cousin, public art. Apparently not. The library doesn't seem to care, and a spokesman for the building's owner, a branch of TD Asset Management, offers only a vague and conciliatory "hope."
Built as a library, this building is unlikely to survive. "Redevelopment" is unlikely to include a place for the large works described by Bruce Deachman. Landfill is the more likely destination.
A plan for these works should be prepared and acted on now, not delayed for the issue to be forgotten.
John Edmond, Ottawa
Seniors help support flagging OC Transpo
Re: Seniors, students line up to give transit commission an earful about fare increases, Nov. 25.
A note to Ottawa Council: My experience of riding the bus as a senior consisted mainly of joining a few other old folks, and lost souls, on a near-empty vehicle. The bus was going to be running in those off-peak hours whether we took it or not. So anything we paid was more than OC Transpo had before we boarded. Be carefully what you ask for.
Rod Packwood, Ottawa
It's time to test and license cyclists
Re: Letter, Pedestrians, make yourselves visible at night, Nov. 25.
Eleanor Abra is, sadly, one of our rare drivers. Her thoughtful letter on night-time visibility once again illustrates our naïve approach to cycling safety.
Of my five cycling mishaps, only one involved another person in a vehicle. Two sent me to hospital with a broken leg, one with a broken head. I walked away from the others.
But the simple fact is that since age nine, all of my cycling accidents were 100 per cent my fault. With the advent of much better bikes, some electrified, it is well past the time for education, testing and graduated licensing of all cyclists. Motorists have more and more distractions every day, making the problem more urgent.
Further, there should be mandatory insurance, with the insurers responsible for testing and insuring the cyclists.
This is such an easy thing to get right. Or we can continue to pedal down this road of stupidity on cycling safety.
Thomas Brawn, Orléans
Renewable energy will help fuel Centre Block
Re: Centre Block 2.0: A peek inside the massive renovation of a national landmark, Nov. 15.
Your feature on the $5-billion renovation of Parliament Hill failed to mention the massive renewable energy facility being installed, though you had two photos of the excavation. If you ever do a follow-up, the Senate can provide details
Bill Eggertson, Ottawa
Work-at-homers are taking advantage
Re: Downtown Ottawa office occupancy still low despite hiked presence of public servants, Nov. 25.
I had been anticipating the return of federal employees to their workplaces until the Treasury Board reported only 70-per-cent compliance with the new rule to be in the office at least three days a week. Enough is enough.
When the pandemic struck, work-from-home was the solution to keep the economy going. Businesses in the core hung on desperately, hoping the end of the pandemic would herald the return of business, but apparently not.
The Treasury Board report makes it appear that our entitled federal workers are not taking the three-day return-to-work policy seriously and some action will be needed to make them comply. It's not like they don't have some of the best jobs in this town. I hope the government will play hardball to ensure compliance. These workers get above-average remuneration and have fantastic benefit plans.
Let's pretend our work from-home-worker has Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday as their in-office days but they are feeling unwell on Wednesday. Would it be possible for them to appear on Thursday or Friday to meet their three-day commitment? They may be unhappy for a bit but they have a great gig.
What these workers need to realize is that our transit system was built to help them get to work. I and all other Ottawa taxpayers have already paid for the bus system. But revenues are way down now and I and other taxpayers have to cover the shortfall.
I wish there were a way to recover the fares public servants don't pay on their work-from-home days to cover their fair share of OC Transpo costs. It isn't fair that they win and everyone else loses.
Dennis Ward, Ottawa
GST holiday is a truly dumb idea
Re: GST holiday passed in House of Commons despite Conservatives, Bloc voting against, Nov. 28.
This has got to be the dumbest idea Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has ever come up with. A month before Christmas, he announces he's going to eliminate the GST on a few items as of Dec. 14, but only do so for a couple of months.
The brunt of this will fall on retailers' shoulders, not just once when they cut the GST, but again, in two months, when it's reinstated. So, who will pay for that? Yes, we consumers. Sure, when the GST is cut, it will likely result in increased sales. But, since none of this happens until Dec. 14, and since it's only for two months, it's unlikely to result in much in the way of savings for consumers, and will likely actually increase costs to them.
And, there are some items that shouldn't be taxed at all in the first place. Such as diapers. And what about incontinence pads for men and women? Why are these taxed?
And Christmas trees? Give me a break.
The only thing that needs to be axed is the Trudeau government. Let's lobby/vote to bring on a federal election. ASAP.
Ken Bowering, Woodlawn
Trudeau drops taxes on the wrong items
During the Christmas season, people tend to eat too much and drink too much. Obesity and diabetes are growing concerns and drinking and driving increases.
So what is the Liberals' plan to combat these two problems? In their infinite wisdom, they decided to drop the GST on carbonated drinks, candies, sugar-laden snacks etc. and to also drop the tax on most beers, wines and alcoholic coolers.
Let's make "junk" food and alcoholic beverages cheaper to buy for the next couple of months; what could possibly go wrong? Brilliant move, Prime Minister Trudeau.
Les Shinder, Nepean
Why should we fear Trump's second term?
Re: Letter, Condolences as you enter the Trump era, America, Nov. 22.
The letter-writer offers his condolences to Americans on having to live through Donald Trump's second term. What exactly does he fear with Trump 2.0? Another four years of low inflation like under the first Trump term? Or four years of steady and consistent growth?
I grant you that the Joe Biden years were more entertaining but Trump, although he was not my president, delivered economic results that allowed me to retire; Biden almost caused said retirement to be cancelled.
The second Trump term is going to be different: the wise counsel of Mike Pence has been replaced by the more aggressive growth philosophy of Elon Musk. But Musk's track record should give us all comfort.
Ken Johnston, Ottawa
Trump is not Canada's friend
Re: Trump demands Canada, Mexico stop U.S. 'invasion' or 'pay a very big price', Nov. 26.
A 25-per-cent tariff on Canadian exports to the United States. Why? Our leaky borders let in 20 kilograms of fentanyl. But how about the thousands of guns that find their way from the U.S. into criminal hands in Canada? We have good reason to tighten up our borders.
Let's not give in to the bully. And let's not support Donald Trump's actions against Mexico like Premiers Doug Ford and Danielle Smith in order to get better treatment for Canada. We have many friends in the world, and Trump is not one of them. Canadians aren't wimps.
Michael Wiggin, Ottawa
Lighthouse dancehall brought us together
Re: This lighthouse dancehall once rocked the Rideau, Nov. 25.
In May, 1952 I met my husband when I was 17 at the Lighthouse. He came from Dunrobin, so it was a special dance for him. I lived in North Gower. For five summers we often danced at the Lighthouse, until we were married in 1957. He passed away in December, 2022.
I have very fond memories of the Lighthouse.
Ruth Kennedy, Dunrobin
