Document 258 of 500

PM holds firm on rebate plan; $250 Cheque for workers aims to help people left out of feds'programs: trudeau

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-holds-firm-on-rebate-plan-250-cheque-workers/docview/3134418298/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+holds+firm+on+rebate+plan%3B+%24250+Cheque+for+workers+aims+to+help+people+left+out+of+feds%27programs%3A+trudeau&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau defended his government's decision to dole out $250 cheques to working Canadians, arguing that many of them have been left out of targeted programs meant to help seniors and young families in recent years.
Speaking in Prince Edward Island on Friday, Trudeau said he had been hearing from Canadians across the country who do not qualify for any of the federal government's programs, whether it is a boost in seniors'pensions, child benefits or childcare spaces.
"I regularly meet a 26-yearold grocery worker, or a 32-yearold school teacher with no kids, or someone in their 40s who's working in a factory, or a 50-yearold plumber whose kids have all grown, who says, 'OK, but we're having troubles too,'" he said.
Trudeau said those Canadians are feeling "squeezed" even though they are working hard and feel as if the federal government is not "showing up" for them.
"Seeing hard-working Canadians who get up, go to work every day and make our economy and our communities successful, and recognizing them specifically ... and showing that we see them, that's what this is all about," he added.
Trudeau announced last week that the government would be pausing the GST/HST on select items - including beer, takeout food, children's toys and Christmas trees - in time for the holidays, but would also send a new rebate for working Canadians in spring next year.
Those rebates are expected to be sent to 18.7 million Canadians who worked last year and made $150,000 or less. They will cost $4.68 billion.
In the past week, federal Liberals faced fierce pressure from the NDP, the Bloc Québécois and even some in their own caucus for the $250 cheques to include retirees, people with disabilities and other vulnerable groups who did not earn an income in 2023.
As a result, the federal government opted to table a bill to solely pass the GST/HST holiday, leaving any legislation and debate on the $250 cheques for another day.
A Liberal source said the reason for splitting the measures in two was to make sure the tax break would be in effect by Dec. 14.
In comparison, the government has a lot more time to make sure the cheques go out to Canadians as they are expected next spring.
Trudeau would not say when his government would be tabling legislation for the rebates, but expects they will be rolling out in April 2025.
He also said his government will look at "different ways" of helping seniors and "all sorts of different people."
Trudeau also blasted opposition parties for not supporting his government's current proposal on the cheques, arguing that they are against working people.
"To see the NDP, which was supposedly the party of workers, turn its back on hard-working Canadians and say, 'no, we shouldn't be delivering this cheque unless we expand it massively,'is not recognizing working Canadians for the challenges they're facing."
He accused Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre of "pretending to care about workers and care about affordability." Poilievre has criticized the Liberals'latest ideas as "irresponsible and inflationist" and said the carbon tax increase on April 1 will raise the cost of living. P.E.I. Premier Dennis King held a news conference alongside Trudeau to announce his province is the latest to agree to join the federal government's National School Food Program after Newfoundland and Labrador, Manitoba and Ontario.
King said he and Trudeau talked about the approaching tax holiday. He said P.E.I. would "willingly participate" in removing the harmonized sales tax for products selected by the federal Liberals during the holidays and until Feb. 15, 2025.
"I did express to the prime minister that I wish we would have had a little more lead time and consultation through that process," he said. "I would have thrown a few more things on the table, maybe, that they exempted."
King said the Liberals'sudden announcement altered his province's finances but said he feels "confident" the province can deal with it.
"Anything we can do to help reduce the cost of living for Islanders at a time when it's been difficult for so many, we're happy to do," he said.
National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Subject: Federal government; Workers; Rebates; Canadians; Cost of living; Holidays & special occasions; Prime ministers; Tax increases

Trump not reliable as a friend of Canada

Author: Wiggin, Michael

Publication info: Wiggin, Michael. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-not-reliable-as-friend-canada/docview/3134418046/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+not+reliable+as+a+friend+of+Canada&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=Wiggin%2C+Michael&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 260 of 500

GST holiday worries small retailers; Many businesses are concerned it will be too complicated and costly, poll reveals

Author: Rankin, Andrew

Publication info: Rankin, Andrew. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gst-holiday-worries-small-retailers-many/docview/3134417648/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GST+holiday+worries+small+retailers%3B+Many+businesses+are+concerned+it+will+be+too+complicated+and+costly%2C+poll+reveals&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.8&amp;au=Rankin%2C+Andrew&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Thousands of small businesses have big concerns and bigger questions about the upcoming two-month GST/HST holiday, according to a recent poll by Canadian Federation of Independent Business.
Last week, the Trudeau government promised to make dozens of items tax free between Dec. 14 and Feb. 15, ranging from children's clothing, toys and video games to Christmas trees and restaurant meals and more. The tax holiday bill is one step closer to becoming law after it was passed by a majority of the House of Commons late Thursday night.
The Conservatives and Bloc Québécois voted against the legislation.
The plan has been welcomed by some, but others see it as a desperate attempt by the struggling Liberals to win votes. The CFIB polled its members on the upcoming tax holiday and heard back from 3,500 of them.
Three quarters of respondents said it would be costly and complicated to implement - small firms reported a median of $1,000 in additional costs to reprogram their point-of-sale systems to remove and then reinstate the tax.
Sixty-five per cent said there is not enough time to implement the change.
Seventy-one per cent said big businesses and online giants will benefit most.
As well, more than two-thirds said it will be difficult to determine which items are temporarily tax-exempt. Most provinces don't require labelling telling consumers which items are taxed.
Sixty-six per cent said they believe consumers will delay purchases and 54 per cent believe consumers will return products to repurchase during the holiday period.
"Small firms - particularly those in retail - do not have the time or resources to effectively make the changes to accommodate this temporary change and very few believe there will be any net benefit," said CFIB president Dan Kelly in a statement.
On top of the GST holiday, the federal government had also planned to send out $250 cheques in the spring to working Canadians who made $150,000 or less. But the opposition wouldn't support the plan. The Bloc wanted seniors included and the NDP also wanted cheques sent to students and people with disabilities.
The four Atlantic provinces and Ontario have a harmonized sales tax, which means the entirety of that levy - 15 per cent in the Atlantic and 13 per cent in Ontario - will be lifted. For the rest of the provinces only the five per cent GST will be lifted on exempted items.
Wendy Friedman owns The Independent Mercantile in Halifax. She appreciates the offer of limited help for consumers and retailers but doubts her business or many others will really benefit.
Several hours of administrative time will go into combing through her merchandise to figure out which are tax exempt, she said. Then there's the process of changing the tax codes for the items that make the cut.
"Then we have to spend the same amount of admin time to reverse it in two months," she said. "You begin to think, 'Is this going to be of any benefit to a small business like ours?'" Friedman said she's also concerned that people might delay shopping, especially for bigger ticket items. Then there's the timing of the tax-free holiday. It would make more sense to push it up two weeks to the beginning of December when many people start their holiday shopping and businesses are typically busiest. Most small retailers make 20 to 30 per cent of there annual sales in December, she said.
"It would give us a lot more runway before the holiday. Whereas where it's starting Dec 14 it's so close to the holiday that it's quite a tight little timeline. Considering this is only giving us 11 days before Christmas, it's not really giving a real boost to holiday shopping."
If government proceeds with the plan, CFIB wants the Department of Finance to give affected small firms a credit of a minimum of $1,000 in their GST/HST accounts to cover the administrative and programming costs. It's also asking the government to order the Canada Revenue Agency to forgive the taxes owed, penalties and interests for any good-faith errors made by small firms rushing to make this change.
Jolyn Swain, owner of Nurtured Products for Parenting in Halifax, said she expects some sleepless nights preparing for the tax holiday. But overall, she believes the tax break is a good thing and encourages people to shop during an otherwise slow period. "It's a unique time to examine where cost savings can happen for parents, especially," Swain said. "There is a lot of spending when you have young kids."
She'd like to see the tax holiday last longer and car seats, which are among the tax-exempt items, be permanently tax free because they're an essential item. Financial Post arankin@postmedia.com

Subject: Small business; Shopping; Cost control; Small & medium sized enterprises-SME

Business indexing term: Subject: Small business Cost control Small & medium sized enterprises-SME

Document 261 of 500

How Ottawa-area businesses may be hurt by Trump's tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-ottawa-area-businesses-may-be-hurt-trumps/docview/3134334216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Ottawa-area+businesses+may+be+hurt+by+Trump%27s+tariffs&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The vow by U.S. president-elect Donald Trump to smack a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian imports into the United States represents "an existential threat to Canada's economic security," according to David Ross, CEO of Ottawa's Ross Video.
"I don't think Canadians really understand what's happening. We're under attack by our closest neighbour. It's like a pandemic without the pandemic," Ross said.
He is hardly a lone voice in the economic wilderness. Just like business leaders and politicians across Canada and Mexico, which Trump has also included in his across-the-board tariff promise, Ross's primary goal is protecting jobs.
Countless economic experts suggest the tariff plan makes no sense because it would ultimately significantly raise prices in the U.S., and believe Trump could be dangling the tariff scare as a way to negotiate major concessions from Canada and Mexico in other areas. The incoming U.S. president has repeatedly said that a flood of illegal immigration and illicit drugs across his country's northern and southern borders must be stopped.
What's the potential impact on the company?
Just the same, Canadian companies must try to prepare for economic chaos.
Ross Video, a live production technology company, employs 500 in Ottawa and another 350 in Iroquois, 70 minutes southeast of the national capital.
All told, the booming business has 1,400 employees worldwide, including 20 research and development outposts in the U.S.
If you've watched TV or attended a major-league sports event, chances are Ross equipment has been involved since it's used by professional franchises and in state-of-the-art stadiums throughout North America.
The cable-suspended camera systems that provide eyes from the sky into what's happening on the field or on the ice are Ross products. Many of the world's business giants, including Google and Amazon, use the materials produced in Ottawa and its backyard.
So, too, do major U.S. broadcast networks, where Trump, ironically, has used Canadian-made products to issue his protectionist messages.
Half of Ross Video's business is in the U.S.
"That's $200 million (in revenues) that's in jeopardy," said Ross, who was in emergency meetings with the company's senior officials on Nov. 27, two days after Trump's trade war tirade landed with a thud north and south of the Canada-U.S. border.
Big picture: Ross would like the company to grow to 2,000 employees and to earn $1 billion in annual revenues by 2030, but the tariff threat is looming like a black cloud in advance of Trump's official return to the White House. Inauguration Day is Jan. 20.
"There are a few companies in the U.S. that do similar work, but not many," Ross said. "Most are in Europe and Japan that are not subject to the 25 per cent tariff. We're at a 25 per cent disadvantage."
In terms of standard trade operating practices between Canada and the U.S., Ross remembers a business conversation with his father, John Ross, who founded the company out of his Montreal basement in 1974.
"He was talking before NAFTA (the 1994 trade agreement between Canada, the U.S. and Mexico) and he said, 'Always add two per cent costs for over the border.'"
A major tariff would also affect smaller businesses with U.S. customers.
That includes Newman Tools, a Stittsville-based industrial and specialty tools distributor founded in 1955.
"With more than 50 per cent of our business in the U.S., it would significantly impact us," chief operating officer Ivy Perry said.
Could companies move operations to the U.S.?
That's not a legitimate possibility for Ross.
Again, protecting jobs here is a priority, and logistically it makes no sense.
"We're only two months away," Ross said. "If (Trump) says there would be an executive order as president on Day 1, there's no time to prepare. Building manufacturing operations takes years. You can't just do it in a month or two. We're talking about thousands and thousands of different assemblies."
The challenge for a smaller-scale operation like Newman Tools would be alerting U.S. clients about the sticker shock of having 25 per cent tacked on to the original purchase price once a product crosses the border.
"It's going to be a conversation I might have to have on every phone call," Perry said. "It might result in people trying to find the product locally."
According to Daniel Tisch, president and CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce, "Addressing this threat is not just an economic challenge, it must be a national priority."
What are business organizations saying?
Seventy-five per cent of Canadian exports are to the U.S. Naturally, reaction to Trump's comments from the Ontario Chamber of Commerce was swift and strong.
"Addressing this threat is not just an economic challenge, it must be a national priority," OCC president and CEO Daniel Tisch said.
The full-court press is on from business leaders demanding that Canadian political leaders fight back against the lofty tariffs being threatened.
"With $3.6 billion in goods and services crossing the border daily - $1 billion to and from Ontario alone - the tariff would have devastating consequences for both countries," Tisch said.
He added that Ontario was a "critical hub" for manufacturing, agriculture, technology and innovation, pointing out that U.S. consumers relied on products manufactured in the province.
Products and technology like those produced by Ross Video.
Businesses and jobs on both sides of the border are at stake.
"It would disrupt supply chains, drive up manufacturing costs, reduce exports, erode investor confidence and fuel job losses," Tisch said.
The Canadian Chamber of Commerce and University of Calgary economics professor Trevor Tombe produced a study saying that a mandatory 25 per cent tariff would cause a recession, reducing the Canadian economy by 2.6 per cent, or approximately $2,000 per person.
It works both ways, though.
Americans would immediately take a hit at the gas pump because 20 per cent of all oil used in that country comes from Canada. The tariffs would be tacked on the price at the gas pump. Patrick DeHaan, who heads petroleum analysis at GasBuddy, told CBS News that prices could rise 30-70 cents per gallon within two days of a 25 per cent tariff being imposed.
A Wall Street Journal report also suggested that the price of a new car would jump by an average of $3,000. The U.S. imports four million cars annually from Mexico and Canada.
Eventually, U.S. grocery prices could also rise.
"Prices will creep up after the supply chain dries up and (stores) have to sell new stuff for 25 per cent more," Ross said. "I don't think Americans understand tariffs."
United States president-elect Donald Trump has threatened to impose a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian exports to the his country.
What about exceptions?
There is some belief that Trump could relax the 25 per cent tariff on particular industries, including oil, as a negotiating ploy while asking Canada to improve border security.
That, of course, would do nothing to help Ross Video or Newman Tools.
"Maybe they would say they would slap a 25 per cent tariff on everything in Canada, except oil," Ross said. "My view is that Canada should be saying there should be a 100 export tariff on everything from the U.S."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, spoke with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump after the tariff threat was announced, then with Ontario Premier Doug Ford and other provincial leaders about the issue.
What are governments doing to fight back?
After speaking with Trump this week, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau adopted a conciliatory tone, saying it was a "good call," including "laying out the facts in constructive ways."
Trudeau and provincial premiers held an emergency meeting on Nov. 27. Despite their political differences on many topics, the aim was to reach consensus on a "Team Canada" approach: speaking as one unified voice against Trump's tariff pledge.
"I don't want to minimize for a moment the gravity of the challenge we now face," Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland said. "Now is not the moment to squabble amongst ourselves."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford is among those loudly pushing for increased border security to slow down illegal border crossings and the movement of guns and drugs into the U.S. That's a message that would appeal to Trump and his base.
For what it's worth, outgoing U.S. president Joe Biden is not a fan of the tariff plan. "It's a counter-productive thing to do," Biden said on Nov. 28. "We're surrounded by the Pacific Ocean, Atlantic Ocean and two allies: Mexico and Canada. The last thing we need to do is screw up those relationships."
kwarren@postmedia.com

Document 262 of 500

Teramura: How an Ottawa cyclist end up in the ER, thinking about Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/teramura-how-ottawa-cyclist-end-up-er-thinking/docview/3134255383/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teramura%3A+How+an+Ottawa+cyclist+end+up+in+the+ER%2C+thinking+about+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recently, I had all night to reflect on provincial government health and transportation policy.
The occasion was a visit to the emergency room at The Ottawa Hospital. Here, I waited with many others, all staring at the monitors that displayed the anticipated wait time depending on the severity of your case. With my bloody facial lacerations and bruises, still sitting in the wheelchair I was brought in on, I certainly looked the part for an appearance in this room. Fortunately, I was only there as a precautionary measure, to get X-rays and a CT scan to verify that I did not have a more serious injury.
Others were not so lucky: one middle-aged man staggered in clutching his chest with the classic body language of the heart attack victim. Another, stumbling in with the characteristic sad hunch of the chronic heroin user, ranted and raved for a bit but was eventually escorted out. A regular. Most, however, did not seem to be exhibiting any obvious injuries or signs of discomfort; I suspect they were there because they had no other options, feeling something was wrong but lacking a family doctor.
The event that put in me in the ER in the first place was a traffic accident while cycling, so naturally my thoughts also turned to recent announcements by the Ontario government. A cautious cyclist, I stick to protected bike lanes and pathways, but as my experience showed, bike lanes still have intersections where cyclists have to contend with cars. In this one, a driver rolled through a stop sign towing a large trailer into my path, forcing me to stop abruptly and crash.
Some readers will have already concluded that, being a cyclist, I must naturally be at fault for having the wrong clothes, no light, or something, so let me be clear: I was in a bright orange jacket, my bike was equipped with a powerful headlamp, and I was travelling at the speed of a 60-year-old man on a 30-year-old mountain bike. The driver was just careless; he would later admit to police he forgot he was towing that trailer.
Many people came to my assistance that evening, but the words of two of them stuck with me. The paramedic's, who observed that "people have forgotten how to drive" as she fitted me with a cervical collar, and what the ER doctor said immediately upon entering my room: "I'm sorry." He wasn't breaking bad news, just apologizing for the chaos in the waiting room over which he had no control. He must be saying that all day.
Early that night, it occurred to me how absurd it is that the Ontario government is sending cheques to us when it can't adequately provide basics such as primary care, emergency services in hospitals, or narcotics addiction treatment.
It's also obvious to me that its plan to tear up bike lanes is equally ridiculous as, in addition to probably sending another client to an overburdened ER somewhere every day, it ignores the reality of what's happening on our roads: we drivers are too numerous, and too many of us have indeed forgotten how to drive. Getting rid of bike lanes will not do anything about traffic volume and motorists' sloppy, inconsiderate use of roads.
We've all seen plenty of bad behaviour by road users of all types. But only one has the ability to cause so much bodily harm. Drivers today are too distracted, too impatient and, frankly, too mean to enjoy the privilege of being the dominant road users in all environments.
If the Doug Ford government wishes to partially download funding the quality and safety of the public realm to us citizens by sending us money, then I will oblige them by donating half of my cheque to The Ottawa Hospital, and half to a cycling advocacy organization. Both donations will bear the dedication: "In Memory of the Ontario Progressive Conservative Government."
Allan Teramura is a cyclist, pedestrian and motorist in Ottawa.
Related
Leiper: Doug Ford's bike-lane legislation is about politics, not safety or traffic flow
Adam: Doug Ford is outsourcing his family doctor problem

Subject: Emergency medical care; Automobile drivers; Traffic accidents & safety; Bicycling

Document 263 of 500

How Donald Trump's tariffs could hurt Ottawa-area businesses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-donald-trumps-tariffs-could-hurt-ottawa-area/docview/3134233950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Donald+Trump%27s+tariffs+could+hurt+Ottawa-area+businesses&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The vow by U.S. president-elect Donald Trump to smack a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian imports into the United States represents "an existential threat to Canada's economic security," according to David Ross, CEO of Ottawa's Ross Video.
"I don't think Canadians really understand what's happening. We're under attack by our closest neighbour. It's like a pandemic without the pandemic," Ross said.
He is hardly a lone voice in the economic wilderness. Just like business leaders and politicians across Canada and Mexico, which Trump has also included in his across-the-board tariff promise, Ross's primary goal is protecting jobs.
Countless economic experts suggest the tariff plan makes no sense because it would ultimately significantly raise prices in the U.S., and believe Trump could be dangling the tariff scare as a way to negotiate major concessions from Canada and Mexico in other areas. The incoming U.S. president has repeatedly said that a flood of illegal immigration and illicit drugs across his country's northern and southern borders must be stopped.
What's the potential impact on the company?
Just the same, Canadian companies must try to prepare for economic chaos.
Ross Video, a live production technology company, employs 500 in Ottawa and another 350 in Iroquois, 70 minutes southeast of the national capital.
All told, the booming business has 1,400 employees worldwide, including 20 research and development outposts in the U.S.
If you've watched TV or attended a major-league sports event, chances are Ross equipment has been involved since it's used by professional franchises and in state-of-the-art stadiums throughout North America.
The cable-suspended camera systems that provide eyes from the sky into what's happening on the field or on the ice are Ross products. Many of the world's business giants, including Google and Amazon, use the materials produced in Ottawa and its backyard.
So, too, do major U.S. broadcast networks, where Trump, ironically, has used Canadian-made products to issue his protectionist messages.
Half of Ross Video's business is in the U.S.
"That's $200 million (in revenues) that's in jeopardy," said Ross, who was in emergency meetings with the company's senior officials on Nov. 27, two days after Trump's trade war tirade landed with a thud north and south of the Canada-U.S. border.
Big picture: Ross would like the company to grow to 2,000 employees and to earn $1 billion in annual revenues by 2030, but the tariff threat is looming like a black cloud in advance of Trump's official return to the White House. Inauguration Day is Jan. 20.
"There are a few companies in the U.S. that do similar work, but not many," Ross said. "Most are in Europe and Japan that are not subject to the 25 per cent tariff. We're at a 25 per cent disadvantage."
In terms of standard trade operating practices between Canada and the U.S., Ross remembers a business conversation with his father, John Ross, who founded the company out of his Montreal basement in 1974.
"He was talking before NAFTA (the 1994 trade agreement between Canada, the U.S. and Mexico) and he said, 'Always add two per cent costs for over the border.'"
A major tariff would also affect smaller businesses with U.S. customers.
That includes Newman Tools, a Stittsville-based industrial and specialty tools distributor founded in 1955.
"With more than 50 per cent of our business in the U.S., it would significantly impact us," chief operating officer Ivy Perry said.
Could companies move operations to the U.S.?
That's not a legitimate possibility for Ross.
Again, protecting jobs here is a priority, and logistically it makes no sense.
"We're only two months away," Ross said. "If (Trump) says there would be an executive order as president on Day 1, there's no time to prepare. Building manufacturing operations takes years. You can't just do it in a month or two. We're talking about thousands and thousands of different assemblies."
The challenge for a smaller-scale operation like Newman Tools would be alerting U.S. clients about the sticker shock of having 25 per cent tacked on to the original purchase price once a product crosses the border.
"It's going to be a conversation I might have to have on every phone call," Perry said. "It might result in people trying to find the product locally."
According to Daniel Tisch, president and CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce, "Addressing this threat is not just an economic challenge, it must be a national priority."
What are business organizations saying?
Seventy-five per cent of Canadian exports are to the U.S. Naturally, reaction to Trump's comments from the Ontario Chamber of Commerce was swift and strong.
"Addressing this threat is not just an economic challenge, it must be a national priority," OCC president and CEO Daniel Tisch said.
The full-court press is on from business leaders demanding that Canadian political leaders fight back against the lofty tariffs being threatened.
"With $3.6 billion in goods and services crossing the border daily - $1 billion to and from Ontario alone - the tariff would have devastating consequences for both countries," Tisch said.
He added that Ontario was a "critical hub" for manufacturing, agriculture, technology and innovation, pointing out that U.S. consumers relied on products manufactured in the province.
Products and technology like those produced by Ross Video.
Businesses and jobs on both sides of the border are at stake.
"It would disrupt supply chains, drive up manufacturing costs, reduce exports, erode investor confidence and fuel job losses," Tisch said.
The Canadian Chamber of Commerce and University of Calgary economics professor Trevor Tombe produced a study saying that a mandatory 25 per cent tariff would cause a recession, reducing the Canadian economy by 2.6 per cent, or approximately $2,000 per person.
It works both ways, though.
Americans would immediately take a hit at the gas pump because 20 per cent of all oil used in that country comes from Canada. The tariffs would be tacked on the price at the gas pump. Patrick DeHaan, who heads petroleum analysis at GasBuddy, told CBS News that prices could rise 30-70 cents per gallon within two days of a 25 per cent tariff being imposed.
A Wall Street Journal report also suggested that the price of a new car would jump by an average of $3,000. The U.S. imports four million cars annually from Mexico and Canada.
Eventually, U.S. grocery prices could also rise.
"Prices will creep up after the supply chain dries up and (stores) have to sell new stuff for 25 per cent more," Ross said. "I don't think Americans understand tariffs."
United States president-elect Donald Trump has threatened to impose a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian exports to the his country.
What about exceptions?
There is some belief that Trump could relax the 25 per cent tariff on particular industries, including oil, as a negotiating ploy while asking Canada to improve border security.
That, of course, would do nothing to help Ross Video or Newman Tools.
"Maybe they would say they would slap a 25 per cent tariff on everything in Canada, except oil," Ross said. "My view is that Canada should be saying there should be a 100 export tariff on everything from the U.S."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, spoke with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump after the tariff threat was announced, then with Ontario Premier Doug Ford and other provincial leaders about the issue.
What are governments doing to fight back?
After speaking with Trump this week, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau adopted a conciliatory tone, saying it was a "good call," including "laying out the facts in constructive ways."
Trudeau and provincial premiers held an emergency meeting on Nov. 27. Despite their political differences on many topics, the aim was to reach consensus on a "Team Canada" approach: speaking as one unified voice against Trump's tariff pledge.
"I don't want to minimize for a moment the gravity of the challenge we now face," Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland said. "Now is not the moment to squabble amongst ourselves."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford is among those loudly pushing for increased border security to slow down illegal border crossings and the movement of guns and drugs into the U.S. That's a message that would appeal to Trump and his base.
For what it's worth, outgoing U.S. president Joe Biden is not a fan of the tariff plan. "It's a counter-productive thing to do," Biden said on Nov. 28. "We're surrounded by the Pacific Ocean, Atlantic Ocean and two allies: Mexico and Canada. The last thing we need to do is screw up those relationships."
kwarren@postmedia.com
X: Citizenkwarren

Document 264 of 500

Not a moment to 'squabble'over U.S. tariff threat: Freeland; Unity among provinces needed amid talk of significant levies on imports

Author: Duggan, Kyle; Karadeglija, Anja

Publication info: Duggan, Kyle; Karadeglija, Anja. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/not-moment-squabbleover-u-s-tariff-threat/docview/3134225357/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Not+a+moment+to+%27squabble%27over+U.S.+tariff+threat%3A+Freeland%3B+Unity+among+provinces+needed+amid+talk+of+significant+levies+on+imports&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.7&amp;au=Duggan%2C+Kyle%3BKaradeglija%2C+Anja&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Unity among provinces and the federal government is critical in the face of the grave challenge posed by the threat of massive new import tariffs from Canada's closest trading partner, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said following an emergency first ministers' meeting.
The premiers met virtually Wednesday with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, along with Freeland, Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc and RCMP Deputy Commissioner Mark Flynn. The meeting came just 48 hours after U.S. president-elect Donald Trump said he will impose punishing new taxes on all Canadian and Mexican imports the same day he is sworn into office in January.
Trump said he will keep the tariffs in place until both countries move to stem the flow of illegal migrants and drugs into the United States.
"I don't want to minimize for a moment the gravity of the challenge we now face," Freeland told reporters on Parliament Hill, shortly after the meeting ended.
She said Canadians should have confidence that while Canada depends on the United States, "the United States also depends on us."
She said that the way to meet the challenge successfully is by presenting a united front "especially when it comes to our conversations with our American neighbours."
"Now is really a moment for us not to squabble amongst ourselves," Freeland said.
She said she felt there was an agreement around the table that it was the time for "everyone to play for Team Canada" - but that unity was already being tested as Freeland spoke.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who chairs the premiers'council, put out a statement following the meeting that accused the federal government of being flat-footed in the face of Trump's threat. He said Ontario has pushed Ottawa for months "to show that Canada understands, cares and is responsive to U.S. security and economic concerns."
"During this evening's meeting, I stressed that the federal government has been slow to react and is stuck on its backfoot," said Ford, who joined the meeting from Toronto.
"I expressed my hope that this evening's meeting is the start of a more proactive approach from the federal government, including by showing that it takes the security of our border seriously by cracking down on illegal border crossings and stopping the transport of guns and illegal and illicit drugs like fentanyl, or risk the economic chaos of Trump tariffs."
Earlier in the day, Ford said there was not enough funding to properly police the border.
Ford said he met with officials from the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration, the Canada Border Services Agency and Ontario Provincial Police before the meeting.
LeBlanc, whose portfolio includes the Canada Border Services Agency, said the RCMP meets weekly with American law enforcement agencies including U.S. Customs and Border Protection and the Drug Enforcement Agency.
LeBlanc said there are conversations about additional resources for the border, and said he would hope that no party in Parliament would block any kind of spending bill to pay for those resources.
LeBlanc was referring to the ongoing stalemate in the House of Commons as the Conservatives prevent debate on most legislation until the Liberals provide unredacted documents on allegations of misspending at a now-defunct federal green technology fund.
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew said that during the meeting, he discussed strengthening federal investment in the RCMP in Manitoba and law enforcement generally.
"The point that I made to the prime minister and the premiers on the call is, listen, Manitobans have long been talking about drugs being an issue in our communities. This is going back many years," Kinew said.
"And so, what's the downside if we invest in law enforcement and we crack down on drug trafficking here, at a time when that message might be well received in the (United States)?" Quebec Premier Francois Legault said he asked Trudeau for a plan to secure the borders, but came out of the meeting without a "clear answer."
"I clearly asked Mr. Trudeau to submit a detailed plan to better secure the borders, to avoid Mr. Trump's 25 per cent tariff," he said. "I didn't get a clear answer, but we hope (for it) because there was support from other premiers as well," he said.
He added: "I don't think it's time to play at, 'Is it true or not that our borders aren't secure."'In a statement issued after the meeting, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith called for the federal government and provinces bordering the United States "to take immediate steps to crack down heavily on these illegal border activities."
"I communicated this very clearly to the prime minister, and further indicated that Alberta will be acting urgently and decisively to patrol our own shared border with Montana, with more details to be announced soon in that regard."
The premiers had requested a meeting on Monday, before Trump issued his tariff threat.
University of Calgary economics professor Trevor Tombe authored a study this fall with the Canadian Chamber of Commerce examining what that could cost Canada. After running the numbers this week for 25 per cent tariffs, he posted on social media that it would cause a recession.
Tombe said the economy in Canada next year would shrink 2.6 per cent, or by about $2,000 per person.
Freeland said while Canada depends on the United States, the reverse is also true. The Americans are heavily dependent on Canada for oil, electricity and critical minerals and metals, she said.
"When it came to the economic relationship, we talked about the fact that trade between Canada and the United States is balanced and mutually beneficial," Freeland said of the meeting. "That Canada is the largest market for the United States by far, larger than China, Japan, the U.K. and France combined."
Trump has pledged to cut American energy bills in half within 18 months, something that could be made harder if a 25 per cent premium is added to Canadian oil imports. In 2023, Canadian oil accounted for almost two-thirds of total U.S. oil imports and about one-fifth of the U.S. oil supply. The Canadian Press With files from Morgan Lowrie in Montreal, Brenna Owen in Vancouver, Allison Jones in Toronto and Steve Lambert in Winnipeg

Subject: Federal government; Law enforcement; Prime ministers; Tariffs; Canada-US relations

An injured cyclist muses on Ford's $200 rebates; Spend it on basic services instead, Allan Teramura says

Author: Teramura, Allan

Publication info: Teramura, Allan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/injured-cyclist-muses-on-fords-200-rebates-spend/docview/3134225040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+injured+cyclist+muses+on+Ford%27s+%24200+rebates%3B+Spend+it+on+basic+services+instead%2C+Allan+Teramura+says&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Teramura%2C+Allan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recently, I had all night to reflect on provincial government health and transportation policy.
The occasion was a visit to the emergency room at The Ottawa Hospital. Here, I waited with many others, all staring at the monitors that displayed the anticipated wait time depending on the severity of your case. With my bloody facial lacerations and bruises, still sitting in the wheelchair I was brought in on, I certainly looked the part for an appearance in this room. Another, stumbling in with the characteristic sad hunch of the chronic heroin user, ranted and raved for a bit but was eventually escorted out. A regular. Most, however, did not seem to be exhibiting any obvious injuries or signs of discomfort; I suspect they were there because they had no other options, feeling something was wrong but lacking a family doctor.
The event that put in me in the ER in the first place was a traffic collision while cycling, so naturally my thoughts also turned to recent announcements by the Ontario government.
A cautious cyclist, I stick to protected bike lanes and pathways, but as my experience showed, bike lanes still have intersections where cyclists have to contend with cars. In this one, a driver rolled through a stop sign towing a large trailer into my path, forcing me to stop abruptly and crash.
Some readers will have already concluded that, being a cyclist, I must naturally be at fault for having the wrong clothes, no light or something, so let me be clear: I was in a bright orange jacket, my bike equipped with a powerful headlamp and I was travelling at the speed of a 60-yearold man on a 30-year-old mountain bike. The driver was just careless; he would later admit to police he forgot he was towing that trailer.

It's also obvious to me that its plan to tear up bike lanes is equally ridiculous as, in addition to probably sending another client to an overburdened ER somewhere every day, it ignores the reality of what's happening on our roads: we drivers are too numerous, and too many of us have indeed forgotten how to drive. Getting rid of bike lanes will not do anything about traffic volume and motorists'sloppy, inconsiderate use of roads.
We've all seen plenty of bad behaviour by road users of all types. But only one has the ability to cause so much bodily harm. Drivers today are too distracted, too impatient and, frankly, too mean to enjoy the privilege of being the dominant road users in all environments.
If the Doug Ford government wishes to partially download funding the quality and safety of the public realm to us citizens by sending us money, then I will oblige them by donating half of my cheque to The Ottawa Hospital, and half to a cycling advocacy organization. Both donations will bear the dedication: "In Memory of the Ontario Progressive Conservative Government." Allan Teramura is a cyclist, pedestrian and motorist in Ottawa.

Subject: Emergency medical care; Automobile drivers; Bicycling

Identifier / keyword: recently; night; reflect; provincial; government; health

Title: An injured cyclist muses on Ford's $200 rebates; Spend it on basic services instead, Allan Teramura says

Publication title: The Ottawa Citizen; Ottawa, Ont.

Document 266 of 500

Today's letters: 'Tent' works for Ottawa hospital. So why not for refugees?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-tent-works-ottawa-hospital-so-why/docview/3133926784/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+%27Tent%27+works+for+Ottawa+hospital.+So+why+not+for+refugees%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hospital 'tent' shows model can work for housing too
Re: Ottawa identifies two sites for tent-like temporary housing for newcomers, Nov. 8.
Late last fall, I spent eight days at the Civic campus of the Ottawa Hospital in the Offload Medicine Transition Unit (OMTU) that some of us affectionately called the "tent." Now, if that "Sprung" structure is good enough to treat patients at Ottawa's top hospital, I'm not sure what people are complaining about.
Apart from top-notch treatment and care, the "tent" accommodates 40 patients and has nursing stations, washrooms, showers and a physiotherapy assessment area. "Built to withstand the four seasons of the Canadian climate, the OMTU is equipped with mechanical systems to allow negative air pressure, oxygen/suction at each headwall, a nurse call system, local paging and emergency power, replicating many of the required conditions of a hospital," the builder says.
This temporary facility was an emergency response to the early days of the COVID pandemic. The two proposed structures for housing refugees are meant to deal with the current housing crisis. Tent, indeed!
Denis Desjardins, Ottawa
Trim Road LRT work is a mess
When the LRT extension to Trim Road was being announced and promoted, our elected officials told us it was a situation of "short term pain for long term gain." I would suggest that it is rather long-term pain for no gains. Not only has the LRT service not been extended to Trim Road (more than two years behind schedule), but apparent construction activities constantly force the closure of lanes on Highway 174, creating traffic delays as well as more local delays on connecting streets such as Jeanne d'Arc Boulevard. All this with no end in sight.
To top it off, the extensive lane closures, such as one on Nov. 22, occur with no apparent work being done. It is time for the city to apologize to its citizens and rein in this out-of-control project.
Denis A. Hotte, Ottawa
Let's go back to cubicle land for public servants
As a public servant for 33 years (1979-2012) across six different departments, I always had my own private office cubicle, and I liked it.
It was great, providing a quiet and productive place to work, some privacy, a place where you could hang your hat, eat lunch, meet with coworkers, store files, and make phone calls. You could personalize it with a couple of photos and other items.
Today, why would anyone want to work in a "co-working" communal area concept, with no privacy and where, due to lingering flu or COVID, common desks, chairs and computers should be wiped down before every use?
Maybe this is why many public servants still want to work at home.
Joe Holmes, Ottawa
Doug Ford could do more to help the hungry
Re: Pellerin: No one in Ottawa should be going hungry - particularly since we can fix it, Nov. 21.
In her column on hunger, Brigitte Pellerin manages to address the issue without a single word about the Ford government's grossly inadequate welfare rates, especially for Ontario Works. Try living on $733 a month.
Reuel S. Amdur, Gatineau
Related
Today's letters: Don't sow fear over housing refugees in Ottawa
Today's letters: Ottawa police don't need to buy horses

Document 267 of 500

Ontario nurses call for patient caps to ease staffing crisis

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-nurses-call-patient-caps-ease-staffing/docview/3133840907/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+nurses+call+for+patient+caps+to+ease+staffing+crisis&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nursing leaders from Canada and around the world met in Ottawa this week to talk about mandating minimum nurse-to-patient ratios, something they say will help retain nurses and lessen staff shortages.
During a period in which nurse shortages have become common, it may seem counterintuitive to call for more hiring, but nursing officials say staffing ratios are an important tool to improve nursing shortages. Unsafe staffing levels are a key reason why so many nurses are quitting or choosing to work part time, said Linda Silas, president of the Canadian Federation of Nurses Unions (CFNU).
The summit, which was hosted by CFNU, brought together national and international nursing leaders, including Canada's Chief Nursing Officer Leigh Chapman and nursing union heads from British Columbia, which is the first province in Canada to introduce minimum nurse-to-patient ratios, and Nova Scotia, which is working on implementing them. The summit also heard from American, British and Australian nursing leaders.
Mandated nurse-patient ratios are new in Canada, but they have been in place in other parts of the world for years.
Kimberly McMillan, associate professor at the University of Ottawa and a speaker at the summit, says there is plenty of evidence that more nurses are better for patients.
"We have had data for decades to say that more nursing hours equals better patient outcomes."
They are also an important nurse safety and nurse retention issue, Silas said. Without that, nurses risk burnout, low job satisfaction and the possibility of moral injuries from being unable to offer the care they know patients need, she added.
"It is a solution for patient safety," Silas said, "but also a solution to retain our nurses."
Half of nurses in Canada work part-time. Silas says nursing officials often hear that is related to job satisfaction and workloads.
"What they are telling us is they can't handle the workload. They love being a nurse, but they hate their job," Silas said.
In Canada, the number of nurses assigned to hospital units is often based on patient acuity levels. That means patients who require critical care typically have one-on-one nursing care. But staffing levels for patients who are not receiving critical care are often inadequate for nurses to have time to do their work properly, Silas said.
Ontario has the lowest rate of registered nurses per 100,000 people, at 651, a number that is dropping, according to data from the Canadian Institute for Health Information (CIHI) released earlier this year.
The RN-to-population ratio in the rest of the country has also declined - from an average of 830 RNs-per-100,000 in 2021 to 825 RNs-per-100,000 in 2022, according to CIHI.
Ontario NDP Health Critic France Gélinas introduced a private member's bill earlier this year that would regulate patient-nurse ratios to prevent burnout, but that bill did not move forward.

Subject: Workforce planning; Patient safety; Job satisfaction; Ratios; Nurses; Critical care; Nursing care

Business indexing term: Subject: Workforce planning Job satisfaction Ratios

Document 268 of 500

First ministers to talk tariff threat; Pm, premiers to meet wednesday after trump vows to add 25% to canadian exports

Author: Levesque, Carine

Publication info: Levesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/first-ministers-talk-tariff-threat-pm-premiers/docview/3133543389/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=First+ministers+to+talk+tariff+threat%3B+Pm%2C+premiers+to+meet+wednesday+after+trump+vows+to+add+25%25+to+canadian+exports&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Levesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has agreed to Ontario Premier Doug Ford's request to hold a First Ministers'Meeting on Wednesday after U.S. president-elect Donald Trump threatened to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian exports.
Trump announced on Monday evening that he would slap the punitive tariffs on all products entering the United States from Canada and Mexico on Jan. 20, 2025, his first day in office, unless those countries stop the flow of drugs and migrants across their borders.
"As everyone is aware, thousands of people are pouring through Mexico and Canada, bringing Crime and Drugs at levels never seen before," Trump said on Truth Social on Monday night.
Trudeau said on his way to a cabinet meeting on Tuesday that he had a "good call" with Trump the night before, two hours after Trump's post.
"We obviously talked about laying out the facts, (talked) about how the intense and effective connections between our two countries flow back and forth. We talked about some of the challenges that we can work on together," he said.
"This is a relationship that we know takes a certain amount of working on and that's what we'll do," he added.
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly said that the threat of tariffs was already high on the government's radar as Trump had floated the idea of slapping 10 per cent tariffs. But she would not say whether the government was taken by surprise by 25 per cent tariffs.
Joly said she has already had many conversations with "key American leaders" and would continue to "operate diplomatically." But she would not go into more detail as to what she has been telling those stakeholders and if it involves threats of retaliatory tariffs.
"I really think that sometimes it's better to do so in private settings," she said.
As chair of the Council of the Federation, Ford wrote to Trudeau to ask him to meet with all premiers to talk about Canada-U.S. relations in a letter sent Monday. Trudeau said he talked to Ford shortly after Trump's announcement and agreed to a meeting this week.
Ford later revealed the meeting will be taking place Wednesday at 5 p.m.
Federal ministers attempted to stay positive in the light of Trump's latest tariff threat.
"We've had challenges before, we'll have challenges again, but they are a long-standing ally and we're going to do what we can to work through kinds of challenges to ensure we protect the interests of Canadians," said Housing Minister Sean Fraser.
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said the government still has two months to convince the incoming U.S. administration to drop the threat of tariffs.
"We're already in contact with our American partners to make sure to highlight the exceptional nature of the relationship with Canada because one needs to know that a decision like this will have an important economic impact in the United States as well."
The government also rejected any suggestion that illegal border crossings from Canada into the United States can be compared to Mexico.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller said the number of Canada's illegal border crossings are "the equivalent, on a yearly basis, of a significant weekend at the Mexico border." U.S. Customs and Border Protection data show its officers recorded nearly 200,000 encounters at the northern border between October 2023 and September 2024. The same period in 2022 saw more than 109,000 encounters and there were around 32,000 in 2020.
The term "encounters" includes apprehensions, people who are deemed inadmissible and those who are expelled from the U.S.
Between October 2023 and September 2024 U.S. officials recorded more than two million encounters at the Mexican border. The two prior years also saw more than two million encounters at the southern border.
Chief border patrol agent Robert Garcia said last month on X that agents in the Swanton Sector, which covers Vermont's border with Quebec, apprehended more than 19,000 people from 97 countries in the last year - more than the last 17 years combined. U.S. Customs and Border Protection said it also seized nearly 5,000 kilograms of illegal drugs at the Canadian border between October 2023 and September 2024. That included 19.5 kilograms of fentanyl.
Comparatively, border agents seized nearly 125,000 kilograms of narcotics at the southern border, including almost 10,000 kilograms of fentanyl.
According to the U.S.
Drug Enforcement Agency, two milligrams of fentanyl is a potentially fatal dose.
From Canada, marijuana is by far the most commonly seized drug, accounting for almost 60 per cent of total seizures. From Mexico it's methamphetamine, accounting for about 57 per cent of seizures at the southern border.
Business groups are already nervous about the prospect of tariffs on Canada's economy.
Candace Laing, president and CEO of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, said now is not the time to be the United States'"nice neighbour."
"We must be prepared to take a couple of punches if we're going to stake out our position. It's time to trade 'sorry'for 'sorry, not sorry,'" said Laing in a statement.
Labour groups, on the other hand, were questioning if Trump is bluffing.
"Is this just sabre-rattling at this moment in time or will this really come to pass? There are so many questions before we come to Jan. 20," asked Canadian Labour Congress president Bea Bruske in a press conference on Parliament Hill Tuesday.
National Post, with additional reporting by The Canadian Press calevesque@postmedia.com

Document 269 of 500

Transit could hike final property tax bill more; OC Transpo's dire status should be the big story of the budget debate

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/transit-could-hike-final-property-tax-bill-more/docview/3133543249/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Transit+could+hike+final+property+tax+bill+more%3B+OC+Transpo%27s+dire+status+should+be+the+big+story+of+the+budget+debate&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One thing that is almost guaranteed in a City of Ottawa budget is that some little sideshow will draw an inordinate amount of public and political attention while the big numbers go mostly undiscussed. This year, it was a proposed 120 per cent increase in the cost of bus passes for seniors, a bad idea easily fixed.
Of rather greater consequence is the fact the city's budget relies on $36 million in hoped-for transit money from federal and provincial governments. If that doesn't happen, property tax increases will go from a marginally acceptable 3.9 per cent to an unsatisfactory 5.9 per cent, just over half of that for transit alone. The clock is ticking toward budget approval day Dec. 11.
Transit's financial time bomb ought to be the dominant story of this budget, but awareness didn't really start until Mayor Mark Sutcliffe started highlighting it late this summer.
Multi-year transit shortfalls of about $120 million a year seemed to come out of the blue and were wildly out of sync with the deficits council had coped with in previous years. Last year, OC Transpo had a $29-million operating deficit that was mostly offset by $27.8 million in operational cost reductions.
So what happened? Was the city taken by surprise? The official answer is that the rapidly increasing transit cost was anticipated in the city's long-range financial plan. Yes, but not in a timely way. A 2019 update of the city's 2017 transit cost forecast noted some increased costs but determined that they could be met with small increases in taxes and fares. It even predicted that taxpayer subsidies of transit would go down over time.
Then the pandemic hit, ridership collapsed and the system was kept afloat with the help of temporary federal and provincial money. Optimists thought everything would return to normal.
By September of 2023, city staff had wrapped their heads around the magnitude of the long-term transit problem. In a briefing for councillors, they estimated that OC Transpo faced a $6.6 billion gap between revenue and expenses over the next 30 years. Compared to their 2019 forecast, fare revenue was down sharply and the cost of pretty much every aspect of the system had gone up more than anticipated. Operating the LRT, in particular, was far more expensive than previously imagined.
The concerning numbers received little media or public attention. By July of this year, city staff said the gap had widened to $8.6 billion. The 2025 budget is one step in dealing with that problem. Transit taxes and fares are up. Transit pass discounting is down, operating efficiencies have been made and capital costs deferred.
In the long run, the tax and fare increases will pare about $1.5 billion off the transit deficit, but this year's problem still isn't solved because the city is $36 million short. Sutcliffe has made a compelling case for help, focusing on the federal government's underpayment of property taxes and the provincial government's generous transit subsidies for Toronto and surrounding municipalities.
It was a point bolstered by a recent report from the province's Financial Accountability Office, which calculated that Toronto and surrounding regions got a $196.49-per-resident transit subsidy in 2024-25. Ottawa got a mere $31.91.
The facts are on Ottawa's side, but the politics are less promising. Both Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau are busy Christmas shopping for votes, spending billions of dollars on sending cheques to all and sundry because of a new-found concern about affordability. It's vote-buying season and the number of votes to be had by giving Ottawa fairer treatment is limited. If either government cuts a special transit deal for Ottawa, other municipalities will be sticking their hands out too. Well, except Toronto. Its hands are already full.
For the prime minister and the premier, the easiest thing to do about Ottawa's city budget problem is nothing at all. The fact that our city lacks an influential cabinet champion at either level doesn't help.
Cross your fingers, but brace yourself for a 5.9 per cent tax increase. Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist and author. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail. com.

Subject: Capital costs; Budgets; Subsidies; Prime ministers; Property taxes; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Capital costs Budgets Subsidies Property taxes Tax increases

Company / organization: Name: OC Transpo; NAICS: 485112

Identifier / keyword: thing; guaranteed; ottawa; budget; little; sideshow

McKenney announced as provincial NDP candidate for Ottawa Centre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mckenney-announced-as-provincial-ndp-candidate/docview/3133542480/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=McKenney+announced+as+provincial+NDP+candidate+for+Ottawa+Centre&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former downtown city councillor Catherine McKenney was announced as the Ontario NDP candidate for the provincial Ottawa Centre riding.
The party announced Nov. 23 on the social media site X that McKenney was nominated as the candidate in the riding. McKenney had thrown their hat in the ring in October for the nomination.
"McKenney, a former Ottawa city councillor, has a proven track record of effective and compassionate leadership," the party said. "We are proud to have Catherine McKenney join Marit Stiles and the Ontario NDP team as we fight to get Doug Ford out and bring in a government that's focused on the basics."
The seat is currently held by NDP MPP Joel Harden, who will instead look to run as the federal NDP candidate in Ottawa Centre against Liberal MP Yasir Naqvi, himself a former MPP for the riding.
An Ontario election is expected no later than June 4, 2026, but Premier Doug Ford has declined to shoot down speculation that he will move for an earlier election date. Postmedia News

Ontario nurses call for patient caps to fix staffing crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-nurses-call-patient-caps-fix-staffing/docview/3133408137/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+nurses+call+for+patient+caps+to+fix+staffing+crisis&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

"It is a solution for patient safety," Silas said, "but also a solution to retain our nurses."
Half of nurses in Canada work part time. Silas said nursing officials often hear that is related to job satisfaction and workloads.
"What they are telling us is they can't handle the workload. They love being a nurse, but they hate their job," said Silas.
In Canada, the number of nurses assigned to hospital units is often based on patient acuity levels. That means patients who require critical care typically have one-on-one nursing care. But staffing levels for patients who are not receiving critical care are often inadequate for nurses to have time to do their work properly, said Silas.
Ontario has the lowest rate of registered nurses per 100,000 people, at 651, a number that is dropping, according to data from the Canadian Institute for Health Information (CIHI) released earlier this year.
The RN-to-population ratio in the rest of the country has also declined - from an average of 830 RNs-per-100,000 in 2021 to 825 RNs-per-100,000 in 2022, according to CIHI.
Ontario NDP Health Critic France Gelinas introduced a private members bill earlier this year that would regulate patient-nurse ratios to prevent burnout, but that bill did not move forward.
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newsletters so we can keep you informed.
Related
Ottawa patients continue to face longer than average emergency waits
Almonte hospital emergency department reopens after staffing-related closure

Document 272 of 500

Denley: Ottawa's transit costs could hike your final tax bill even more

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/denley-ottawas-transit-costs-could-hike-your/docview/3133394071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Denley%3A+Ottawa%27s+transit+costs+could+hike+your+final+tax+bill+even+more&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One thing that is almost guaranteed in a City of Ottawa budget is that some little sideshow will draw an inordinate amount of public and political attention while the big numbers go mostly undiscussed. This year, it was a proposed 120-per-cent increase in the cost of bus passes for seniors, a bad idea easily fixed.
Of rather greater consequence is the fact the city's budget relies on $36 million in hoped-for transit money from federal and provincial governments. If that doesn't happen, property tax increases will go from a marginally acceptable 3.9 per cent to an unsatisfactory 5.9 per cent, just over half of that for transit alone. The clock is ticking toward budget approval day Dec. 11.
Transit's financial time bomb ought to be the dominant story of this budget, but awareness didn't really start until Mayor Mark Sutcliffe started highlighting it late this summer.
Multi-year transit shortfalls of about $120 million a year seemed to come out of the blue and were wildly out of sync with the deficits council had coped with in previous years. Last year, OC Transpo had a $29-million operating deficit that was mostly offset by $27.8 million in operational cost reductions.
So what happened? Was the city taken by surprise?
The official answer is that the rapidly increasing transit cost was anticipated in the city's long-range financial plan. Yes, but not in a timely way. A 2019 update of the city's 2017 transit cost forecast noted some increased costs but determined that they could be met with small increases in taxes and fares. It even predicted that taxpayer subsidies of transit would go down over time.
Then the pandemic hit, ridership collapsed and the system was kept afloat with the help of temporary federal and provincial money. Optimists thought everything would return to normal.
By September of 2023, city staff had wrapped their heads around the magnitude of the long-term transit problem. In a briefing for councillors, they estimated that OC Transpo faced a $6.6 billion gap between revenue and expenses over the next 30 years. Compared to their 2019 forecast, fare revenue was down sharply and the cost of pretty much every aspect of the system had gone up more than anticipated. Operating the LRT, in particular, was far more expensive than previously imagined.
The concerning numbers received little media or public attention. By July of this year, city staff said the gap had widened to $8.6 billion. The 2025 budget is one step in dealing with that problem. Transit taxes and fares are up. Transit pass discounting is down, operating efficiencies have been made and capital costs deferred.
In the long run, the tax and fare increases will pare about $1.5 billion off the transit deficit, but this year's problem still isn't solved because the city is $36 million short. Sutcliffe has made a compelling case for help, focusing on the federal government's underpayment of property taxes and the provincial government's generous transit subsidies for Toronto and surrounding municipalities.
It was a point bolstered by a recent report from the province's Financial Accountability Office, which calculated that Toronto and surrounding regions got a $196.49-per-resident transit subsidy in 2024-25. Ottawa got a mere $31.91
The facts are on Ottawa's side, but the politics are less promising. Both Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau are busy Christmas shopping for votes, spending billions of dollars on sending cheques to all and sundry because of a newfound concern about affordability.
It's vote-buying season and the number of votes to be had by giving Ottawa fairer treatment is limited. If either government cuts a special transit deal for Ottawa, other municipalities will be sticking their hands out too. Well, except Toronto. Its hands are already full.
For the prime minister and the premier, the easiest thing to do about Ottawa's city budget problem is nothing at all. The fact that our city lacks an influential cabinet champion at either level doesn't help.
Cross your fingers, but brace yourself for a 5.9-per-cent tax increase.
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist and author. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com
Related
Adam: Doug Ford needs to step up to fix Ottawa's public transit mess
Pellerin: If Ottawa must force us to drive downtown, at least make it possible to park
