Document 321 of 500

Adam: Doug Ford is outsourcing his family doctor problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/adam-doug-ford-is-outsourcing-his-family-doctor/docview/3123268644/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Adam%3A+Doug+Ford+is+outsourcing+his+family+doctor+problem&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In recent weeks, the Ontario government has made a series of headline-grabbing announcements on fixing the primary-care crisis, but the question remains whether these will make a real difference, or are mere window-dressing for election purposes.
By law, Ontarians must go the polls in June 2026, but Premier Doug Ford won't commit to that date, amid mounting speculation of an election call next spring. This raises questions about the motivation behind the flurry of announcements on primary care, an area where Ford has fallen short since coming into office.
First, Ford tapped former federal health minister Jane Philpott to lead an "action team" to connect every Ontarian to a family doctor or nurse practitioner by 2029. Philpott is an inspired appointment. She is a Liberal, respected dean of Queen's University's medical school, and widely regarded as a leading expert on health care. By taking the job, she insulates Ford from any criticism on the issue in an election campaign - if there is one.
Philpott has five years to complete the task, but if, eventually, Ford doesn't like her prescription, he can reject it, no harm done.
The premier also announced a ban on foreign medical students from Ontario universities from 2026, and limits on out-of-province intake to five per cent of admissions. That policy is a red herring because the family doctor shortage has little to do with foreign or out-of-province student admissions. The government's own figures show that out of the 3,833 students in Ontario's six medical schools in 2023/24, only 10 were international students. That's 0.26 per cent of the total, and yet, this is a government priority.
There were 473 out-of-province students, about 12 per cent of total enrolment, and Ford's plan will cut this to five per cent. According to Western University's Schulich School of Medicine and Dentistry, six per cent of this year's class - or 11 out of 190 students - are from out-of-province or international schools. Which says that this is not the central problem.
More significantly perhaps, the government is setting aside $88 million to pay tuition and other costs for 1,360 undergraduate students who commit to become family doctors. The grant, which also begins in 2026, could potentially connect 1.36 million Ontarians to family doctors. But, if students buy in, it would be 2032 before the policy bears fruit, because it takes at least six years to become a family doctor.
Encouraging more students to become family doctors is a good thing, but it is far from the solution. The Ontario College of Family Physicians says the province needs 2,500 family doctors to fix the problem, and what's needed is an answer to why doctors are burning out and leaving, and why medical students are not rushing to fill their shoes.
The main problem is that the practice itself has become unsustainable. High overhead costs, red tape and administrative chores that bury doctors in paperwork instead of caring for patients, plus a lack of a central referral system, are among things that contribute to doctor burnout. Obsessing over a non-existent problem with international students, or fixating on out-of-province enrolment that has marginal impact, won't solve the doctor shortage.
Make family practice sustainable and you have a healthy system that makes doctors want to stay, and incentivizes medical students to get in and make a life of it. There really is no point in encouraging students to become family doctors if they are going to find the practice is broken.
No question Ford has spent billions on health care, but despite his best efforts, the primary care crisis has defied a solution. The family-doctor shortage, which is at the heart of the crisis, has gotten worse, not better, under Ford's watch. Four years ago, 1.8 million Ontarians had no family doctor. Today, the figure is 2.5 million
This is Ford's never-ending headache. Now that the premier has contracted-out the problem to Philpott, we can only hope she succeeds, for the sake of those 2.5 million Ontarians.
Mohammed Adam is an Ottawa journalist and commentator. Reach him at nylamiles48@gmail.com
Correction:
Information about the students applying to the Schulich School of Medicine and Dentistry has been corrected. All applicants to the Schulich program must be Canadian citizens or permanent residents of Canada.
Related
Adam: Here's what the pay of an Ontario family doctor looks like
Adam: Can nurse practitioners fill the family doctor gap in Ontario?

Document 322 of 500

Kot: Doug Ford's $3-billion tax rebate should have gone to education

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kot-doug-fords-3-billion-tax-rebate-should-have/docview/3123213902/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kot%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+%243-billion+tax+rebate+should+have+gone+to+education&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What difference will Premier Doug Ford's $200 cheque make to me or to most Ontarians? While it will help to buy a few bags of groceries, there will be no long-lasting effects for the betterment of our society.
The Ontario government plans to give 15 million Ontarians $200 each, which will cost the province $3 billion. Imagine how much good $3 billion could do, if spent carefully, by a thoughtful, wise, compassionate government.
I will leave it to others to comment on the health-care system and other worthy areas of society that could use that large infusion of cash. My concern is for the education system, as I am a retired special education teacher and we have learning disabled people in our family.
Recent conversations with parents and teachers leave me saddened at the lack of progress in serving special education students in this province. The situation is getting worse, not better. It is so serious that the Ontario Human Rights Commission launched the Right to Read Inquiry, with its report issued in February of 2022.
In 1988, the former Ottawa Board of Education had two classes for learning disabled students in every elementary school. There was a limit: only eight students per class and those lucky eight had a special education teacher and an educational assistant.
Those classes are long gone. LD students, along with other exceptionalities - those with autism, behavioural, vision and hearing challenges, just to name a few - are all in regular classrooms. Somehow, classroom teachers are expected to attend to all their needs, all these exceptionalities, while covering a more complex curriculum. It is a herculean task.
If I had the $3 billion to spend, I would spend it on salaries in the education system. First, I'd put a cap on class sizes, no more than 20 students. That would mean hiring a lot more teachers but with only 20 students in the classroom, a teacher could really give them more individual attention.
Those teachers would still need help, so I would hire hundreds, maybe thousands, of educational assistants. EAs are essential to care for today's diverse student population. Of course, the salaries of EAs should be dramatically increased. Why do we underpay those whose services are so essential to our society's well-being: EAs, personal service workers and daycare workers - ah, but I digress.
There are other professionals whom all school boards need to hire. Struggling students need to have a psycho educational assessment, done by a school psychologist so that an accurate picture of their abilities is known. The wait times for students to be seen and assessed by a school psychologist go on for years. As a spec-ed teacher, I've often played the role of God, as I juggled kids' names on that list. The sooner children are assessed, the sooner they should be able to receive services necessary for their success. Some of the professionals needed to deliver those services are speech therapists, occupational therapists and social workers.
If this government spent $3 billion lowering class sizes and hiring classroom teachers, special education teachers, educational assistants, psychologists, social workers, occupational therapists and speech therapists, that would go a long way to ensuring long-lasting positive outcomes. Premier Ford would really get a bang for his buck.
Our province's children need to be well cared for, right from the start. If they are educated by knowledgeable professionals in small class settings, with the necessary services, they will emerge as confident, capable adults.
Their time in our schools is limited: only 14 years from Junior Kindergarten to Grade 12. How we serve them in that short time has the biggest influence on the types of citizens they will become. As Crosby, Stills, Nash and Young advised, Teach your children well
(Or ... how would you spend $3 billion? )
Mary Ellen Kot is an Ottawa writer and former teacher.
Related
Egan: Ottawa's city government just doesn't matter anymore
Leiper: Doug Ford's bike-lane legislation is about politics, not safety or traffic flow

Subject: Speech therapists; Special education; Students; Special education teachers; Classrooms; Learning disabilities; Social workers; School psychologists; Class size

The premier is outsourcing his doctor problem

Author: Mohammed, Adam

Publication info: Mohammed, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-is-outsourcing-his-doctor-problem/docview/3123198271/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+premier+is+outsourcing+his+doctor+problem&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Mohammed%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Palliative care: there's no place like home; Quality end-of-life care needn't be in hospital, says Dr. Jennifer Brodeur

Author: Brodeur, Jennifer

Publication info: Brodeur, Jennifer. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/palliative-care-theres-no-place-like-home-quality/docview/3123198201/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Palliative+care%3A+there%27s+no+place+like+home%3B+Quality+end-of-life+care+needn%27t+be+in+hospital%2C+says+Dr.+Jennifer+Brodeur&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Brodeur%2C+Jennifer&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "We had no idea this care existed." I hear this a lot from patients and families in my work as a community palliative care physician. While many people have heard of palliative care, most are unaware of the home-based palliative care provided by community palliative care teams.
There is a medical workforce hiding in plain sight in our city: one that provides end-of-life care to people who are dying at home. Physicians, nurses, paramedics, personal support workers, and other health-care providers are working alongside patients and families to optimize patients'care, manage symptoms, provide end-of-life education and support to patients and their caregivers, all outside of a hospital.
While such care may seem like a novel idea, prior to the advent of modern medicine the home was the primary location of death. At the turn of the 20th century, the construction of hospitals moved general medical care out of the home. Developments in medical education, research and technology in the 1900s paved the way for advancements in internal medicine, surgery and critical care. Death, a natural and inevitable event, became regarded as a failure of medicine.
A death-denying culture emerged. Patients would seek hospital care in their last days and hours, and a progressive shift toward hospital deaths occurred. As a result, communities lost the generational knowledge and skills of supporting a natural death at home. The effects of this still exist today. Statistics Canada data from 2021-2022 reveal a staggering 55.7 per cent of deaths in Canada occur in hospitals - a statistic that has remained relatively consistent since before the COVID-19 pandemic. This is despite evidence that the majority of people would prefer a home death.
While hospitals continue to excel at delivering medical interventions focused on prevention, cure and life prolongation, they are not optimal environments for patients with serious incurable illness approaching end-of-life. Furthermore, for people who are survivors of institutional-based trauma, having to receive end-of-life care in a hospital can trigger serious and complicated reactions.
The home setting provides patients with privacy, comfort and connection to loved ones. Skilled community palliative care teams add valuable holistic care that honours the human experience by integrating into a person's world, rather than the other way around. Palliative care providers empower patients and caregivers through education regarding symptoms and their management, as well as provide support and resources to navigate the natural and predictable events that occur during the normal dying process. This collaboration often leads to the achievement of a good death: one that is peaceful, dignified and surrounded by loved ones.
For patients and families experiencing end-of-life at home, palliative care is far from hidden; it is the most important and impactful contact in their daily lives. We need to talk about it openly, so the support and care provided does not come as a surprise when it is needed, but an expectation. Dr. Jennifer Brodeur MD, CCFP PC is the Medical Director of Hospice Care Ottawa. These palliative care physicians also contributed to this article: Dr. Melini Gupta MD, CCFP PC; Dr. Paul Hacker MD; and Dr. Regine Krechowicz MD, CCFP PC.

Subject: Palliative care; Caregivers

Identifier / keyword: existed; patients; families; community; palliative; physician

How does city government even matter anymore?; Ford and the feds now set the rules, says Kelly Egan

Author: Egan, Kelly

Publication info: Egan, Kelly. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-does-city-government-even-matter-anymore-ford/docview/3122824144/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+does+city+government+even+matter+anymore%3F%3B+Ford+and+the+feds+now+set+the+rules%2C+says+Kelly+Egan&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Egan%2C+Kelly&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford wants control over bike lanes in Ontario. He also wants a roughly 55-kilometre tunnel under the city of Toronto, proof that the Rams, the F-150s and the Escalades are running the world.
Two wheels, or four, the premier is wideawake, worried about the places where rubber meets the road. The whole thing is bizarre.
Inasmuch as the premier of Canada's most populous province should have better things to worry about, the bike-lane issue raises an important question.
Do Ontario cities even matter anymore? Does it really matter who the mayor is, what the council is up to, who votes how? The contrary evidence is staring at us. LeBreton Flats. This is a vacant, dead-eyed, federally owned property - possibly proof God hates us - and likely the site of a new hockey arena. This is a transaction between the senior level of government and a private corporation. The city's role? It may toss in some free water pipes and nice curbs. Point is, the city is not in the driver's seat, only the back of the bus.
The new Ottawa Hospital. The city had hardly any role in choosing the location, right by Dow's Lake. Have you driven by the new parking garage? You can see it from Mars. In any case, the location was finalized after - well, let's just call it dipsy-doodling - by two federal cabinet ministers from opposing parties. It is becoming clearer with each passing month that the location is wrong. The federal government is, lately, unloading real estate as if it were the last hour of the Glebe garage sale. So build this mammoth thing on lovely green space, while the likes of Tunney's Pasture sits there aging, or rusting, often brutally desolate. People, this is our city and our hospital. And yet a weird clutch of "insiders" told us where to put it. Transit. Well, one hardly knows where to start. The city is facing a shortfall of $120 million to keep a lousy system running. Of course, we don't know how to fix this, so go cap in hand to senior levels of government. It has probably escaped notice the federal government is running a $46-billion deficit this year. Sure, it has lots of money to help OC Transpo. The insanity of it all, really.
As for Phase 2 of LRT, I have never seen the city so dug up, wounded and scarred for a service that may not get used much, will be over budget, late on arrival, and, only on lucky days, on time. (And Highway 417, also Ontario's doing.)
In your neighbourhood, like mine, people are talking about this new zoning regime. It was pretty much rammed down our throats by Ontario. The single house next door may become a fourplex, probably without a bike lane, but maybe with a nine-lane subterranean tunnel for cars. It only reminds us who is in charge. Not Ottawa. We don't seem to matter.
Our education system is pretty much provincially dictated and much of the news from "local" school boards involves trustees attacking each other. Is there meaningful local input to education anymore? The police force. Governed by the Ontario Police Services Act, the OPS proudly announced access to an Ontario-funded helicopter. You can't find an officer to attend your break-in, but a helicopter? Garbage and recycling also have a strong provincial influence, as does hydro.
Lansdowne Park? The city outsourced its soul to the private sector, didn't it? And is at it again. All of which is to say city government, especially ours, feels more and more irrelevant.
I couldn't honestly tell you what the goals of this council are, except creating a "safe, reliable" environment, which is a meaningless stupidity. They don't dream or create, they just "manage," and not especially well.
The City of Ottawa has a $4.6-billion annual budget, 17,000 or so employees, and an annoying penchant for whining for more money while begging others to fix our problems.
Dear Ottawa: Do one great thing, just one, so that, as a city, we still matter. Kelly Egan is a retired city columnist.

Identifier / keyword: premier; control; lanes; ontario; roughly; tunnel

Today's letters: Leaf blowers are efficient, not evil

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-leaf-blowers-are-efficient-not/docview/3122454237/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Leaf+blowers+are+efficient%2C+not+evil&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When leaf blowers are a good idea
Re: Deachman: Leaf blowers - the autumn assault on Ottawa's eardrums is well underway, Oct. 23.
I enjoyed Bruce Deachman's opinion columns most of the time, like the one on using goats to manage invasive plant species in our green spaces. But last week, writing on leaf blowers, he just got my goat (excuse the pun).
Managing property an acre in size, in a "forested" community, requires a lot of effort in the fall as the leaves invade my property from all around. Like most people, we value our time and look for ways to do things in the most effective, timely way.
I am an owner of a 800cc leaf blower with a maximum air velocity of 239 mph. It moves not only leaves but branches and zillions of acorns. I could go electric with a handheld blower at 145 mph that has a very high-pitched whining sound and "spend days bothering my neighbours with noise" as it ineffectively moves leaves a couple of feet with each pass. I chose to buy a blower that gets the job effectively done in a few hours.
You can purchase attachments to also safely blow the leaves out of the gutters, without getting on a slippery roof.
As a senior, my time is precious so it is a win-win for the mighty leaf blowers when purchased to get the job done in a timely safe way so I can enjoy the peace and quiet of our beautifully forested neighbour.
Jim Houston, Woodlawn
Don't ban foreign medical students
I compliment Premier Doug Ford for his efforts to increase the number of family doctors in Ontario by introducing the "Learn and Stay" program designed to reduce the financial burden on Ontario medical students and encourage them, upon graduation, to set up practice in Ontario, which is woefully short of family doctors.
However, I am less sanguine about the premier's plan to ban international students from admission to Ontario medical schools. In 2023-24, about 3,800 students were registered in Ontario medical schools. Of that number, only 10 were international students, hardly a tsunami unfairly denying a large number of Ontario students a seat in the classroom. In light of these numbers, the banning of international students from Ontario medical schools sails, perhaps unwittingly, very close to the borders of xenophobia.
Douglas Parker, Ottawa
We need civility in our Parliament
One of the hallmarks of a well-functioning democracy is civility and constructive dialogue. If name-calling, lies and excessive partisanship are accepted, we all lose.
Name-calling, lies and unfounded slogans (although popular with some) do nothing to inform the public on important issues. Such behaviour can also drag others into similar conduct and further degrade discussion.
Just look at what is happening south of the border. It's time to call our elected officials to account and restore a well functioning Parliament.
Michael Wiggin, Ottawa

Don't ban foreign medical students

Document 329 of 500

Today's letters: Limiting protest in Ottawa - there is a middle path

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-limiting-protest-ottawa-there-is/docview/3121881015/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Limiting+protest+in+Ottawa+-+there+is+a+middle+path&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's how 'bubble zones' can work for all
Re: Council must be cautious about limiting right to protest, Oct. 24; and A 'bubble bylaw' would erode our essential rights, Oct. 25.
Two of my favourite Ottawa Citizen columnists have expressed serious concerns about a proposed bylaw to prevent protests around certain institutions by banning demonstrations within a designated protective "bubble." In defence of the proposed bylaw, Mayor Sutcliffe wrote, "No one should be threatened or intimidated when attending their place of worship, school community centre or any other gathering."
Mohammed Adam responded with, "The right of assembly is essential to a functioning democracy because it embodies free expression and association." Bruce Deachman wrote, "Freedom of belief and expression, and peaceful protest, are enshrined in the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms."
Strangely, I agree with all three statements. People who are not violating the rights of others should not feel threatened or intimidated anyplace at any time. The right to express and explain my disagreement with policies or actions is one that I treasure.
Fortunately, a compromise bylaw that protects everyone's rights is possible. Protests within the bubble should be allowed but limited to five people who are not allowed to threaten anyone but are otherwise free to express their opinions. A larger protest gathering should be permitted nearby but outside the bubble. Electronic communication between the groups should be allowed so that all protesters can hear what is being said within the bubble. Individuals should be allowed to "rotate" between the groups providing that the bubble never has more than five protesters.
In this way, the right to protest can be preserved without anyone feeling physically intimidated.
Dave Parnas, Ottawa
Keep protesters away from school children
I believe that a bubble bylaw is a good thing when it concerns schools. School children should not be taken hostage by whatever group for whatever reason, over whatever is the conflict of the day.
Children go to school to learn and civics lessons are important. But those lessons should take place in an organized setting. Groups of demonstrators representing various opinions and shouting at each other are not an organized setting.
Such a situation would be particularly difficult in certain schools where children may have come to Canada from different parts of the world where conflicts are the norm. Their families may have come to Canada to get away from conflict and give their children a better future. Children should never be asked to take sides in whatever conflict is the flavour of the day.
Let children remain children as long as they can. This is not an attack on anyone's essential rights.
Andrée Champagne, Ottawa
A further harm from loud leaf blowers
Re: Leaf blowers - the autumn assault on Ottawa's eardrums is well underway, Oct. 23.
Thank you for your timely article. One aspect not mentioned is that while the noise of a leaf blower may be an annoyance to most, it is much more than that to people with hearing sensitivities. My nephew is autistic and this noise is frightening and painful to him. And all for a "tidy" landscape?
Sandy Smallwood, Ottawa

Today's letters: Ottawa is as arbitrary about bike lanes as Doug Ford is

Document 330 of 500

Reading the voter tea leaves; Elections in n.b.,saskatchewan show poilievre should keep laser focus on affordability, avoid culture wars

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reading-voter-tea-leaves-elections-n-b/docview/3121784063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reading+the+voter+tea+leaves%3B+Elections+in+n.b.%2Csaskatchewan+show+poilievre+should+keep+laser+focus+on+affordability%2C+avoid+culture+wars&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conser vative Leader Pierre Poilievre has likely kept a close eye on this month's provincial election races in New Brunswick and Saskatchewan, where hot-button culture war issues like gender identity in schools haven't appeared to move the needle with voters.
But political strategists say it's incumbent fatigue, not disagreements over pronouns and single-sex spaces, that has weighed down conservative campaigns in both provinces.
"It's tough for incumbents right across Canada right now," said Conservative strategist Fred DeLorey.
DeLorey, who ran the last federal Conservative campaign in 2021, said he doesn't think that the decisive loss of the Blaine Higgs-led New Brunswick Progressive Conservatives last Monday will slow Poilievre's growing momentum in Atlantic Canada.
"We're comparing apples and pineapples," said DeLorey.
"Poilievre is hitting it out of the park right now, and his main message is affordability ... There's no cautionary tale for him (from Higgs's loss) other than that he's on the right track and shouldn't leave that track."
Voters in Saskatchewan seemed more interested in change than culture wars, with 55 per cent saying it's time for a new provincial government last week.
They went to the polls on Monday; the results were not in by press time.
The governing Saskatchewan Party has been in power for 17 years, by far the longest tenure of any government in Canada.
Saskatchewan Party Leader Scott Moe spent the campaign's home stretch on defence after stumbling into a messy scandal involving the "outing" of two children of an NDP candidate following a changeroom incident in greater Regina.
He brought up the incident during an impromptu mid-campaign announcement in which he said he'd immediately issue a directive banning "biological boys" from girls'school change rooms if re-elected - a promise that wasn't included in the Saskatchewan Party's platform. Moe says he wasn't aware of the children's identities at the time.
Saskatoon-based pollster Lang Mc-Gilp said it wasn't a good use of Moe's time heading into the closer-than-usual election.
"The top four issues of the election have stayed pretty consistent: health care, affordability, taxes and education," said McGilp.
"You can debate whether (gender identity issues) fit in any of these boxes, but there's a lot of other stuff that's more top of mind with voters."
New Brunswick and Saskatchewan are the only two provinces with formal parental notification and consent policies on the books. Alberta is expected to become the third later this year.
Higgs sparked a national debate last year with his resolute defence of controversial revisions to New Brunswick's Sexual Orientation and Gender Identity policy, popularly known as Policy 713. Higgs said last summer that he was willing to call an early election over the changes, which established the first provincewide parental notification and consent requirements in Canada for student name and pronoun changes in K-12 schools.
Polling at the time showed strong support, in Atlantic Canada and beyond, for parental notification requirements, with Canadians more ambivalent on the question of parental consent.
Before the year was out, Moe would adopt similar guidelines, upping the ante by pre-emptively using the notwithstanding clause to push through his so-called Parents'Bill of Rights.
Poilievre himself trod lightly on the matter, declining to comment directly on Policy 713, but later told an ethnic media outlet that he believed "parental rights (should) come before the government's rights."
Jon Robinson, a progressive strategist based in greater Saint John, N.B., said that the split in the Progressive Conservative caucus over the changes to Policy 713 likely hurt Higgs more than the changes themselves.
"This became a very personal issue for (Higgs)," said Robinson, noting that Higgs stayed highly involved in his local church while premier and was largely aligned with the province's Christian right on the issue.
Robinson said Higgs's unwillingness to compromise on the contentious revisions reinforced already negative perceptions of his "autocratic" leadership style.
"Higgs was cast as someone where it's his way or the highway," said Robinson. "And this stuck with him."
Two members of Higgs's cabinet resigned, and two more were demoted, amid party infighting.
Robinson added that controversy surrounding the nomination of Christian TV host Faytene Grasseschi to run for the Progressive Conservatives in a safe Saint John area riding exacerbated internal divisions heading into the election.
(Grasseschi narrowly lost to ex-Progressive Conservatives MLA John Herron, who ran as a Liberal.)
DeLorey said that a wave of resignations and retirements among incumbent Progressive Conservative MLAs further stacked the deck against Higgs.
"We have much smaller seats in the Maritimes than you do in the rest of the country," said DeLorey, who grew up in neighbouring Nova Scotia. "People get to know their local MLAs quite well.
"So when you have so many incumbents who don't re-offer, that's a huge blow to the campaign from the very start."
DeLorey said it would be unfair to pin the high turnover on Higgs's leadership style, noting that the veteran premier was going for an almost unheard of third term in a province with a long history of political swings.
"It's a major achievement to win two terms in New Brunswick, let alone three."
He added Liberal leader and premier-designate Susan Holt's lack of a personal connection to Justin Trudeau gave her a boost by allowing her to credibly distance herself from the toxic Trudeau brand, something her counterpart Zach Churchill will have a harder time doing when Nova Scotians head to the polls next month.
National Post rmohamed@postmedia.com

Subject: Parental rights; Provincial elections; Conservatism; Leadership; Election results; Negative campaigning; Incumbency; Voters; Gender identity; Consent

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: conser; vative; leader; pierre; poilievre; close

Document 331 of 500

City ER patients face longer waits; Provincial statistics show access to emergency care is suffering

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-er-patients-face-longer-waits-provincial/docview/3121783996/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+ER+patients+face+longer+waits%3B+Provincial+statistics+show+access+to+emergency+care+is+suffering&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa residents wait longer than most people in Ontario for emergency care, according to recent provincial statistics.
Waits to see a physician and get treatment are above the provincial average at every hospital in the city.
At Queensway Carleton Hospital, patients are waiting twice as long as the provincial average for an initial assessment by a doctor, according to recent provincial statistics.
On average, Ontario residents wait two hours in emergency departments before receiving initial assessments by physicians, according to Health Quality Ontario, the agency that monitors the province's health operations.
But that is far from the whole story.
Overall stays in emergency are much longer. On average, non-emergency patients spend a total of 3.2 hours in the emergency department and high-urgency patients spend an average of 4.7 hours in emergency.
Patients waiting to be admitted to hospital are there for significantly longer: an average of 19.2 hours, and just 27 per cent of them within the provincial target of eight hours.
Those patients are frequently waiting in hallways or cubicles on stretchers meant for short-term use, something that leading Ontario geriatrician Dr. Samir Sinha compared to torture, especially for the elderly who make up a high number of patients waiting to be admitted to hospitals.
At the General campus of The Ottawa Hospital, CHEO and the Montfort, patients wait an average of 2.7 hours for initial assessments by physicians. At the Civic campus of TOH, patients wait 2.8 hours for initial assessment, on average.
At Queensway Carleton, the average wait for an initial assessment is four hours, or double the provincial average. That is the third-longest wait time across Ontario.
The longest wait times in the province - as of the end of August - were at Hawkesbury and District General Hospital, east of Ottawa.
In other measures, Queensway Carleton Hospital fares better compared to other Ottawa hospitals.
It takes longer for high-urgency patients to get treated at The Ottawa Hospital and Montfort, for example, and QCH, at 18.3 hours, is below the provincial average of 19.2 hours for patients to get admitted to hospital from emergency. The waits are longer at other Ottawa hospitals.
Those waits drew the attention of Premier Doug Ford this past week. During a news conference, Ford partially blamed the problem on too many patients going to emergency departments for non-emergency issues.
"The emergency department is for emergencies, not little Johnny cutting his leg or so on and so forth," Ford was quoted as saying. He also said emergency doctors he talked to estimated that 50 per cent of people shouldn't be in the emergency department.
His comment drew immediate pushback from physicians and doctors'organizations, who said they misrepresented the problem by blaming it on patients and could discourage some from seeking the medical care they needed.
The Ontario Medical Association called on the province to focus on increasing access to doctors, not limiting it.
"Ontario's emergency departments are there to provide care when it's needed most, and patients should never feel discouraged from seeking help when and where they need it," the OMA said in a statement.
Perth emergency physician Dr.
Alan Drummond was co-author of a position statement from the Canadian Association of Emergency Physicians that found, among other things, emergency department overcrowding was largely due to lack of access to hospital beds.
"Contrary to popular perceptions, ED overcrowding is not caused by inappropriate use of emergency departments or by high numbers of lower acuity patients presenting to the ED; the inability of admitted patients to access in-patient beds from the ED is the most significant factor causing emergency department overcrowding in Canadian hospitals," the statement said.
On Saturday, Drummond said it was frustrating to hear Ford focus blame on patients after years of research that had shown hospital capacity was the main driver of crowded emergency departments, not patient overuse.
"There has been a lot of work internationally and in Ontario looking at the issue of crowding and it has been largely disproven," Drummond said. "It is sad to see governments continue to focus on the myths and put all of their efforts into trying to blame and shame patients. Until we get our heads around the fact that we need to reduce hospital capacity to a safe occupancy level of 85 per cent, we will continue to deal with this."
Ontario has the second-lowest rate of hospital beds per population in Canada.
Emergency department officials at Queensway Carleton acknowledged this past week that patients were waiting too long for care and said they were working on improving efficiency in the busy department to help reduce those wait times.
"There are many factors that cause our wait times to be long, too many to count," said Dr. Adam Nicholson, chief and medical director of emergency services at the hospital. "In the end, unfortunately, our patients are waiting too long to be seen, they are the ones to suffer."
They urged patients to continue to come to the hospital when they needed to and emphasized that the sickest patients would get seen first.
"We understand how frustrating it can be for our patients and families," said Paula Archambault, the hospital's clinical director of critical care, emergency and mental health.
"If a patient feels they need to come to the emergency, they should come. We take their concerns seriously and are proud of Queensway Carleton Hospital and how much patient satisfaction we have recorded. It is a huge point of pride and we don't want to see that change."
She said the hospital had launched a project to significantly reduce the amount of time patients waited to be admitted to in-patient beds. That wait time has improved by 7.7 hours over last year, Archambault said.
The hospital is also working on projects to improve the amount of time until patients have initial assessments by changing the way patients flow through the emergency department.
Queensway Carleton has seen a recent spike in the complexity and acuity of patients.
"Certainly that is a factor in the wait times," Nicholson said. "When patients are more complex, it takes longer."

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Departments; Patient satisfaction; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals Physicians

Ontario aiming to become clean energy superpower; Minister says province well-positioned to scale up system to boost jobs, revenue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-aiming-become-clean-energy-superpower/docview/3123777847/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+aiming+to+become+clean+energy+superpower%3B+Minister+says+province+well-positioned+to+scale+up+system+to+boost+jobs%2C+revenue&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's energy minister wants the province's electricity minister wants the province's electricity system to not just be able to meet an expected 75 per cent increase in demand, but to exceed it and be able to sell excess power to other jurisdictions, promising details in a forthcoming energy plan.
Stephen Lecce released a document last week that outlines his vision for a clean, reliable and affordable grid, as well as how to integrate electricity planning with other aspects of the energy system.
The release follows an announcement from the Independent Electricity System Operator, which said that demand is increasing faster than previously anticipated and is set to grow by 75 per cent leading up to 2050.
The Ministry of Energy and Electrification document says the province will continue prioritizing nuclear and hydroelectric generation for baseload power, but that Ontario also needs natural gas generation for reliability as it is more able to respond to peak demands.
In 2021, the electricity system was 94 per cent emissions-free, and that is now down to 87 per cent as the province relies more on natural gas generation and nuclear units undergo refurbishment - but the ministry says that natural gas will help reduce emissions in the province overall by supporting broader electrification.
Ontario's grid is one of the cleanest in the world, and the province can use that to its advantage to become an energy superpower, Lecce said.
"Our priority is obviously first and foremost securing affordable energy for the people of Ontario," he said in an interview.
"But yes, we do believe Ontario is well-positioned. We already are a net exporter of clean energy into the U.S. We want to scale that up with a focus on delivering jobs and revenue back to Ontarians."
Ontario's electricity system has ties with Manitoba, Quebec, Minnesota, Michigan and New York, and Ontario has been a net exporter to them since 2006, the document said, but that energy is sometimes sold at a loss.
Now, the IESO is developing an export strategy to generate new revenue streams.
"Many of Ontario's interconnected jurisdictions have an anticipated shortfall or a clean energy commitment to meet (i.e. New York, Maryland and Illinois) or both (i.e. Michigan and Minnesota) but are currently reliant on resources like coal, which could be replaced with clean energy imports," the ministry document said. Ontario can also become a leading exporter of energy technology, Lecce said.
When it comes to expanding electricity generation to meet domestic demand, the province is already undertaking a large procurement for new resources, and is planning four small modular nuclear reactors as well as looking into a new, large-scale nuclear plant.
Ontario will need to do more, Lecce said, though he did not divulge any specific projects.
"My commitment is to build that plan using the lowest-price options, competitive procurements and making affordability the No. 1 priority," he said.
Advocacy group Environmental Defence said it welcomes the announcement of a "plan to plan" after the Progressive Conservatives years ago removed a requirement for the province to issue regular long-term energy plans. The last one was published in 2017. The Canadian Press

Subject: Energy; Electricity; Clean technology; Natural gas

Location: New York; United States--US; Michigan; Minnesota

Egan: Ottawa's city government just doesn't matter anymore

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/egan-ottawas-city-government-just-doesnt-matter/docview/3121579315/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Egan%3A+Ottawa%27s+city+government+just+doesn%27t+matter+anymore&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford wants control over bike lanes in Ontario. He also wants a 55-kilometre-ish tunnel under the city of Toronto, proof that the Rams, the F-150s, the Escalades are now running the world.
Two wheels, or four, the premier is wide awake, worried about the places where rubber meets the road. The whole thing is bizarre.
Inasmuch as the premier of Canada's most populous province should have better things to worry about, the bike-lane issue raises an important question.
Do Ontario cities even matter anymore? Does it really matter who the mayor is, what your local councillor is up to, who votes how?
The contrary evidence is staring at us.
LeBreton Flats. This is a vacant, dead-eyed, federally owned property - possibly proof God hates us - and likely the site of a new hockey arena. This is a transaction between the senior level of government and a private corporation. The city's role? It may toss in some free water pipes and nice curbs. Point is, the city is not in the driver's seat, only the back of the bus.
The new Ottawa Hospital. The city had hardly any role in choosing the location, right by Dow's Lake. Have you driven by the new parking garage? You can see it from Mars. In any case, the location was finalized after - well, let's just call it dipsy-doodling - by two federal cabinet ministers, from opposing parties. It is becoming clearer with each passing month that the location is wrong. The feds are, lately, unloading real estate as if it were the last hour of the Glebe garage sale. So build this mammoth thing on lovely green space, while the likes of Tunney's Pasture sits there aging, or rusting, often brutally desolate. People, this is OUR city, OUR hospital. And yet a weird clutch of "insiders" told us where to put it. Arggh ...
Transit. Well, one hardly knows where to start. The city is facing a shortfall of $120 million to keep a lousy system running. We, of course, don't know how to fix this, so go cap in hand to senior levels of government. It has probably escaped notice the federal government is running a $46-billion deficit this year. Sure, it has lots of money to help OC Transpo. The insanity of it all, really.
As for Phase 2 of LRT, I have never seen the city so dug up, wounded and scarred for a service that may not get used much, will be over budget, late on arrival, and, only on lucky days, on time. (And Highway 417, also Ontario's doing, good Lord ... )
In your neighbourhood, like mine, people are talking about this new zoning regime. It was pretty much rammed down our throats by Ontario. The single house next door might become a fourplex, probably without a bike lane, but maybe with a nine-lane subterranean tunnel for cars. It only reminds us who is in charge. Not Ottawa. We don't seem to matter.
Our education system is pretty much provincially dictated and much of the news from "local" school boards involves trustees attacking each other. Is there meaningful local input to education anymore?
The police force. Governed by the Ontario Police Services Act, the OPS proudly announced access to an Ontario-funded helicopter. You can't find a cop to attend your break-in, but a helicopter?
Garbage and recycling also have a strong provincial influence, as does hydro.
Lansdowne Park? The city outsourced its soul to the private sector, didn't it? And is at it again. All of which is to say city government, especially ours, feels more and more irrelevant.
I couldn't honestly tell you what the goals of this council are, except creating a "safe, reliable" environment, which is a meaningless stupidity. They don't dream or create, they just "manage," and not especially well.
The City of Ottawa has a $4.6-billion annual budget, 17,000 or so employees, and an annoying penchant for whining for more money while begging others to fix our problems.
Dear Ottawa: Do one great thing, just one, so that, as a city, we still matter.
Kelly Egan is a retired city columnist, busily shaking his cane at passing clouds. kellyegan197@gmail.com

Today's letters: Smartphones required for parking? That's just dumb

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-smartphones-required-parking-thats/docview/3121484207/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Smartphones+required+for+parking%3F+That%27s+just+dumb&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Parks Canada shouldn't kowtow to cellphone companies
Re: No credit card? No smartphone? No parking: Parks Canada ditches cash option at Rideau Canal lots, Oct. 22.
I'm no Luddite. But I find it irksome when some organization obliges me to change technologies, not because it is better for me, but because it is convenient for them in some manner, or improves their bottom line.
I eschew cellphones. Not because I can't afford one, but because they induce bad habits, impulsiveness, and lead to ignorance of one's surroundings. I don't feel compelled to photograph everything, and I have a phone at home.
I also have plenty of loose change in my pocket to put into parking meters.
I've run into several circumstances where parking requires payment, or at least registration, by phone. Obliging me to carry a cellphone because it makes YOUR business easier or more profitable is quite simply bullying. That access to public parks should be limited to those willing to pay for cellphones is shameful, and kowtowing to telecomm companies.
It's ironic that we demand businesses be accessible to those reliant on assistive devices, yet at the same time we are so compliant when they demand we adopt the devices they find
economical for themselves.
Mark Hammer, Ottawa
Just give this man a parking pass
Can't Parks Canada use some common sense and issue senior Chris Nicolas a lifetime parking pass? Precedent? So what - figure it out.
Many seniors enjoy these parks, but not the hassle of smartphones or pay stations.
Janet McGuire, Ottawa
Manotick truck ban sets the right example
Re: Trucks banned on Manotick Main Street by City of Ottawa, Oct. 22.
Congratulations to Manotick for convincing the city to put an end to the truck route running down the main street of your village. The downtown Ottawa residents along Rideau Street and King Edward Avenue have been trying to do the same for about four decades.
There are on the order of 800 trucks an hour running past my front door. The long-proposed bypass truck tunnel has passed the feasibility study and I believe the environmental assessment as well. Now, we just need Premier Doug Ford to peel off a couple of billion from his Highway 401 tunnel project to finally put an end to the downtown truck madness.
Steve White, Ottawa
Ban those trucks in downtown core
One can only hope that the good citizens of Manotick have led the way for Ottawa Council to see the light and enact a similar ban on trucks rumbling through Ottawa's downtown core on their way to other cities.
Courage, Mayor Sutcliffe. Do the right thing.
Peter Sharp, Ottawa
Limiting protests: where do we stop?
Re: Deachman: Ottawa council must be cautious about limiting protests, Oct. 18.
I like Bruce Deachman's common-sense approach about protest-free "bubbles" around many sensitive centres. He raises obvious questions about which places should be so designated and under what criteria.
Designating abortion centres as protest-free bubble zones opened up this idea for other places. But where do you stop now? Surely peaceful protesters should be allowed everywhere, otherwise we are not that different from dictatorships whose governments decide what can be protested, where and when.
Democracies are messy because of the plurality of opinions but that is the price of freedom, including freedom of expression, especially voiced by minorities. Limiting the scope of protesters is a first step to shutting them down, and in the long run may curtail the democratic progress we say we cherish.
Let's not forget that not so long ago, women were not allowed to vote and only their vigorous, persistent and not-always peaceful protests changed that. There are numerous examples of fruitful progressive outcomes arising from protest movements. These lessons should not be lost on our civic leaders contemplating "bubbles."
Rafal Pomian, Ottawa
Resist this move to authoritarianism
Thank you for Bruce Deachman's article making an excellent case as to why Ottawa Council (and the public) must think carefully about any move to limit peaceful protests near institutions such as churches, schools etc.
My husband and I are 88 and are active in the pro-Palestinian marches for Gaza (and now Lebanon). It is incomprehensible that our municipal government and some media often paint these protest marches as hateful, violent and dangerous.
I see the move to curtail the right to peaceful protest as a move towards authoritarianism, and we must stand firm against such dangerous moves.
In another article in the Citizen, uOttawa Prof. Joel Westheimer argued for our education system to help students understand the significance of social movements, how the public can organize to take collective action to work for change. And what an inspiring quote by renowned educator John Dewey: "Democracy must be born anew with every generation, and education is its midwife."
Fran Schiller, Ottawa
'Bubble zones' don't prohibit protest
I am a big fan of Bruce Deachman. But he gets it wrong in this article. Mayor Mark Sutcliffe and Coun. Allan Hubley have it right.
Creating space between demonstrators and the recipients of their protest does not prohibit expressions of dissent and peaceful protest. This "bubble bylaw" will not stop hateful language, megaphones, drums, blocking traffic or any other annoyances to others. What can be wrong with demonstrating on the other side of the street? How does this undercut the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms?
What about my right to safety? Deachman asks how far away would be far enough? How about: out of my face.
Evelyn Greenberg, Ottawa
Deachman displays a double standard
After reading Bruce Deachman's most recent two columns - one on bubble zones, the other on leaf blowers - I have come to the following conclusion: If I walk down his street with a megaphone, yelling such things as "Death to Canada" or "F- Trudeau", then that will be OK, as long as I don't employ a leaf blower.
Give your head a shake, sir.
Todd Campbell, Ottawa
Students taught only one approach
Re: Westheimer, Keeping students from political events undercuts their civic education, Oct. 23.
Most of us would agree with Joel Westheimer when he says that education should be about critical thinking and learning "how to think, not what to think." I went into education 25 years ago with that Dewey-inspired belief as well.
However, it is common knowledge now that a puritanical, illiberal and anti-western "progressive" ideology has in fact pushed a specific agenda in our public schools, our universities and our public institutions. They are teaching our students to believe there are only righteous oppressed people and evil oppressors, and our students are taking up the cause.
Perhaps our children have indeed been taught to be engaged citizens, but I would argue they are not being taught to be thoughtful and reflective.
Mike Sternberg, Ottawa
Trillium Line isn't environmentally friendly
Re: Trillium Line passes trial run with 99.5 per cent score, Amilcar announces 'with humility and great joy,' Oct. 21.
OC Transpo may well be proud of its success in bringing its new Trillium Line through the trial period. But wait a moment.
It's not a new Light Rail Train, it's a Heavy Rail Train. That's the kind of rails and motive power used in Canada for a century: diesel engines burning fossil fuel, emitting carbon dioxide and other pollutants into the atmosphere.
This HRT does nothing to contribute to Ottawa's ambition to cut its emissions of carbon dioxide. Au contraire, it will add to them. The only possible benefit may be that it will be sufficiently reliable, unlike the current LRT, to persuade a few citizens to leave their personal vehicles at home.
John Hollins, Gloucester
Where bike-path planning goes askew
Re: This new Ottawa bike lane comes with a pole in the middle, but why is it there? Oct. 21.
OK, city planners had an excuse for that pole in a bike lane; but they must have used trained squirrels when designing the bike lane heading south on Longfields Drive.
A wide bike lane suddenly disappears at Chapman Mills Drive as a curb with pole and crosswalk button juts out into the road. The path indicated for bicycles is to suddenly swerve to the right and mix with any pedestrians that are waiting to cross.
The bike path resumes with a sharp jog in the curb that the snow plow can't follow, leaving feet of snow in winter and a residue of thick sand and mud that still persists.
Brian Schacht, Manotick
The invasive phragmites plants at Carleton University in Ottawa are threatening natural species.
The real threat to 'Dow's Great Swamp'
Re: Invasive species could destroy 'Dow's Great Swamp,' Oct. 17.
The article is yet another in a series scaring us about the looming environmental catastrophe from purple loosestrife, zebra mussels or wild parsnip.
From the photograph that accompanied this article, it seems patently obvious to me that the greatest environmental threat comes from asphalt, concrete and the car.
Geraint Lewis, Ottawa
Trudeau masters the art of understatement
Re: Trudeau says drastic immigration cuts are because 'we didn't get the balance quite right,' Oct. 24.
If there were an award for understatement, the prime minister would receive it for this gem on immigration levels: "We didn't get the balance quite right."
Of course they didn't, because his government never consulted with experts on immigration, food banks, homeless shelters, health-care services, housing, schools, etc. before it flung the borders wide open to anyone wanting to come here.
How long did it take to finally close Roxham Road? Our prime minister refuses to listen to others, including his own caucus and the public, to the detriment of this country.
No one will forget this other gem from the prime minister: "A Canadian is a Canadian is a Canadian." Until they aren't, apparently. Too little, far too late; the horse has long left the barn, and taken voters' trust with it.
Judy Warren, Ottawa
How to fire a prime minister
Observers have pointed out that the federal Liberal party has no mechanism in place to oust its leader.
Some may recall that John Diefenbaker lost his job as Tory prime minister due to a cabinet revolt, not through a formal process. However, I guess the current crop of Liberal ministers is willing to risk a fate similar to that experienced by the Ontario Liberals under Kathleen Wynne, who led her MPPs like lemmings over an electoral cliff.
Geoff King, Ottawa
Yes, of course we're all worse off
There are many valid reasons to vote out incumbent governments given our frustration with their handling of the unprecedented challenges they have all faced over the past few years. But answering "Yes" to the question of whether we feel we're worse off than we were five, or nine, years ago is not one of them.
Of course we're worse off. We've been through a global war against a virus. That war may not have left us with the rubble of destroyed buildings, but the aftermath is real and in many ways parallel, with a rise in mental health issues, a surge in the number of immigrants and refugees, a continuing disruption of the supply chain, and an overall rise in the cost of living. Look around the world: It is not "Justin-flation," it's post-COVID-inflation.
In Britain, the aftermath of the Second World War lasted for a decade. Rationing continued until the early 1950s. We'd learned to eat less and waste nothing, but oh, the pain of the continued rationing of clothing. I was a teenager, and I got through my post-secondary years with a few black sweaters, a couple of pairs of black pants and an assortment of scarves. I saved for months for a dress for the graduation formal.
Many of my generation sympathize with the difficulties of younger people and the necessary but hopefully temporary need for the lowering of expectations and the tightening of belts. But if we join the current swing to vote out our incumbent government, I doubt it will be because of the high cost of living. We've been there before.
Annette Bellamy, Ottawa
Order of Canada for this chef?
Re: Ottawa Mission serves Thanksgiving meals Monday, bringing total for week to a record 17,257, Oct. 14.
Chef Ric Allen-Watson should be nominated for the Order of Canada. He has shown not only absolute dedication to the cause of feeding people in need, but has shown textbook organizational skills to get things done.
Meals for thousands at Thanksgiving, food trucks, chef training etc. This guy is GOOD!
Michel Tremblay, Cumberland
Friend is fine, thanks to others
Recently, a lady friend and I were shopping in Bells Corners. My friend (we are both seniors) tripped on a curb in the shopping centre and fell quite hard.
I can't tell you how many lovely people stopped, walked over and tried to help. One offered to call 911 and another very nice man got her to her feet and made sure she was OK.
I just wanted to give them a sincere thank you and let them know that their care and concern were very much appreciated.
My friend is fine. No broken bones.
Anita Redfern, Ottawa

Document 335 of 500

Drug used to slow ALS progression shows promise in treating deadly brain cancer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/drug-used-slow-als-progression-shows-promise/docview/3121292670/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Drug+used+to+slow+ALS+progression+shows+promise+in+treating+deadly+brain+cancer&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An old drug could bring new hope for people suffering from the most common, and deadly, primary brain tumour in adults.
uOttawa researcher Dr. Arezu Jahani-Asl, who holds a Canada Research Chair in Neurobiology of Disease, leads a team that has found a drug currently used to slow progression of the neurological disorder ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis) shows promise in treating glioblastoma.
Jahani-Asl's team found evidence that the drug - called edaravone - shows promise in suppressing the self-renewing cancerous stem cells that challenge treatment of glioblastoma. Those brain tumour stem cells are at the core of treatment resistance and tumour recurrence in patients with glioblastoma.
Finding ways to target those cells could have major implications for global efforts to combat brain cancer, said Jahani-Asl. Her team reported on the findings in the journal Stem Cell Reports.
Patients diagnosed with the aggressive form of cancer have an average survival rate of about two years, something that has not improved significantly in recent years despite progress in understanding the tumour.
Tragically Hip front man Gord Downie, who died in 2017, and former Ottawa Centre NDP MP Paul Dewar, who died in 2019, both put a face to the disease that affects about four per 100,000 people in Canada, according to the Brain Tumour Foundation of Canada. It affects more men than women and has a peak incidence of between the ages of 45 and 75.
Dr. Arezu Jahani-Asl is leading research at uOttawa that has found a drug currently used for the neurological disorder ALS shows promise for treating the deadly brain tumour glioblastoma.
Jahani-Asl said her research shows that edaravone specifically targets cancer stem cells "and is particularly effective in combination with ionizing radiation. The study suggests that edaravone, given in combination with ionizing radiation can be effective in eradicating cancer stem cells, and thus is expected to decrease the chance of resistance to therapy and recurrence in glioblastoma patients."
That could extend patients' survival rates, although Jahani-Asl said it is too soon to know the real-world impact of the drug.
Standard treatment for glioblastoma involves surgery followed by ionizing radiation and chemotherapy. Because of the nature of the tumours - which are diffuse and have finger-like tentacles that typically infiltrate the brain - surgery cannot remove the entire tumour. The goal of the ionizing radiation and chemotherapy is to kill the remaining tumour cells, she said.
Tragically Hip front man Gord Downie performs at the Canadian Tire Centre in Ottawa on Thursday, Aug. 18, 2016. Downie died in 2017.
The promising findings have been seen in animal models. The next step, said Jahani-Asl, is finding the best ways to optimize drug dosage in combination with ionizing radiation and chemotherapy.
Testing drugs for human use is typically slow and extremely costly. Repurposing drugs, such as the one being tested in Jahani-Asl's lab can significantly speed up that advancement from research to clinical trials because the drugs have already been approved for human use. It is one reason there has been a growing interest in trying to find new uses for existing drugs, especially when it comes to battling cancer.
"We think it is very promising," she said. "All I can say is it would take less time and effort than a newly developed drug, but I can't give you a time line."
Jahani-Asl's research program at the uOttawa Faculty of Medicine is focused on developing novel therapeutic strategies for complex and devastating brain diseases.
Jahani-Asl said she has been moved by the glioblastoma patients and family members she has met.
"This is such a deadly disease. It is almost a death sentence. It is a heartbreaking situation for any family."
She said her research has received donations from patients' families who want to see the outlook for patients improve. "It has been very touching."
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newsletters so we can keep you informed.

Ontario lowers minimum age for regular breast cancer screening
Dr. John Bell awarded lifetime achievement prize by Canadian Cancer Society

Document 336 of 500

Ottawa patients continue to face longer than average emergency waits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-patients-continue-face-longer-than-average/docview/3121066423/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+patients+continue+to+face+longer+than+average+emergency+waits&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa residents wait longer than most people in Ontario for emergency care, according to recent provincial statistics.
Waits to see a physician and get treatment are above the provincial average at every hospital in the city.
At Queensway Carleton Hospital, patients are waiting twice as long as the provincial average for an initial assessment by a doctor, according to recent provincial statistics.
On average, Ontario residents wait two hours in emergency departments before receiving initial assessments by physicians, according to Health Quality Ontario, the agency that monitors the province's health operations.
But that is far from the whole story.
Overall stays in emergency are much longer. On average, non-emergency patients spend a total of 3.2 hours in the emergency department and high-urgency patients spend an average of 4.7 hours in emergency.
Patients waiting to be admitted to hospital are there for significantly longer: an average of 19.2 hours, and just 27 per cent of them within the provincial target of eight hours.
Those patients are frequently waiting in hallways or cubicles on stretchers meant for short-term use, something that leading Ontario geriatrician Dr. Samir Sinha compared to torture, especially for the elderly who make up a high number of patients waiting to be admitted to hospitals.
At the General campus of The Ottawa Hospital, CHEO and the Montfort, patients wait an average of 2.7 hours for initial assessments by physicians. At the Civic campus of TOH, patients wait 2.8 hours for initial assessment, on average.
At Queensway Carleton, the average wait for an initial assessment is four hours, or double the provincial average. That is the third-longest wait time across Ontario.
The longest wait times in the province - as of the end of August - were at Hawkesbury and District General Hospital, east of Ottawa.
The Hawkesbury and District General Hospital.
In other measures, Queensway Carleton Hospital fares better compared to other Ottawa hospitals.
It takes longer for high-urgency patients to get treated at The Ottawa Hospital and Montfort, for example, and QCH, at 18.3 hours, is below the provincial average of 19.2 hours for patients to get admitted to hospital from emergency. The waits are longer at other Ottawa hospitals.
Those waits drew the attention of Premier Doug Ford this past week. During a news conference, Ford partially blamed the problem on too many patients going to emergency departments for non-emergency issues.
"The emergency department is for emergencies, not little Johnny cutting his leg or so on and so forth," Ford was quoted as saying. He also said emergency doctors he talked to estimated that 50 per cent of people shouldn't be in the emergency department.
His comment drew immediate pushback from physicians and doctors' organizations, who said they misrepresented the problem by blaming it on patients and could discourage some from seeking the medical care they needed.
The Ontario Medical Association called on the province to focus on increasing access to doctors, not limiting it.
"Ontario's emergency departments are there to provide care when it's needed most, and patients should never feel discouraged from seeking help when and where they need it," the OMA said in a statement.
Perth emergency physician Dr. Alan Drummond was co-author of a position statement from the Canadian Association of Emergency Physicians that found, among other things, emergency department overcrowding was largely due to lack of access to hospital beds.
"Contrary to popular perceptions, ED overcrowding is not caused by inappropriate use of emergency departments or by high numbers of lower acuity patients presenting to the ED; the inability of admitted patients to access in-patient beds from the ED is the most significant factor causing emergency department overcrowding in Canadian hospitals," the statement said.
On Saturday, Drummond said It was frustrating to hear Ford focus blame patients after years of research that had shown hospital capacity was the main driver of crowded emergency departments, not patient overuse.
"There has been a lot of work internationally and in Ontario looking at the issue of crowding and it has been largely disproven," Drummond said. "It is sad to see governments continue to focus on the myths and put all of their efforts into trying to blame and shame patients. Until we get our heads around the fact that we need to reduce hospital capacity to a safe occupancy level of 85 per cent, we will continue to deal with this."
Ontario has the second-lowest rate of hospital beds per population in Canada.
The Queensway Carleton Hospital is pictured in this file photo.
Emergency department officials at Queensway Carleton acknowledged this past week that patients were waiting too long for care and said they were working on improving efficiency in the busy department to help reduce those wait times.
"There are many factors that cause our wait times to be long, too many to count," said Dr. Adam Nicholson, chief and medical director of emergency services at the hospital. "In the end, unfortunately, our patients are waiting too long to be seen, they are the ones to suffer."
They urged patients to continue to come to the hospital when they needed to and emphasized that the sickest patients would get seen first.
"We understand how frustrating it can be for our patients and families," said Paula Archambault, the hospital's clinical director of critical care, emergency and mental health.
Archambault added: "If a patient feels they need to come to the emergency, they should come. We take their concerns seriously and are proud of Queensway Carleton Hospital and how much patient satisfaction we have recorded. It is a huge point of pride and we don't want to see that change."
She said the hospital had launched a project to significantly reduce the amount of time patients waited to be admitted to in-patient beds. That wait time has improved by 7.7 hours over last year, Archambault said.
The hospital is also working on projects to improve the amount of time until patients have initial assessments by changing the way patients flow through the emergency department.
Queensway Carleton has also seen a recent spike in the complexity and acuity of patients.
"Certainly that is a factor in the wait times," Nicholson said. "When patients are more complex, it takes longer."
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newsletters so we can keep you informed.

Youth, 16, faces murder charges in slaying of 15-year-old boy
Firefighters free person pinned in overturned vehicle

How to fire a prime minister

Author: King, Geoff

Publication info: King, Geoff. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-fire-prime-minister/docview/3120936225/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+to+fire+a+prime+minister&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=King%2C+Geoff&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Observers have pointed out that the federal Liberal party has no mechanism in place to oust its leader.
Some may recall that John Diefenbaker lost his job as Tory prime minister due to a cabinet revolt, not through a formal process. However, I guess the current crop of Liberal ministers is willing to risk a fate similar to that experienced by the Ontario Liberals under Kathleen Wynne, who led her MPPs like lemmings over an electoral cliff.
