Pellerin: Doug Ford's tunnel idea is simple, and simplistic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pellerin-doug-fords-tunnel-idea-is-simple/docview/3114116003/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pellerin%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+tunnel+idea+is+simple%2C+and+simplistic&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We got new, complex and complicated rules this week about the conditions under which Ottawa councillors can solicit donations from developers. But if there's one thing I have learned in my years on this blue dot, it's that the more complex your rules are, the higher the number of loopholes you find.
I wish we could make rules about everything, the simple way Ontario Premier Doug Ford does.
You think I'm kidding, and maybe I am, but his idea to dig a tunnel under Highway 401 is brilliant in its simplicity. There is no reason why we should tolerate 12 lanes of ugly, smelly and loud highway if we can dig a tunnel for all those vehicles to descend into.
Who cares how many dozens of billions of dollars it'll cost? In fact, we should totally invest all the money we have, plus all the future earnings of the next six generations to create Mole Cities all over everywhere, to ensure only people on foot, bikes and other modes of active transportation get to enjoy life above ground without cars bothering them.
Ford is already on the record saying we shouldn't have any more bike lanes if that means reducing space for private cars. With this tunnel idea every street becomes a bike lane. Isn't it glorious?
Think of how many fewer signs we'd need at intersections to avoid collisions between people confused about how to take a turn in the semi-protected bike lane and those who think any space wider than 16 inches is big enough to fit a car that needs to park "just for two minutes."
And imagine Queensway Underground, where all the complaints from public servants stuck in return-to-office hell wouldn't be heard by anyone. Same with interprovincial traffic. Tunnels under the Ottawa River would get cars and heavy trucks across and straight to Highway 417 without the need to snake through downtown streets. Bonus: We wouldn't need to replace the Alexandra Bridge with something so painfully bland - say, why do all three chosen designs have to be white anyway? Is this the Model T of bridges?
But seriously.
The challenge for every city, including Ottawa, is to move people and goods in ways that are efficient, smart, safe, reliable and environmentally sustainable. Burying problems isn't usually a solution - though occasionally it can be, like not making the main heavy-truck route go through the touristy areas of the capital city of this G7 country.
There was once serious thought about making traffic go under instead of through Ottawa's downtown. A 2016 feasibility study concluded it would be possible to build a tunnel linking the Macdonald-Cartier Bridge to Highway 417 at Nicolas Street, at an estimated cost of $1.7 to $2 billion in 2015 dollars - it'd be a bargain at three times the price to divert as many as 25,000 vehicles per day from downtown streets. Especially as so many vehicles currently on said downtown streets aren't there because their drivers want to be; they're there because that's the route from one provincial highway to another.
I like the way Quebec City Mayor Bruno Marchand (notoriously pro-cycling and a bit on the woke side) says the solution to the 21st-century challenge of urban mobility isn't an "either/or," but an "and." We won't get to where we need to be by being closed-minded and ideological. We all have different and varying transportation needs. We must let smart people with the right vision design systems that work for everyone.
The trick, with transportation as with political contribution rules, is to keep things simple without veering into the simplistic. Tunnels are not a solution to congestion on the 401, and they are extraordinarily expensive and disruptive to build. Yet to solve our transportation challenges we need more thoughtful approaches than just building more lanes. Sometimes what's needed is to divert traffic away from densely populated areas - and maybe buying back the Highway 407 toll/ring road, as the premier has speculated, is what Ontario needs to do.
Let's have the clarity of mind to apply the right solutions to the correct problems. Let's not be trapped by our own tunnel vision.
Brigitte Pellerin (they/them) is an Ottawa writer.

Document 386 of 500

Today's letters: Desk 'lottery' an insult to public servants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-desk-lottery-insult-public/docview/3113230920/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Desk+%27lottery%27+an+insult+to+public+servants&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Do we expect them to work on the floor?
Re: CRTC raffling off office space in 'absurd and insulting' charity drive for public servants, Sept. 27.
The news story about the CRTC's federal lottery to get a place to work in the office is appalling. It's an insult to the public servants, who do their jobs, but not often with much support from the public - or from their management, it would appear.
I know that I would feel undervalued and exploited if I were told that it was important for me to come in to the office to do work that I had done effectively since 2020, but it wasn't important for my employer to make sure that I had a place to work. Finding a comfortable place on the floor is not happening. Nor is working in a lobby, break room or - what next? The washrooms?
Public servants deserve a modicum of respect, and at least a reasonable place to do their work. And not to be told to make up the extra commuting time if they get into the office to find no desk or chair, and have to go back home.
And then, to be cajoled into donating to a charity by the hope of having a desk for a month? Appalling.
I'm a former public servant and retired teacher - but as a teacher I had consistent use of a desk.
Eleanor Abra, Ottawa
Fight hatred: embrace our diversity
I was pleased to see special funds allotted recently to address the increasing hate crimes involving Jews, Muslims, LGBTQ+ people and other targeted minorities. Those of us who are "mixed race" should be equally and positively treated as full Canadians.
In addressing the increase in hate crimes, I would also emphasize the positive actions that should be highlighted, such as the current Islamic History Month. This is not to forget Black History and Asian History celebratory months. Greater emphasis must also be placed on the implementation of the official Canadian Multiculturalism Month (unanimously designated by Parliament in 1988).
The federal government must address hate crimes by corrective intervention and appropriate legal measures while positively affirming our underlying diverse and unified way of life.
In concrete terms: I will be celebrating Islamic History Month by attending the Ottawa Muslim Women Organization's special public lunch; respecting the Jewish community New Year commemoration; and celebrating the annual festive Diwali of the Hindu community. This is the full Canadian way.
Roman Mukerjee, Ottawa
New bridge a symbol of our connectedness
Re: NCC unveils shortlisted concepts for replacing Alexandra Bridge, Oct. 2.
Replacing the old Alexandra Bridge is a good idea and it's probably about time, although the new one won't begin to be constructed until 2028. The exciting part is that the shortlisted bridge plans are all "signature" bridges, which will add to the beauty of the Ottawa/Gatineau landscape.
Similar to the Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco, the Rialto in Venice, the Confederation Bridge in Prince Edward Island, or even the London Eye in Britain, the new bridge plans are graceful and eye-catching and will surely become an iconic landmark in the Ottawa region, akin to Parliament Hill or the Canadian Museum of History.
Bridges are an important part of public architecture. They are metaphors for connection, shortening the notion of distance; conversational pieces; meeting places; and they don't discriminate - anyone can cross them.
This new bridge is a future bridge, and will most likely someday change people's idea of the National Capitol Region. It will be a monument to progress, majestically reaching out and over the historic Ottawa River, spanning two provinces and looking down at all the thousands of years of history that have taken place below.
Douglas Cornish, Canada
Lansdowne should be done this way
Re: Ambitious plan for south side of Wellington Street shows how building design should be done, Oct. 1.
Architect Toon Dreessen cites the "Block 2" plan to redevelop a portion of Wellington Street across from Parliament Hill as an innovative and creative project, one which should provide "a welcoming, safe and accessible public realm." One learning from this initiative, he suggests is: "Hold proper design competitions for public projects, creating better value and engaging local talent, giving the public a voice in the design process."
What a shame this wasn't followed with another major Ottawa project, Lansdowne "Mall." Yes, Lansdowne Mall, because that's what we got when a former mayor and city council, together with the Ottawa Sports and Entertainment Group, hijacked a proposed public design competition for Lansdowne Park, leaving us with a failed shopping mall populated by condos, offices, banks and private schools. There's even a dentist's office and car dealership.
Have the current mayor and council learned anything from this debacle? Not likely. Look forward to them digging the hole even deeper, literally, when they provide final approval in the new year to Lansdowne 2.0, which includes the building of a 40-storey and a 29-storey high rise in the middle of our former park. This isn't the city that fun forgot, this is the city that never learns.
Arieh Bonder, Ottawa
Our next mayor? Toon Dreessen, please
Many thanks for printing the article on Block 2? by architect Toon Dreessen. This particular piece presents information on how all levels of government can work together to make our city Ottawa "the beautiful capital we aspire to be."
In 2022, the federal government sought proposals for the block "bordered by O'Connor, Metcalfe and Sparks Street, (which) fronts onto Wellington Street, directly across from Parliament Hill." Unlike other major projects in the city that have seen only one design considered - remember the tragedy at Lansdowne Park - Public Services Procurement Canada put out a design competition and we are seeing different alternatives.
After explaining the details and arguments, Dreessen concludes that "Block 2 is a unique opportunity to be different" and asks readers to support federal investment in our downtown, "bringing jobs, new office space and creative ideas to make our best Ottawa possible."
Dreessen has made similar arguments in the Citizen about how to run such big projects. And always, they have been ignored by City Hall. I would like to see Dreessen become mayor. He has the knowledge and vision to make Ottawa become a great city.
Patty Deline, Ottawa
Let's figure out Sparks Street first
I have visited Washington, London and Paris on several occasions and while I agree that Ottawa could create a world-class G7 capital city on par with those cities, I wonder just how this can be achieved given some of our challenges.
None of the three cities named has the same climate as Ottawa. While Winterlude has been Ottawa's answer to attract people to the city at the end of January, unlike Paris, which has a thriving café lifestyle, I find it hard to get motivated to stroll Ottawa's streets in the middle of winter looking for a place to sit and enjoy some refreshments while bundled in a parka.
All three cities have extensive and functioning public transit systems, which efficiently move thousands of people around their urban areas. Currently, this something Ottawa is still trying to construct.
I also look at the Sparks Street pedestrian mall. It was a forward-thinking idea when introduced in 1967, yet more than a half-century later, it is still under-utilized. So, why don't we figure out how to make Sparks Street successfully work all year-round first, before we decide to close off Wellington?
Ed Storey, Nepean
Meanwhile, over at 24 Sussex Drive ...
Toon Dreessen makes suggestions for this whole city block,
to create a people place for everyone who expects Ottawa to be
a "world-class capital city" like Washington, Paris or London.
But there is a dark cloud however, not too far from the Parliament Buildings: It's 24 Sussex Drive, supposed home of Canada's prime ministers, neglected and left to rot. I smell a rat.
Helene Doucet, Ottawa
Patient-centred care works wonders
Re: The Ottawa Hospital showed me we CAN treat patients with dignity, Sept. 27.
It was heartwarming to read Dahlia Belfer's observations about her interaction with staff at the IR clinic at the General campus of The Ottawa Hospital.
To feel respected by those caregivers at a time when one feels so vulnerable and anxious is a testament to the patient-centred service that can be delivered when the patient as a person is the focus.
From a my own experience at the cancer treatment centre at Hotel Dieu and Kingston General Hospital, I acknowledge how much easier the ordeal is when one is surrounded by competent, committed staff who believe in service founded on compassion and human interest. Fostering an approach to patients that recognizes those shared human connections is a skill that could receive priority in the organization of medical treatment if leaders believe in promoting comfortable experiences for all patients.
Celia. M. Carter, Brockville
Flexi-boards aren't always safer
Re: Today's letters: Flexi-boards don't make Ottawa roads safer, Sept. 25.
When there is a flexi-board in the middle of the road and no shoulder on that road, it increases my chances, as a cyclist, of being involved in a collision with a vehicle.
The car must stay in its lane and I as a cyclist must stop and wait for the traffic to clear, as there is not enough room for both of us.
Luanne Doll, Cornwall
How can we get Doug Ford's attention?
Re: Why Mark Sutcliffe's 'fairness for Ottawa' campaign should target Doug Ford too, Oct. 3.
Mohammed Adam is absolutely correct: the Ontario government does not care about our transit woes. Doug Ford ran for mayor of Toronto and when he lost, he ran for premier. Ever since, he has acted like he was the mayor because he controls the purse strings.
Ford only pays attention to Ottawa when the federal government partners in some project. Look at how he did nothing during the trucker convoy crisis until the feds stepped in. Ottawans have no political clout in the present government but there must be some way we can put pressure on him.
Cathy Haley, Ottawa
Is the city spending on real priorities?
The city has declared that public transit is a priority, and that more funds will have to come from taxpayer pockets to cover a shortfall in revenues. This is based on the highly questionable assumption that current tax dollars are being spent on essential services, and that there is no room to reallocate funds to transit.
If transit is truly a priority, then it should be treated as such, and funds reallocated from other, lower-priority areas. A good example is the millions in grants given to third parties to fund their pet programs. The most recent amount shown on the city's budget website is that in 2019 the city gave $64.9 million to 806 recipients. That total is undoubtedly a lot higher now, but good luck in trying to find a current dollar figure on the city budget site.
Is all this money well spent? I'm sure all these recipients would claim that they are doing good things - but are they essential? Think about the $85,000 grant to Capital Pride in 2024. How many more like that are there that are desirable to some, but not as essential as the city claims transit is. Perhaps these groups should do more of their own fundraising and the freed-up taxpayer funds assigned to transit.
Questions should also be asked about the use being made of the millions the city is taking in from traffic cameras. Why are these funds not being assigned to supporting public transit?
There is much the city can do within its existing revenues to support transit. Yes, that does mean politicians will have to make some choices. But make them before crying poverty to other levels of government - and keep your hands out of our pockets until you have done so.
John E. Hitchinson, Nepean
How about free transit to Sens' games?
I have been to but one hockey game at The Canadian Tire Centre, a 4-0 win over my HABs.
The excellent facility was clean, solid and functional. I was astonished at how good our lines of sight were from our relatively cheap seats. The only problem was getting there and parking.
Solution: Until they spoil the scenic soil at LeBreton Flats, perhaps The Senators organization might consider, as well as upgrades and needed maintenance of CTC, footing the bill for extra OC Transpo transit/trains from downtown to and from the rink on game and concert nights. Perhaps a free ride with a game or concert ticket?
This would take huge numbers of cars and their noise and pollution off Highway 417 on those dates. It would allow patrons to imbibe more at the events, bringing bigger bucks for owner Michael Andlauer. It would avoid drunk driving post-game. It would familiarize people with OC Transpo's excellent new service on the new rail line to Kanata.
You can easily step away from this idea if I am proven wrong after one season.
Thomas Brawn, Orléans

Today's letters: Police horses, meet Ottawa's night mayor (or mare?)

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-police-horses-meet-ottawas-night/docview/3113040412/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Police+horses%2C+meet+Ottawa%27s+night+mayor+%28or+mare%3F%29&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Horsing around against crime
Re: Do Ottawa police really need horses to help fight crime? Oct. 1.
Isn't the RCMP somewhat curtailing the famous Musical Ride? If Ottawa's newly appointed "night mayor" can add duties as a new "night mare," maybe we can redeploy some horses to clean up crime, starting with the ByWard Market.
We could put the horses before the court!
Ben Kutner, Ottawa
Good Samaritans when I was in 'knee-ed'
Earlier this week, I went to Service Ontario to renew my licence and OHIP card. After waiting for a considerable time on a chair that was uncomfortable after a knee replacement a few months ago, I got up feeling discomfort in that knee. When I came out of the building I dropped some papers and when I went to pick them up I fell on the sidewalk.
Two kind gentlemen helped me up. One then went into the pharmacy and bought me a box of Kleenex and a package of wipes to help with the bleeding, while the other got me safely to my car.
I just wanted to let them know how much I appreciated their assistance. Nothing is broken, but I have lots of swelling and my face is a mess. I will make a donation to The Food Bank to honour their good deeds. There are angels among us.
Joy Baxter, Ottawa

Document 389 of 500

School bus contract talks stalled in Renfrew county; More than 10,000 students, families impacted as discussions end with no deal

Author: Dixon, Anthony

Publication info: Dixon, Anthony. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/school-bus-contract-talks-stalled-renfrew-county/docview/3112985141/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=School+bus+contract+talks+stalled+in+Renfrew+county%3B+More+than+10%2C000+students%2C+families+impacted+as+discussions+end+with+no+deal&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Dixon%2C+Anthony&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Transportation disruptions continue for over 10,000 students along with their families, and school communities across Renfrew County after discussions between school bus companies, the transportation consortium, and leadership from the two school boards ended without an agreement, despite two revised offers with higher rates.
Renfrew County Joint Transportation Consortium (RCJTC), along with the directors of education for the Renfrew County District School Board (RCDSB) and the Renfrew County Catholic District School Board (RCCDSB) met Wednesday with a group representing school bus companies.
The representatives for the school bus companies maintained the position that a multi-year contract starting with a 20 per cent increase in year one with a total increase of 35 per cent over five years was the only offer they would accept.
According to the RCJTC, this was the same position the school bus companies had maintained since Aug. 30 and would result in $8.8 million over the five years being transferred from other areas of the school boards'budgets to transportation, impacting services to students.
"This is hugely disappointing," said Justin Jeffrey, general manager of RCJTC. "We've been working with both the school boards and the Ministry of Education to identify any possible solution to help close the gap between funding and contract rate expectations and get service running for students. The bus companies have indicated they are not willing to budge."
Mark Allan, a spokesperson for the local school bus operators, has stated throughout the dispute that the operators have faced 30 per cent to 70 per cent increases in costs to keep buses running safely, but Ontario's Ministry of Education has refused to address these inflationary costs and is not providing sufficient funds to school boards to cover transportation. Allan has said new buses are 71 per cent more expensive, bus parts, tires, and brakes are up 50 per cent, driver wages have increased 38 per cent, and insurance costs are up 30 per cent.
In an RCJTC news release issued on the evening of Sept. 2, the consortium said it, along with the RCDSB and RCCDSB, had worked with the Ministry of Education to find additional flexibility within their existing budgets by transferring dollars from education funding to transportation.
The consortium said the latest offer made to the bus operators was an 18 per cent increase in Year 1, plus two per cent per year for the remaining four years, meaning a total of 26 per cent over five years.
The consortium stated this latest offer would require more than $4 million over the five years to be transferred from other areas of the school boards'budgets to transportation and "... would see school bus companies in Renfrew County holding one of the richest contracts in the province."
"We know how hard this situation has been on families across the county and we have been coming back to the table to find a solution that would work. At the same time, we haven't been provided with any concrete financial data to justify the massive increase the operators are demanding from the school boards," Jeffrey said. "The school boards can't be expected to keep pulling money out of classrooms."
On Wednesday evening, approximately 200 parents, community members, and education workers from across Renfrew County, along with NDP MPP and Opposition education critic Chandra Pasma took part in a virtual meeting to share frustration about the ongoing student busing dispute in Renfrew County.
During the meeting, concerns were raised that the education funding issues affecting transportation being experienced currently in Renfrew County will happen in other areas of the province.
"The continued impact of cuts to education funding have forced boards of education to make choices about what services to slash to meet the ongoing underfunding in public education," said Karen Littlewood, Ontario Secondary Schools Teachers'Federation president. "More than 40 per cent of school boards are reporting budget deficits, even after already reducing the services and programs available to students."
RCJTC said it continued to explore options and remained open to meeting with school bus companies on an individual basis. The recent Request for Service Qualifications (RFSQ) to identify alternative school bus companies will also be updated to reflect the financial details of the latest contract offer. adixon@postmedia.com

Subject: School buses; Consortia; Students; Budgets; Funding; Education

Document 390 of 500

Sutcliffe's plea for help aims at wrong target

Author: Mohammed, Adam

Publication info: Mohammed, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sutcliffes-plea-help-aims-at-wrong-target/docview/3112984145/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sutcliffe%27s+plea+for+help+aims+at+wrong+target&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Mohammed%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the midst of Mayor Mark Sutcliffe's "fairness for Ottawa" campaign, Premier Doug Ford popped up to promise a multi-billion-dollar tunnel under Highway 401 in the Greater Toronto Area to make driving easier and more comfortable for suburban commuters.
The tunnel would go from Brampton to Scarborough, a distance of about 55 kilometres, and one expert put the cost at $1 billion a kilometre. Ford, however, assures all of us that it won't cost "hundreds of billions of dollars," for which, I guess, provincial taxpayers in Ottawa should be grateful.
But think about this, Ottawa: Here is a premier who can't find money for transit in the city, but who can conjure up billions to dig a hole in the ground for cars in the GTA. As I thought about what this says regarding Ford's attitude toward Ottawa, I read a revealing analysis of Ontario's transit funding by Coun. Glen Gower, chair of the transit commission. I then reread the city's financial report presented to council last month, and, putting it all together, began to wonder if Sutcliffe and his council are aiming their ire over the lack of funding at the right government.
There is no doubt the main reason for Ottawa's financial crisis is the soaring cost of public transit, not payments-in-lieu-of-taxes. Transit is what has thrown the city into a financial tailspin. The city is now shouldering 56 per cent of LRT costs that were to be shared three ways, with municipal, provincial and federal governments each paying one-third. Then, there is the $120-million deficit accrued largely because of a pandemic ridership slump, which led to talk of apocalyptic 2025 property tax increases. And OC Transpo's long-term deficit, which was projected to be $6.6 billion by 2048 - if you can think that far ahead - has now grown to $8.9 billion.
Can the federal government help with more transit funding? Sure, it can, and should. But remember that transit is the direct responsibility of the provincial government, which must be held accountable.
Aside from money for the irrational tunnel, the GTA gets billions of dollars year after year for transit, while Ottawa gets small change in comparison. In November 2023, Toronto got a one-time payment of $330 million, and another three-year $330-million operating support subsidy. And Ottawa? In March, it got $543 million over 10 years for road improvements and a new interchange on Highway 417 at Barnsdale Road. No money for transit.
But according to Gower's analysis, the average household in Ottawa has contributed $5,250 through provincial taxes to transit projects in the GTA in the last few years. In contrast, the same household has contributed about $285 in provincial taxes to Ottawa transit projects. For every dollar of Ottawa's provincial taxes that pays for transit in the city, the analysis shows more than $18 goes to a project in the GTA.
Clearly, a big reason for our financial crisis is that our provincial taxes are paying for transit in the GTA, while we in Ottawa are saddled with huge costs.
Which leads to the question: Why are the mayor and council not making this provincial neglect the central argument of their fairness campaign? Where is the pressure on Ontario to treat Ottawa half as decently as it treats the GTA? Who is beating the drum for fairness from the Ontario government? In case you thought Ottawa just likes to whine about transit funding, the Ontario Financial Accountability Office just made our case. In a recent study, it said Ottawa will see the steepest decline of anywhere in the province next year in terms of per-resident transit subsidies.
"The FAO projects that the Ottawa economic region will experience the largest reduction in per-resident subsidies from $59.61 in 2022-23 to $31.91 in 2024-25, primarily due to the conclusion of the Safe Restart Agreement," the report said. But Toronto subsidies keep rising.
Perhaps, it is time for the mayor and council to rethink their strategy, and redirect their fire at the right target. Mohammed Adam is an Ottawa journalist and commentator. Reach him at nylamiles48@gmail.com.

Subject: Taxes; Economic crisis; Councils; Funding; Subsidies; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxes Economic crisis Subsidies Tax increases

Adam: Why Mark Sutcliffe's 'fairness for Ottawa' campaign should target Doug Ford too

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/adam-why-mark-sutcliffes-fairness-ottawa-campaign/docview/3112749532/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Adam%3A+Why+Mark+Sutcliffe%27s+%27fairness+for+Ottawa%27+campaign+should+target+Doug+Ford+too&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the midst of Mayor Mark Sutcliffe's "fairness for Ottawa" campaign, Ontario Premier Doug popped up to promise a multi-billion-dollar tunnel under Highway 401 in the Greater Toronto Area to make driving easier and more comfortable for suburban commuters.
The tunnel would go from Brampton to Scarborough, a distance of about 55 kilometres, and one expert put the cost at $1 billion a kilometre Ford, however, assures all of us that it won't cost "hundreds of billions of dollars," for which, I guess, provincial taxpayers in Ottawa should be grateful.
But think about this, Ottawa: Here is a premier who can't find money for transit in the city, but who can conjure up billions to dig a hole in the ground for cars in the GTA. I then reread the city's financial report presented to council last month, and, putting it all together, began to wonder if Sutcliffe and his council are aiming their ire over the lack of funding at the right government.
There is no doubt the main reason for Ottawa's financial crisis is the soaring cost of public transit, not payments-in-lieu-of-taxes. Transit is what has thrown the city into a financial tailspin. The city is now shouldering 56 per cent of LRT costs that were to be shared three ways, with municipal, provincial and federal governments each paying one-third. Then, there is the $120-million deficit accrued largely because of a pandemic ridership slump, which led to talk of apocalyptic 2025 property tax increases. And OC Transpo's long-term deficit, which was projected to be $6.6 billion by 2048 - if you can think that far ahead - has now grown to $8.9 billion
Can the federal government help with more transit funding? Sure, it can, and should. But remember that transit is the direct responsibility of the provincial government, which must be held accountable.
Aside from money for the irrational tunnel, the GTA gets billions of dollars year after year for transit, while Ottawa gets small change in comparison. In November 2023, Toronto got a one-time payment of $330 million, and another three-year $330-million operating support subsidy. And Ottawa? In March, it got $543 million over 10 years for road improvements and a new interchange on Highway 417 at Barnsdale Road. No money for transit.
But according to Gower's analysis, the average household in Ottawa has contributed $5,250 through provincial taxes to transit projects in the GTA in the last few years. In contrast, the same household has contributed about $285 in provincial taxes to Ottawa transit projects. For every dollar of Ottawa's provincial taxes that pays for transit in the city, the analysis shows more than $18 goes to a project in the GTA.

Which leads to the question: Why are the mayor and council not making this provincial neglect the central argument of their fairness campaign? Where is the pressure on Ontario to treat Ottawa half as decently as it treats the GTA? Who is beating the drum for fairness from the Ontario government?
In case you thought Ottawa just likes to whine about transit funding, the Ontario Financial Accountability Office just made our case. In a recent study, it said Ottawa will see the steepest decline of anywhere in the province next year in terms of per-resident transit subsidies.
"The FAO projects that the Ottawa economic region will experience the largest reduction in per-resident subsidies from $59.61 in 2022-23 to $31.91 in 2024-25, primarily due to the conclusion of the Safe Restart Agreement," the report said. But Toronto subsidies keep rising.
Perhaps, it is time for the mayor and council to rethink their strategy, and redirect their fire at the right target.
Mohammed Adam is an Ottawa journalist and commentator. Reach him at nylamiles48@gmail.com

Document 392 of 500

Focus on adoption instead: poilievre; Abortion issue; New position in written response to activist group

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/focus-on-adoption-instead-poilievre-abortion/docview/3112645929/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Focus+on+adoption+instead%3A+poilievre%3B+Abortion+issue%3B+New+position+in+written+response+to+activist+group&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Focusing on legislation that promotes adoption would do "greater good" to help pregnant women than restricting access to abortion, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says in a new letter.
His office outlined the leader's position in correspondence sent to an anti-abortion supporter, who wrote the leader in early September calling on him to ban the procedure, suggesting Poilievre has changed his stance from being a "pro-lifer" over his 20-year career as a member of Parliament.
Campaign Life Coalition, the anti-abortion organization that shared the exchange with the National Post, points to how Poilievre voted against gay marriage in 2005, but, by 2020, said he felt it had been a success.
"While my government will not introduce or pass a law banning abortions, there are many other ways to support women who want to bring a child into the world. I do not believe that abortion should be the only option available to women faced with an unexpected pregnancy," Poilievre's letter from Sept. 11 reads.
"We can ease the challenges of putting a child up for adoption by supporting women through all nine months of pregnancy ... I believe focusing on bills that promote adoption and help pregnant women through crises would do greater good."
Asked how a Poilievre government would make adoption easier, spokesman Sebastian Skamski did not provide details but instead slammed how the Liberals voted against a private member's bill proposed by Saskatchewan MP Rosemarie Falk, which sought to create a 15-week employment insurance benefit for adoptive parents.
The Liberals ultimately introduced their own measure, which Falk herself told the House of Commons back in April provided "cause for cautious optimism."
Poilievre had supported her proposal and released a video where he reflected on his own adoption back in 1979, calling it "the greatest gift I've ever been given."
Abortion has been among the thorniest social issues Poilievre has had to navigate since becoming leader, one that has also dogged leaders before him.
When Poilie vre considered running for the Conservative leadership the first time in 2020, he said any future government of his would not reopen the issue, but would maintain free votes for MPs on matters of conscience.
Poilievre also confirmed during the party's 2022 leadership race that he considers himself to be "prochoice."
During that race, a leaked draft of a yet-to-be-made public U.S. Supreme Court decision overturning Roe v. Wade thrust the issue into the spotlight, which Poilievre responded to by saying that a future government of his would not introduce or pass any laws restricting abortion.
His wife, Anaida Poilievre, who is also campaigning for him, said during a French-language interview aired last December that the couple was "pro-choice."
The Liberals still point to the voting record and statements made by Poilievre's MPs, as the party has long been home to a well-mobilized social conservative wing, who believe the country must restrict access to abortion.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals have long attacked the Conservatives for having such views, including after a report last week that Saskatchewan MP Jeremy Patzer visited a U.S. church with anti-abortion views.
Poilievre's office was also quick to distance itself from statements made by Alberta Conservative MP Arnold Viersen, who appeared on a Liberal MP's podcast to say he hopes for the day abortion is "unthinkable," and if given the chance again would vote down gay marriage.
The Liberals also attacked a private member's bill proposed by Saskatchewan MP Cathy Wagantall, which was defeated last year, that proposed to make it an aggravating offence for someone to knowingly harm a pregnant women - a measure that proponents of abortion access warned opened the door for legislating more protections to a fetus, though Conservatives argued the bill didn't even mention abortion.
In his September letter to the anti-abortion supporter, Poilievre said those who oppose the procedure have a home with the Conservatives.
"Pro-life Canadians are welcome in our party. The Conservative Party of Canada is a big tent with a strong tradition of free votes of conscience, and I intend to maintain this status quo," it read.
The letter also added that Conservatives oppose the Liberals'pledge to strip crisis pregnancy centres of their charitable status. Advocates of abortion access say many of them spread false and misleading information about the procedure.
Poilievre pledged to "stand up" to government attacks on "organizations that help pregnant women."
Skamski said that for Poilievre, as the country's possible next prime minister, "no laws or rules will be passed that restrict women's reproductive choices. Period."
"Common-sense Conservatives support the value of adoption and supporting parents who provide a loving home to adopted children," he said. "Mr. Poilievre understands the power of adoption firsthand, having been born to a 16-year-old mother and adopted by two school teachers as a child."
He added that Canadians can expect a future Poilievre-led government to be one that "minds its own business," including when it comes to personal decisions about "their bodies."
Campaign Life Coalition president Jeff Gunnarson said in an interview he was "disappointed" by Poilievre's position on abortion, but said he felt his stance on adoption "useful."
The group came out against Poilievre during the 2022 leadership election, which he won with almost 70 per cent of the votes. The coalition instead asked supporters to vote for Ontario MP Leslyn Lewis, who was the only one of the five candidates to promise restrictions on abortion. She finished a distant third.
RightNow, another anti-abortion group, had encouraged the same. It also criticized the party for disqualifying a social conservative candidate during the nomination race for the safe Conservative riding of Oxford, Ont., last year, which was ultimately won by Arpan Khanna, now an MP who served as Poilievre's former national outreach coordinator.
Gunnarson questions whether Poilievre would make promoting adoption a high priority should he form the next government and whether he would consider dedicating money to the issue.
Despite supporters'disappointment with Poilievre, Gunnarson said many social conservatives are likely to still cast a ballot for the party on election day.
"If you don't promote (abortion) and you don't increase access and you don't put more money towards it, that's a positive," he said.
"The bar is so low in Canada, that's considered a good thing."
Poilievre's office did not answer when asked what the leader would do about federal funding for sexual health centres and services that promote education and access to abortion, as well as similar services offered overseas.
National Post staylor@postmedia.com

Document 393 of 500

Ontario plans to raise speed limit on 400-series highways, premier says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-plans-raise-speed-limit-on-400-series/docview/3112547425/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+plans+to+raise+speed+limit+on+400-series+highways%2C+premier+says&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TORONTO - Ontario Premier Doug Ford says the province is planning to increase speed limits on 400-series highways across the province.
The government increased speed limits from 100 km/h to 110 km/h on six sections of provincial highways in 2022 after several successful pilot programs that first began in 2019.
Earlier this year, the province raised the speed limit on 10 more sections of highways across the province, including a 70-kilometre stretch of Highway 416 from Highway 401 to Ottawa.
Ford said Wednesday that he had directed Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria to raise the speed limit to 110 km/h on all 400-series highways "where it is safe to do so."
Ford said he was leaving no stone unturned in his goal to help fight gridlock and reduce travel times.
The move comes as Ford has also directed transportation officials to explore building a tunnel under Highway 401 across the Greater Toronto Area.
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newsletters so we can keep you informed.

Today's letters: The Belltown Dome deserves special care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-belltown-dome-deserves-special/docview/3111870180/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+The+Belltown+Dome+deserves+special+care&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Belltown Dome was a great bargain
Re: Demise of the Belltown Dome - Is this what awaits Ottawa's other urban rinks? Sept. 18.
The Belltown Dome, built in 1979, was intended to be a relatively inexpensive community recreation facility costing about the same as an inflatable structure but much less to maintain. I have an initial brochure mentioning a cost of $620,000 but I recall that the price crept up to about $800,000 at completion. Wow - what a deal!
The original membrane was very attractive. Unfortunately, it was quickly vandalized (cut) and became disfigured until replaced by the current "shell."
I was the structural engineer under contract with Expodome Intl. who did the original structural analysis and design of the "funky" all-aluminum space frame with geodesic end caps.
Belltown is now almost twice as old as the Canadian Tire Centre, so thoughts of repurposing are not unexpected. As with the Biosphere in Montreal, consideration should be given to saving the relatively maintenance-free structure, just because it reflects a time when civic construction could be creative without blowing huge capital budgets.
Jim Rawlings PEng., Ottawa
Two ways to help improve the capital
Re: Here are four ways to fund, improve transit in the city and cut car use, Sept. 24.
Upon reading the column, I was near overwhelmed by thoughts of Plus ça change, plus c'est la même chose, and déjÃ vu all over again. I wrote a related column, "Taking steps towards the end of the automobile era" that was published in the Ottawa Citizen in 1975.
Over the almost 50 years since my 1975 column, I have encountered many thousands of reports about world-class cities, and they share two important lessons for anyone who seeks to associate "world-class" with Ottawa.
First, land use and transportation planning are integrated from the outset as a first principle, thereby minimizing the need and often brutal challenge to retrofit.
Second, most of these cities adopted sustainable transport principles and practices several decades ago, and have succeeded in achieving significant shifts from trips by private motor vehicle to trips by transit, cycling and walking, all to rave reviews.
Ottawa, due in large part to the pro-developer bias of too many councils, needs to do a 180-degree turn on the development front for at least three decades to begin to fix Ottawa's land use and transportation mess.
Do our current mayor and councillors seem likely to seize the day? So far, signs point to a continuation of the same old, same old.
Barry Wellar, Professor Emeritus, University of Ottawa
What will we call our 401 tunnel?
Re: Doug Ford wants to build 'one of the world's longest tunnels' under Highway 401, Sept. 25.
If the Boston tunnel is called "the Big Dig," should Premier Ford's version be called "the Big D(o)ug"?
Paddy Fuller, Ottawa
Ottawa needs more age-friendly housing
Oct. 1 is recognized as the International Day of Older Persons, a good time to reflect on the place seniors occupy in our society. As the percentage of older adults in Ottawa increases, reaching 25 per cent by 2035, I ask: how age-friendly is our city?
Data reports that 17 per cent of older adults are homeless, 4000+ are on a waiting list for social housing and many who want to downsize have few choices to move to.
City planners must support the building of more innovative, affordable and accessible housing options that encourage community engagement, and are near amenities with good access to home care and community support services. Clearly, it will require all levels of government working together.
Creating more age-friendly housing for older adults will affirm and respect the equal place that older adults have in our society. It will also be good for people of all ages, backgrounds and vulnerabilities.
An age-friendly city is the kind of city I want to live in.
Valerie Wright, Ottawa
Do we need reminding about climate?
Here in Orléans, we received from the desk of MP Marie-France Lalonde a gently worded reminder of how woke Canadians can actually benefit from carbon prices rebates.
But why, oh why, with climate related catastrophes aplenty occurring with frightening regularity in Canada and the world at large - why do we need a government grift to get us to act in ways to avert environmental degradation and global heating?
Is the evidence not up enough Canadian noses yet? Is it not destroying enough backyards?
Is it too much rocket science to drive more carefully in a well-tuned car, take public transit, bike or walk, or insulate your home or turn down the thermostat a puny degree or two?
We are not sheep ... are we?
Thomas Brawn, Orléans

Today's letters: Liberal MPs in Ottawa are supposed to fight for the city, aren't they?

Document 396 of 500

Senack: Nepean MPP Lisa MacLeod has earned respect as she leaves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/senack-nepean-mpp-lisa-macleod-has-earned-respect/docview/3110182756/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Senack%3A+Nepean+MPP+Lisa+MacLeod+has+earned+respect+as+she+leaves&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Through controversy and triumph, Lisa MacLeod has been a pit bull in Ottawa politics. The proud Nova Scotian's recent decision to not run again for the Ontario legislature comes at the right time for her and the riding she has served for two decades.
The news didn't necessarily come as a surprise. After too many scandals and negative front-page headlines to count in recent years, it would be near impossible for her to bounce back.
But while I welcome the change in reign that will come with MacLeod's seat being open for the first time in six election cycles - almost 20 years - no one can deny her unwavering commitment to the city.
When tornadoes ravaged parts of Arlington Woods in 2018, MacLeod distributed food at Larkin Park for days until the lights came back on. Politically, there were investments to build more schools, funding for the Barnsdale/Highway 416 interchange and a financial commitment for the eventual Barrhaven Downtown.
Through MacLeod's leadership, the Vimy Memorial Bridge was finally built and organizations such as the Barrhaven Legion and Food Cupboard soared. Millions were raised and invested in the Queensway Carleton Hospital and the Children's Hospital of Eastern Ontario.
Perhaps one of MacLeod's greatest legacies is Rowan's Law, named after 17-year-old John McCrae Secondary School student Rowan Stringer, who died from Second Impact Syndrome in 2013. The bill made Ontario a national leader in concussion safety, which brought along protocols for when a player is injured, rules to minimize the risk of concussions when playing sports, and an annual review of awareness. It has saved lives.
There was also Nick's Law, a private member's bill that was tabled in memory of Nick Cody, an 18-year-old Barrhaven teen who died from an overdose of MDMA in 2013.
These were projects that came from the heart. And anyone who knows Lisa MacLeod on a personal level knows, she has a big one. Yet unfortunately, diagnosis of depression and bipolar disorder led to some "slip ups" and behaviour that was uncalled for. It was never intentional.
It's hard for those who aren't in the public eye to fully grasp the pressures that come with people watching your every move. In a mental health podcast episode released Sept. 16, MacLeod admitted that the toll of it all became so much that she tried to take her own life in May 2022.
"I didn't want to live anymore," she said. "I felt like I was living in a fishbowl and I couldn't breathe."
Those who made politics personal could have had a life lost on their hands.
Since that time, MacLeod has kept away from media cameras and party politics, choosing instead to finish the job on her own terms. She hasn't attended press conferences for announcements she disagrees with, nor has she posted cookie-cutter party messaging on social media.
I've known MacLeod for close to a decade. I've seen the politician we all know, but also Lisa the human. When my mom got sick with COVID while in hospital, MacLeod pushed for more safety protocols to be in place, with successful results. She made homemade stew and dropped it off at our house. A few months later when my mom was feeling better, she took us to Boston Pizza for dinner.
This is not to say I agree with MacLeod's politics; in fact it's unlikely I'll ever vote Conservative. But behind every politician is a human.
The riding of Nepean will be a battlefield when voters head to the polls provincially. Liberal candidate Tyler Watt came in second in the last provincial election with 34 per cent of the vote - about 2,000 votes less than MacLeod, her smallest margin to win ever.
Today, MacLeod is the only Progressive Conservative politician in the city. Ottawa-West Nepean went NDP in 2022, Kanata-Carleton went Liberal during a byelection in 2023, and earlier this year Carleton's Goldie Ghamari became an independent after being ousted by her caucus.
If Doug Ford ever desires to wake up and start caring about Ottawa, his time to do so is now.
Charlie Senack is an Ottawa writer and editor of the Kitchissippi Times newspaper. Reach him at: senackcharlie@gmail.com.
Related
'I was in crisis': MPP Lisa MacLeod praised for 'courageous' discussion of mental health
Left out of Doug Ford's new cabinet, Nepean MPP Lisa MacLeod taking 'time off' to improve health; other eastern Ontario ministers keep portfolios

MacLeod's heart has always driven her political life; Behind every politician is a human, Charlie Senack says

Author: Senack, Charlie

Document 398 of 500

'Sure winner'in the suburbs or 'blitzkrieg'on good planning?; Ontario's bike Lane Debate; Province's proposed restrictions could hurt city's transportation plan, councillors say

Author: Crawford, Blair

Publication info: Crawford, Blair. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sure-winnerin-suburbs-blitzkriegon-good-planning/docview/3108457159/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Sure+winner%27in+the+suburbs+or+%27blitzkrieg%27on+good+planning%3F%3B+Ontario%27s+bike+Lane+Debate%3B+Province%27s+proposed+restrictions+could+hurt+city%27s+transportation+plan%2C+councillors+say&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Crawford%2C+Blair&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government's plan to prioritize cars over bikes will undercut Ottawa's new Transportation Master Plan and the province's own work to make housing more affordable, Kitchissippi Ward Coun. Jeff Leiper says.
The provincial Conservatives under Premier Doug Ford are reportedly planning to make it illegal for municipalities to install bike lanes if that means blocking existing car lanes. The proposal is part of the government's Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act to be introduced in October, according to a report Friday by CBC.
"I hope when people who are supporting these kinds of policies think about their kids and their grandkids and the kinds of cities they want them to be able to live in," Leiper said.
"This isn't a battleground in the non-existent war on cars. It's a blitzkrieg on planning for affordable cities."
Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria would not comment on the proposal at a media availability Friday, saying only the government was committed to ending gridlock.
"My job, as a minister of transportation, is to make sure people have choice, whether you want to take a bike, whether you want to ride public transit, whether you want to drive on a highway to get to work. Our government is making sure you have that choice and options," Sarkaria told reporters.
Lisa MacLeod, Conservative MPP for Nepean, signalled her support with a post Friday on the social media platform X.
"A winner for sure in Ottawa exurban and suburban neighbourhoods who voted overwhelming in the last city election to oppose more bike lines," MacLeod posted.
Ottawa's first segregated bike lane was established on Laurier Avenue in 2017 and is at the heart of the city's downtown cycling network. It has been especially busy this month as federal government workers return to the office three days a week. On Wednesday, Leiper and a group of volunteers did an informal traffic survey at the intersection of Laurier and Bay Street and found that 42 per cent of traffic was either bicycles or occasional e-scooters.
"In the morning especially, I'll see six, seven, eight bikes in just one block of Laurier," Leiper said.
Ottawa's Transportation Master Plan, to be completed next year, envisions that by 2046 more than half of all travel in the city will be by active transportation, public transit or by carpooling.
"That is going to be impossible to meet without putting the cycling infrastructure in place to safely accommodate the volume of people cycling that we need to reach that number," Leiper said.
He said there had been a disconnect between the Ontario government's push for more housing - and removing rent control on new builds - and its underfunding of public transit and active transportation. Housing is so expensive many residents can't afford a car, he said.
"The affordability question around housing is intimately tied to transportation policy. And, when cars are increasingly unaffordable, we're going to have to rely, in large part, on active transportation," Leiper said.
"Scott Street is the perfect example. Scott Street is exactly where the province has told us to put those tall towers, which no longer have rent control. And, if you're going to put cycling infrastructure in, you're going to have to retrofit it. How can we retrofit safe cycling structure without removing car lanes? I can't square that circle."
Capital Ward Coun. Shawn Menard said there was no evidence that bike lanes increased congestion.
"The experience we have had with street renewal where bike lanes are added is that safety and efficiency of travel are greatly improved," Menard said in a text exchange. "The Bank Street Bridge changes have not seen any more congestion than before the changes, and safety has been enhanced for everyone who drives, walks, bikes and rolls. I suspect, like many things the Ford government has enacted, this will cost taxpayers more and not improve outcomes for transportation."
The cycling advocacy group Bike Ottawa is planning to lobby the province to oppose the change.
"It's an unfortunate direction for the government to take," Bike Ottawa vice-president Dave Robertson said.
"It's not cars versus bikes. It's about the mobility of people in our city centres. It's not going to make things better for drivers. That's just not true.
"The only way we're going to reduce congestion is to get people out of cars," Robertson said. "I can't say that often enough."

Subject: Automobiles; Public transportation; Bicycles; Planning; Bicycling; Rent control

Business indexing term: Subject: Rent control

Document 399 of 500

Ontario's bike lane debate: 'Sure winner' in suburbs or 'blitzkrieg' on good planning?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-bike-lane-debate-sure-winner-suburbs/docview/3107618795/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+bike+lane+debate%3A+%27Sure+winner%27+in+suburbs+or+%27blitzkrieg%27+on+good+planning%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government's plan to prioritize cars over bikes will undercut Ottawa's new Transportation Master Plan and the province's own work to make housing more affordable, Kitchissippi Ward Coun. Jeff Leiper says.
The provincial Conservatives under Premier Doug Ford are reportedly planning to make it illegal for municipalities to install bike lanes if that means blocking existing car lanes. The proposal is part of the government's "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act" to be introduced in October, according to a report Friday by CBC.
"I hope when people who are supporting these kinds of policies think about their kids and their grandkids and the kinds of cities they want them to be able to live in," Leiper said.
"This isn't a battleground in the non-existent war on cars. It's a blitzkrieg on planning for affordable cities."

"My job, as a minister of transportation, is to make sure people have choice, whether you want to take a bike, whether you want to ride public transit, whether you want to drive on a highway to get to work. Our government is is making sure you have that choice and options," Sarkaria told reporters.
Lisa MacLeod, Conservative MPP for Nepean, signaled her support with a post Friday on the social media platform X.
"A winner for sure in Ottawa exurban and suburban neighbourhoods who voted overwhelming in the last city election to oppose more bike lines," MacLeod posted.
https://x.com/MacLeodLisa/status/1836974294426898782
Ottawa's first segregated bike lane was established on Laurier Avenue in 2017 and is at the heart of the city's downtown cycling network. It has been especially busy this month as federal government workers return to the office three days a week. On Wednesday, Leiper and a group of volunteers did an informal traffic survey at the intersection of Laurier and Bay Street and found that 42 per cent of traffic was either bicycles or occasional e-scooters.
"In the morning especially, I'll see six, seven, eight bikes in just one block of Laurier," Leiper said.
Ottawa's Transportation Master Plan, to be completed next year, envisions that by 2046 more than half of all travel in the city will be by active transportation, public transit or by carpooling.
"That is going to be impossible to meet without putting the cycling infrastructure in place to safely accommodate the volume of people cycling that we need to reach that number," Leiper said.
He said there had been a disconnect between the Ontario government's push for more housing - and removing rent control on new builds - and its underfunding of public transit and active transportation. Housing is so expensive many residents can't afford car, he said.
"The affordability question around housing is intimately tied to transportation policy. And, when cars are increasingly unaffordable, we're going to have rely, in large part, on active transportation," Leiper said.
"Scott Street is the perfect example. Scott Street is exactly where the province has told us to put those tall towers, which no longer have rent control. And, if you're going to put cycling infrastructure in, you're going to have to retrofit it. How can we retrofit safe cycling structure without removing car lanes? I can't square that circle."
Capital Ward Coun. Shawn Menard said there was no evidence that bike lanes increased congestion.
"The experience we have had with street renewal where bike lanes are added is that safety and efficiency of travel are greatly improved," Menard said in a text exchange. "The Bank Street Bridge changes have not seen any more congestion than before the changes, and safety has been enhanced for everyone who drives, walks, bikes and rolls. I suspect, like many things the Ford Government has enacted, this will cost taxpayers more and not improve outcomes for transportation."
The cycling advocacy group Bike Ottawa is planning to lobby the province to oppose the change.
"It's an unfortunate direction for the government to take," Bike Ottawa vice-president Dave Robertson said. "It's not cars versus bikes. It's about the mobility of people in our city centres. It's not going to make things better for drivers. That's just not true.
"The only way we're going to reduce congestion is to get people out of cars," Robertson said. "I can't say that often enough."
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newsletters so we can keep you informed.

Where to eat kebabs in Ottawa: Sizzling lamb, beef, and chicken skewers fresh off the fire

Document 400 of 500

Is Sutcliffe's 2.9% tax hike right for Ottawa?

Author: Mohammed, Adam

Publication info: Mohammed, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-sutcliffes-2-9-tax-hike-right-ottawa/docview/3107340094/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Sutcliffe%27s+2.9%25+tax+hike+right+for+Ottawa%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-09-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Mohammed%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ahead of budget 2025 deliberations, Mayor Mark Sutcliffe has warned about property tax increases, massive transit fare hikes and service cuts the likes of which the city hasn't seen in decades.
In its budget directions released Monday and approved Wednesday, we learned that property tax increases could be 2.9 per cent, transit levy hikes between 2.9 and 37 per cent and fare increases between 2.5 and 75 per cent. Worst case: a single ride on OC Transpo could cost $6.65, up from $3.80. A monthly pass could go from $128 to $225. All this is because of a lack of federal and provincial funding that has left OC Transpo with a $120-million deficit.
It is all very dramatic, and it sounds a tad apocalyptic. Sutcliffe wants you to blame federal and provincial governments. He takes a shot at his critics: "The people advocating for big tax increases are basically saying it's OK for the transit projects in the GTA to get 100 per cent funding from other levels of government while we pay over half in Ottawa."
No, Mr. Mayor, no one is saying that. Ottawa residents know and resent the fact that when it comes to transit, the city has been shafted for years. It is disingenuous to suggest otherwise. Besides, nobody wants higher taxes. Trust me, I'd rather pay 0.1 per cent than 2.9 per cent. But then, if you want to build a livable city, you need appropriate services to match the ambition, such as transit that is reliable and affordable, recreational services that meet everyday pockets, and roads that are not littered with potholes.
What many people want is not higher taxes, but a balanced budget with reasonable property tax increases that allow us to maintain city-building services. Indeed, knowing from the very beginning that a budget crunch was coming, the city could have spread tax increases a bit higher over the last two years to put us in a better position to deal with what lies ahead.
While Sutcliffe is right to feel aggrieved about transit funding, it is worth noting that when he had a chance to negotiate a deal with Ontario, transit did not seem to be his top priority.
The 10-year, $543-million deal for Ottawa that Premier Doug Ford announced in March, was more about roads than transit. The deal, which Sutcliffe hailed as a "big win for Ottawa," included the uploading of Hwy. 174, which then-Ontario premier Mike Harris dumped on Ottawa in 1997. The funding included money for housing, repairs and upgrading of major connecting and rural roads, as well as a new interchange at Hwy. 416 and Barnsdale Road.
The only nod to transit was $80 million for the Kanata North Transitway, contingent on matching funds from the federal government.
Here is a city that's facing a big transit deficit, and yet when he had the premier's ear, Sutcliffe, as best we can tell, negotiated money largely for roads, not his biggest need. Sutcliffe made his deal, and it is now too late to complain. One can only hope for some funding from provincial and federal governments.
Of course, no matter what, we are not going to see taxes going up steeply, or a 75 per cent transit fare increase. That's a bluff. Still, the question is whether Sutcliffe's 2.9 per cent is right for the times. This should be a vigorous budget debate based on financial reality, not on fulfilling election promises.
And this time, Ottawa residents should accept Sutcliffe's invitation to make their voices heard on what they really want.
Then we will see what councillors are made of as they craft the budget. Mohammed Adam is an Ottawa journalist and commentator. Reach him at nylamiles48@gmail.com.

Subject: Fares; Budgets; Funding; Property taxes; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Property taxes Tax increases

Company / organization: Name: OC Transpo; NAICS: 485112

Document 401 of 500

'Hallway'care up 22 per cent since 2018: CUPE; Problem worsening since Ford elected, 'symptomatic of lack of capacity': union

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hallwaycare-up-22-per-cent-since-2018-cupe/docview/3107339981/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Hallway%27care+up+22+per+cent+since+2018%3A+CUPE%3B+Problem+worsening+since+Ford+elected%2C+%27symptomatic+of+lack+of+capacity%27%3A+union&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-09-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Patients are spending more time in hallways and "unconventional spaces" at some Ottawa hospitals than they were in 2018 when Ontario Premier Doug Ford vowed to end hallway medicine.
In Ottawa, that includes dozens of patients spending nights in emergency departments or other unconventional spaces every day, something that can increase patient harms and stress on health workers.
At the Queensway Carleton Hospital, 3,779 patients spent the night in the emergency department during the 2023/24 fiscal year compared with 2,513 in 2017/18 - a 50 per cent increase.
Natasha Curley, a spokesperson for QCH, said addressing hallway medicine is a priority for the hospital and it "continues to look for innovative ways to ensure every patient receives timely and appropriate care."
Martin Sauvé, director of communications at Montfort Hospital, said surge beds are now required to be open on a regular basis. "This used to be needed mostly during viral season and winter months, when the number of hospital admissions is typically higher, but we now tend to see such volumes throughout the year."
Michaela Schreiter, spokesperson for The Ottawa Hospital, said it prepares for higher volume periods "and has plans in place to ensure that everyone receives the care they need in a timely manner."
According to a recent report from the news outlet The Trillium, The Ottawa Hospital had an average of 75 patients in unconventional spaces, including the temporary care unit in the former ambulance bay at the General campus, last January. The media outlet obtained the information through access to information.
Hallway health care is a complex and worsening problem that CUPE, which represents hospital workers in Ontario, says will not improve without a significant provincial investment in new hospital beds and staff over the next decade. CUPE recently released a report on the hospital capacity crisis in Ontario.
"It is a horrible way to experience health care," said Michael Hurley, president of CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions, who himself spent 55 hours in the emergency department at the General campus of The Ottawa Hospital a couple of years ago when he was ill. "It is symptomatic of the lack of capacity."
While Health Minister Sylvia Jones has talked about adding 3,000 hospital beds over the next decade, the union says Ontario will need 13,800 hospital beds during that period - based on data from the Institute for Clinical Evaluative Sciences (ICES) and Statistics Canada. Those resources will be needed to maintain the current level of care amid population growth and the demographic bulge of aging Boomers. The union says Ontario needs to invest an additional $2 billion a year to meet the needs.
In Ottawa that would translate to 1,400 new beds over the next 10 years as well as 10,000 additional staff in order to maintain services, according to the CUPE report.
The Ontario government has been investing in new hospital beds and building new hospitals, including a new Civic campus in Ottawa, scheduled to open in 2028.
Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for Health Minister Jones, said the Ford government has made record investments in health care, including increasing the health care budget by more than 31 per cent since 2018 and investing $85 billion into the health system this year. She added that the province is also "getting shovels in the ground" for 50 hospital projects, including in Ottawa, and has introduced initiatives to help people get care outside of hospitals.
The minister's office did not directly answer a question about the trend in hallway medicine in Ontario since 2018.
Curley, a spokesperson for QCH, says, among other things, the hospital has opened additional beds, developed a "robust" surge plan and launched a program to improve the efficiency in the transfer of patients from the emergency department to in-patient acute care beds. Last year, the hospital decreased that time by 7.7 hours.
She said all the hospitals in the city are working together, including with Ottawa paramedics, to address long wait times for the community.
Montfort has also added programs to address the issue, including a remote monitoring program, including a post-surgical program, allowing some patients to be discharged sooner, and a post-discharge assessment clinic for followup. Montford, like other hospitals, has also opened transitional care beds.
The report from CUPE notes that Ontario is already behind other provinces and most developed countries when it comes to the number of beds and health care staff per population. A number of Ontario hospitals - including The Ottawa Hospital - are running deficits.
CUPE also says the province's claim that it added 3,500 new hospital beds since 2020 contradicts information the unions received from the hospitals, which say there has been an increase of 2,161 beds since the pandemic. CUPE's report also says more than double that many new beds are needed for hospitals to cope with demand.
"Based on the most recent CIHI data, Canada as a whole has 7.7 per cent more hospital beds per capita than Ontario. The result is very high hospital bed occupancy, cancelled surgeries and increased usage of unconventional spaces to treat patients with a 22 per cent increase in 'hallway health care'since Ford's election in 2018," according to the CUPE report: No Respite: Ontario's failure to plan for patients.
The Trillium, which covers Queen's Park, reported last week that during January of 2024, there were, on average, 2,000 patients a day being cared for in hallways or unconventional spaces in Ontario hospitals. That is double the number in 2017, according to the media outlet, which obtained the data through access to information.
Hurley of CUPE said the growing number of people on stretchers in hallways and elsewhere is causing frustration, anger and sadness among staff.
"They are seeing all these people languishing on these stretchers waiting for care and knowing that the impact it has on them is really significant."
Hurley said some operating room nurses have expressed shock at the state of some patients who have been on stretchers for prolonged periods of time and may have developed bed sores or soiled themselves. Operating room nurses are spending more time "cleaning them up" before surgery, he said.

Subject: Hospitals; Patients; Emergency medical care; Access to information
