Document 478 of 500

Most Canadians feel overtaxed, believe Ottawa overspends: poll; Accountability also lacking, results indicate

Author: Trépanier, Antoine

Publication info: Trépanier, Antoine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/most-canadians-feel-overtaxed-believe-ottawa/docview/3086143081/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Most+Canadians+feel+overtaxed%2C+believe+Ottawa+overspends%3A+poll%3B+Accountability+also+lacking%2C+results+indicate&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Tr%C3%A9panier%2C+Antoine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A majority of Canadians consider themselves overtaxed by a federal government that spends too much and unwisely. And Quebecers are the ones who most feel the Trudeau government is "investing" too much.
An Ipsos-MEI poll published on Thursday shows that 63 per cent of Canadians think the federal government's spending is either "too high" or "much too high," which is a sharp increase compared to a similar poll ran by Ipsos last year. They also feel the government isn't transparent or accountable enough for the spending spree.
In Quebec, 74 per cent said the Trudeau government spends too much, which is significantly more than those who said so in Western Canada (64 per cent), Ontario (56 per cent) and Atlantic Canada (55 per cent).
"Quebecers, I think, are reflecting the fact that they feel they aren't getting the value from all those taxes they pay," said Sean Simpson, senior vice-president at Ipsos.
He points out the fact that Quebec being one of the most heavily taxed jurisdictions in the world could influence the results.
Also, some programs recently adopted by the federal Liberals are already in place in Quebec. For instance, Canada's $40-billion childcare program aimed at providing $10-a-day child care doesn't apply to Quebec, which already offers the service.
The survey found that only 18 per cent of the 1,038 adult Canadians surveyed believe the federal government is effectively allocating funds to address the most important issues. According to Simpson, the "most important issues" among Canadians are health care, housing and affordability.
"Canadians have been watching federal government spending grow and grow for years, with no apparent effect on services," said Renaud Brossard, vice president of communications at the Montreal Economic Institute (MEI), which did the poll with Ipsos.
"It's time for the Trudeau government to put the brakes on spending and get its fiscal house in order, as the population is clearly demanding," said Brossard.
For months, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland have said that "the government is fighting for Canadians every day" and that their "economic plan is building an economy that works for everyone."
But the Ipsos numbers align with those in a recent Postmedia-Léger poll that found a majority of young Canadians (Gen Z and millennials) aren't big fans of plans for more government spending.
Last week, a different poll released by the Fraser Institute reported that 70 per cent of respondents believed that some Canadians don't pay their fair share of taxes, but only 35 per cent thought that high-income earners should pay more in taxes.
According to the Ipsos-MEI survey, 77 per cent of Canadians think the taxes they pay are too high for the services they receive.
"There is a disconnect between the Canadians say their priorities are and what they see the government focused on," Simpson said.
And while 72 per cent of respondents consider the amount they pay in income tax to be too high, Canadians remain more divided over the federal carbon tax. Fortyeight per cent oppose the tax while 39 per cent support it.
The survey also found that Canadians are skeptical that the new capital gains tax hike the Liberals announced in their budget this year will impact only the "wealthiest Canadians," as Freeland has insisted.
Nearly three out of four Canadians believe that the capital gains tax hike will affect the middle class. In addition, 60 per cent of respondents think it will have a negative impact on the Canadian economy.
"The population feels increasingly overtaxed, and the capital gains tax hike seems to be feeding into this perception," said Brossard. "Canadians'eyes are open, and they recognize that this tax hike will not just be levied on the ultra-wealthy, but will also reduce middle-class prosperity."
The online poll was conducted among 1,038 Canadians over 18 years old between July 10 and 14. The results are considered accurate to within plus or minus 3.7 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
National Post atrepanier@postmedia.com

Document 479 of 500

Liberal ministers rebuke premiers, tell them to 'enforce the law'on bail; Repeat Offenders

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-ministers-rebuke-premiers-tell-them/docview/3085006683/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+ministers+rebuke+premiers%2C+tell+them+to+%27enforce+the+law%27on+bail%3B+Repeat+Offenders&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.4&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two federal ministers have rebuked premiers who have called for Ottawa to review and amend its bail legislation after another high-profile murder by a known violent criminal, telling the provinces that it's time for them to "step up and enforce the law."
Justice Minister Arif Virani and Public Safety Minster Dominic LeBlanc made the comment in a joint statement late Wednesday, after receiving a letter signed jointly by the 13 provincial and territorial leaders demanding even tougher bail conditions than the changes the federal government passed into law last year.
"We listened to the provinces and territories when they asked for changes to the Criminal Code - now they need to step up and enforce the law," Virani and LeBlanc said in their statement.
"Provinces are responsible for the administration of bail, and more needs to be done to make sure police and prosecutors have the tools they need for effective monitoring and enforcement."
In the letter, the premiers thanked Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government for listening to their call from the year before where they demanded the Liberals toughen up access to bail following the shooting death of Ontario Provincial Police Const. Greg Pierzchala in late 2022.
Court documents showed one of the two people charged in his death had initially been denied bail on an unrelated assault and weapons charge, but was ultimately released.
What followed was a series of meetings between federal and provincial justice ministers that culminated in then justice minister David Lametti tabling Bill C-48, to revise bail provisions in the Criminal Code, which was passed into law in December.
Among the changes it made was expanding the use of reverse-onus provisions for certain offenders, which means that a person who has been charged must show a court why he or she should be released, rather than a Crown prosecutor having to prove why the offender should stay behind bars until trial.
Premiers are now demanding more action, pointing to the recent slaying of Tori Dunn, a British Columbia woman who police allege was murdered last month by an Ontario man with a long history of criminal violence who was out on release while awaiting trial on another aggravated assault charge.
The case highlights the need for the federal government to re-evaluate its bail measures, the premiers said in their letter, pointing to how its revisions were meant to make it more difficult for repeat, violent offenders to access pretrial release.
The Liberals should also undertake an analysis of bail data since its tougher provisions became law, the premiers added.
In their statement Wednesday, the federal ministers said collecting data on bail lies within a province's responsibility. During last year's study of its bail-reform legislation, Department of Justice officials testified before a Senate committee that not all provinces do.
"Provincial courts, prosecutors and police hold all the data needed to evaluate how the bail system is working," Virani and LeBlanc said.
"We expect provinces and territories to collect and share that data diligently, as they committed to do."
Shakir Rahim, who directs the criminal justice program at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association, says that study underscored the need for governments to work together to collect better bail data as the country has an incomplete picture of how the system is working.
While statistics show the majority of those in provincial jails are legally innocent and awaiting trial, he says what is not known about bail includes data such as "how many people who were denied bail committed an alleged offence?" "We often hear these stories that obviously are moving and important for us to analyze and take action on, where allegedly the bail system has failed, but we don't have information on the whole to say 'here are the patterns or the trends we can speak to.'" National Post, with additional reporting from The Canadian Press staylor@postmedia.com x.com/StephTaylornews

Document 480 of 500

What happened to Trump could happen here too; Political violence must be stopped, says Lisa MacLeod

Author: Macleod, Lisa

Publication info: Macleod, Lisa. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-happened-trump-could-happen-here-too/docview/3084629691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+happened+to+Trump+could+happen+here+too%3B+Political+violence+must+be+stopped%2C+says+Lisa+MacLeod&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Macleod%2C+Lisa&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump got shot. After the usual condemnations, thoughts and prayers, it was business as usual: the left equating him with the devil, the right with a saviour. A few saw him for what he was in the moment: a man, a husband and a father who is running for president of the United States and whose assassination attempt fundamentally challenged the cornerstone of democratic values - not just in the U.S., but in every free nation, including Canada.
Death threats, harassment, violence and vandalism directed at Canadian politicians have been present for some time. Canadians, however, have convinced ourselves that it doesn't happen here. That the politician who does make the news for a safety incident somehow deserved it. Still others prefer to contextualize and make excuses for why the incident occurred. As in the U.S., the toxicity that exists in extremities of party politics can be found here.
It has to stop. Your political opponent is not a threat to democracy. Our collective ambivalence in stopping undemocratic incitements, however, is.
In the tragic comedy that was my life for three years, a woman was arrested not once, but three times, for describing in explicit detail how she was going to kill me. I was hurt when academics, fellow politicians and a coalition she belonged to all justified the harm that she caused me. I once locked myself in my Toronto apartment for three days, and by the time the past Ontario election rolled round, I couldn't take anyone else's desired harm toward me for a second more, so I harmed myself. Don't underestimate the psychological impact physical threats and acts can have.
That's why it's time we sounded the alarm and had an adult conversation in Canada before someone in political office gets hurt, or something worse happens.
It is a major step in the right direction that RCMP Commissioner Mike Duheme is urging federal legislation on the matter, and that later this summer the sergeant-at-arms of the Ontario Legislature, Tim McGough, will convene a nationwide discussion on the issue. It will undoubtedly highlight sobering statistics from McGough's House of Commons counterpart, Patrick McDonell, who earlier this year revealed that 530 files were opened on threats against MPs in 2023 - an increase of almost 800 per cent over five years.
Let's put some names to that number: MPs Melissa Lantsman, Marco Mendicino, Arif Virani, Kevin Vuong, Mélanie Joly, Marc Miller, to name a few. It's happening to provincial politicians, too. Ontario Opposition Leader Marit Stiles had her office vandalized; former B.C. cabinet minister Selina Robinson, after leaving cabinet, dealt with death threats; so, too, did Parti Québécois Leader Paul St-Pierre Plamondon. All of this is designed to intimidate politicians from doing their jobs effectively. You will notice as well, they come from different parts of the country and different political ideologies. The myopic mindset is not from a monolithic right or left wing. It's present across the political spectrum. Unfortunately, after the usual condemnations, harassment by "the other side" picks up like clockwork until the next threat deemed credible comes around, prompting police to stop by the politician's constituency office or residence to discuss a shelter-in-place and evacuation plan.
We like to think we Canadians are nice and polite, and mostly we are. We can get passionate with our politics - I've been known to show my Celtic flair on occasion - but my fear is we have collectively remained complicit in not taking threats against politicians more seriously and in a timely manner.
Often we hear about threats to our democracy, whether it's foreign influence, terrorism or war. Tellingly, we don't equate a threat to democracy with an assault on a politician elected in a free and fair vote. It's at that moment, however, that our democracy is weakened and its resulting fragility is made increasingly more difficult to reverse.
Whether you love or loathe Trump, the attempted assassination weakened the democratic values of his country. For us to wait and see how this impacts Canada is foolhardy and irresponsible. Canada's enduring commitment to our democracy depends on action. Lisa MacLeod is the longtime MPP for Nepean.

Subject: Vandalism; Death threats; Democracy; Assassinations & assassination attempts; Politics; Politicians

Location: United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: donald; trump; usual; condemnations; thoughts; prayers

Document 481 of 500

Ottawa CAS, union going back to the table; Declining staff numbers critical issue for OPSEU members in ongoing talks

Author: Morrison, Carine

Publication info: Morrison, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-cas-union-going-back-table-declining-staff/docview/3084629676/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+CAS%2C+union+going+back+to+the+table%3B+Declining+staff+numbers+critical+issue+for+OPSEU+members+in+ongoing+talks&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Morrison%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Determined to secure better wages and job protection, the Ontario Public Service Employees Union will head back to the bargaining table next week to continue talks with the Children's Aid Society of Ottawa. Talks will resume on Monday with a mediator present.
Michele Thorn, an adoption worker, member of the bargaining committee and president of the union Local 454, said the parties resumed negotiations last weekend and continued until Tuesday, when talks broke down and the employer "walked away from the table."
One of the key issues for workers is the provincial government's plan to cut an additional 20 employees from the Children's Aid Society of Ottawa's current roster of 320. The organization, which is funded by the province and works to protect children experiencing abuse and provide families with assistance, once had more than 400 staff members.
"We have crucial work to do with the families and we can't do it with losing any more workers," Thorn said, adding that the union was looking for job protection for the life of the collective agreement. "We're already bleeding workers."
Negotiations began last November.
In March, 85 per cent of unionized workers voted to strike, and they walked off the job on July 8.
Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles and other politicians, including NDP MPP for Ottawa Centre Joel Harden, visited the union's picket line on Wednesday.
Kelly Raymond, executive director of the society, said in an email that its non-union and management team continued to provide "critical child protection services," prioritizing immediate child and youth safety issues.
"Our primary objective is to ensure continuity of services," Raymond said. "Since the strike began, we have met with the union and will continue to do so in order to work toward a resolution that benefits all parties involved. We are committed to working together to achieve our shared goals of sustainable child well-being."
Thorn said the union was also looking to secure better wages for staff, adding that CASO had gone "backwards" during negotiations and was no longer offering full retroactive pay.
CASO's most recent three-year contract offer, Thorn said, included wage increases of 4.0, 2.3 and 2.3 per cent. However, without retro pay, she said that deal wouldn't even equal seven per cent overall.
Before the strike, Thorn said a deal with wage increases totalling 8.2 per cent was on the table. She said the union needed to be re-offered at that amount as they "didn't go on strike to accept a worse offer financially."
Thorn said the union had mediated talks with a provincially appointed conciliator earlier this summer, but they weren't productive.
Some progress has been made over the past few days, she said, on issues that would be at no cost to the employer.
"We've been accepting the erosion of staff and services for years because of underfunding, and it's at a breaking point and so we can't accept it anymore," Thorn said, adding that government funding was based on the volume of children in care and had steadily decreased as CASO staff had done preventive work. "We're expecting there will be more cuts if we don't get that protection."
Michael Demone, spokesperson for the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services, said in an email that the government had invested more than $1.6 billion in 50 child welfare societies.
This year, Demone said, it increased funding for child protection services by $14 million in addition to last year's increase of $76.3 million.
Through the 2024 Ontario budget, Demone said, the child welfare sector will receive an increase of $36.5 million.
"This additional funding is a base increase and will be ongoing," Demone said. With files from Postmedia staff

Children's Aid Society of Ottawa, union heading to mediation as strike continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/childrens-aid-society-ottawa-union-heading/docview/3084445257/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 483 of 500

Guilbeault goes to battle with another province; Environment; this time it's Quebec, and it's over Caribou

Author: Trépanier, Antoine

Publication info: Trépanier, Antoine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/guilbeault-goes-battle-with-another-province/docview/3084111941/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guilbeault+goes+to+battle+with+another+province%3B+Environment%3B+this+time+it%27s+Quebec%2C+and+it%27s+over+Caribou&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.4&amp;au=Tr%C3%A9panier%2C+Antoine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault has found himself in another spat with a province, and this time it's not one in the West.
For months, Guilbeault has been demanding Quebec adopt a new plan to protect the province's woodland caribou from the impact of logging for industrial activities and road-network expansions, as herd numbers in the province appear to be dwindling.
Now the federal minister is going over the province's head, with an emergency decree ordering his own plan, outraging Quebec's provincial government and the Bloc Québécois.
Last year, Guilbeault told his cabinet colleagues that by law the federal government had a duty to intervene. They told him to negotiate with his provincial counterpart, Quebec's Environment Minister Benoit Charette, to resolve the issue.
He did. Until last month, when the minister used his federal powers to issue an emergency order to protect caribou habitat in Quebec and prohibit activities that contribute to "imminent threats".
"I cannot idly sit by and let a species disappear because the Quebec government is not willing to do what it had committed to do," Guilbeault said in an interview.
In 2023, the Quebec government estimated the caribou population to be between 6,162 and 7,445 individual animals. Ottawa maintains the majority of Quebec's caribou populations are declining because of the loss or alteration of its habitat.
Guilbeault is known for getting into heated spats with the provinces.
The former Greenpeace activist is known for his tense public relationships with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith and Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe over the carbon tax, clean electricity regulations and his proposed oilsands emissions cap. He has also clashed with Ontario Premier Doug Ford after the federal government tried blocking a proposed highway in the province using a federal act that was later found unconstitutional.
This time, he is dealing with a government that reveres questions of jurisdiction like no other province in the country.
"I didn't pick a fight with the Quebec government. I think I've explained very clearly that I was legally obligated to act the way I did," Guilbeault said.
Woodland caribou has been designated a "vulnerable" species since 2005 in Quebec and a "threatened" species since 2003 in Canada. Quebec is home to approximately 15 per cent of Canada's woodland caribou population.
The emergency order, which could be implemented in August, is still at the consultation stage. If Quebec presents a new plan soon, Guilbeault says he will back down.
The province argues that it has spent more than $100 million since 2019 on the protection and recovery of caribou in the province and says it is committed to long-term monitoring of herd populations. Quebec recently announced two protected-area caribou projects, representing nearly 100,771 square kilometres.
"Considering all the efforts made in recent years, the Quebec government considers the adoption of an emergency decree unjustified," said a statement from Charette's office sent to National Post.
Quebec has approximately 500,000 kilometres of logging roads, which Ottawa blames for declining numbers because the roads are used by bears and wolves to more easily hunt caribou. With his decree, Minister Guilbeault will prohibit activities that contribute to imminent threats against the species.
This could mean the industry being required to reforest abandoned roads in Val-d'Or, Charlevoix and Pipmuacan regions. The order could also lead to a decline in logging activity and significant job losses.
"We find it difficult to understand the federal government's stubbornness in imposing a decree that would have potentially devastating effects for several communities, when Quebec is a leader in biodiversity conservation," the statement from Charette's office said.
In rural communities, mayors and business leaders have expressed their profound concerns with the federal government's decision.
In an interview, the CEO of Quebec's Forest Industry Council, Jean-François Samray, said that if the decree is adopted, the industry could lose 6,500 jobs.
"We feel like Quebec has done enough in the last 20 years to be able to present a credible plan to the federal government and show them actions were taken in regards of the caribou," he said.
Quebec's chief forester Louis Pelletier, who is responsible for determining the logging potential of publicly owned forests, has said the federal decree announced would reduce the quantity of wood produced in the province each year by 1.4 million cubic meters, or about four per cent.
There are nine caribou in Vald'Or and 30 in Charlevoix. All of them respectively live in enclosures set up by Quebec few years ago.
According to the feds, the population in Val-d'Or has already crossed a threshold where it will die out, and the one in Charlevoix is very close to reaching it.
Environmental groups argue that governments must ensure the protection of caribou in the province because the species and its habitats are protected by law both at the provincial and federal levels, caribou are important for biodiversity and they are a spiritual and cultural icon for Indigenous people.
Guilbeault said he acknowledges the potential economic impact and anger over his decree, but is sticking to it.
"I'm the minister of environment and climate change. I'm basically the government stick when it comes to the environment. It makes some people happy, it makes some people unhappy. Being a cabinet minister is not a popularity contest," he said.
Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has called the order "Trudeau's job-killing decree and intrusion into Quebec's jurisdiction" and promises to scrap it if his party wins government. The Bloc also completely rejects Guilbeault's decree.
"It is not Ottawa's role to deal with Quebec's natural resources," said Mario Simard, the Bloc MP for Jonquière, an area that relies heavily on the forestry industry.
"We will never, never accept the decree," he added.
In a news conference on Parliament Hill last week, BQ Leader Yves-François Blanchet called on Guilbeault to immediately back down.
He also called on him to recognize that "he does not have the legitimacy to act alone and replace the government of Quebec."
Meanwhile, the Conservatives have been pressing for an emergency meeting of the natural resources committee of the House of Commons this month to discuss the matter, but the Bloc has not yet supported it.
National Post atrepanier@postmedia.com

Subject: Federal government; Jurisdiction; Caribou; Natural resources; Climate change; Biodiversity

Document 484 of 500

LCBO has bright future, Premier Ford says as two-week-long strike comes to an end

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-has-bright-future-premier-ford-says-as-two/docview/3083710912/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+has+bright+future%2C+Premier+Ford+says+as+two-week-long+strike+comes+to+an+end&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's main liquor retailer has a bright future, Premier Doug Ford said Monday as thousands of employees returned to work after a two-week strike.
The Liquor Control Board of Ontario said its stores will be open for business on Tuesday.
"I have confidence in the LCBO," Ford said at a news conference in Kitchener, Ont.
"They meet the first three thresholds: great quality, great people serving you and at a competitive price, so we're going to keep moving forward on it."
He also said the LCBO makes most of its money as the primary alcohol wholesaler in the province rather than through its retail stores.
"The fact is, per capita, we make the least amount of money in the entire country to the exception of P.E.I when it comes to alcohol sales," he said.
Ford's previous plan was to get beer, wine and ready-to-drink cocktails in convenience stores and all grocery stores by 2026, completing a 2018 election campaign promise. But in May he announced that would instead happen this year, capping speculation of an early election that Ford did not outright deny.
Convenience stores will be allowed to sell beer, wine and coolers starting Sept. 5 while newly licensed grocery stores can do so starting Oct. 31.
An "early implementation agreement" with The Beer Store involves the province paying the company up to $225 million to help it keep stores open and workers employed. The province is also giving brewers a rebate on an LCBO fee that normally brings in $45 million a year, and it is giving retailers a 10 per cent wholesale discount.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union said it faced an existential crisis with Ford's plan to open up the alcohol market.
The union representing the LCBO workers had said it believed Ford's plan to expand alcohol sales to convenience and grocery stores would threaten union jobs and the public revenue the LCBO provides to the province.
The union said the sticking point was Ford's plan to allow convenience and grocery stores to sell ready-to-drink cocktails, the fastest growing market within alcohol sales in the province. It was seeking job security, wage increases and better benefits.
Ford admitted ready-to-drink cocktails are fast sellers and make up about nine per cent of the LCBO's sales.
"They can't even keep ready-to-drink on the shelves, so we can see that market growing within the LCBO," he said.
Ford sped up his alcohol liberalization plans after the strike began on July 5, allowing grocery stores already licensed to sell beer and wine to also sell ready-to-drink cocktail beverages as of Thursday last week.
LCBO workers ratified on Sunday a new deal that includes an eight per cent wage increase over three years, the conversion of about 1,000 casual employees to permanent part-time positions and no store closures over the course of the agreement.
The ratification came after the deal was up in the air on Friday, after both OPSEU and the LCBO announced a tentative agreement had been reached but the union said the strike would continue after the employer refused to sign a return-to-work protocol.
The LCBO fired back by saying that the union had made new monetary demands after a deal was reached. OPSEU president JP Hornick said on Friday that part of the union's return-to-work proposals included seeking to have striking workers compensated.
Ford said he had never heard of such a thing after an agreement was reached.
"Imagine if that's taking your tax dollars for paying people that weren't working, it's absolutely crazy," he said.
The union leadership "came to their senses and we got a deal signed," he said.
Ford said he spoke to more than 500 LCBO workers during the strike, in addition to the usual flood of texts and calls he receives from Ontarians.
"But as many people as I talked to, I had as many people call me up: 'Get rid of the LCBO. We don't need them, put them in retail,"' Ford said.
"I just I don't believe that, I believe in supporting the 9,000 hard-working people."
- The Canadian Press

Document 485 of 500

LCBO workers ratify deal to end strike, stores to reopen Tuesday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-workers-ratify-deal-end-strike-stores-reopen/docview/3083238086/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+workers+ratify+deal+to+end+strike%2C+stores+to+reopen+Tuesday&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The LCBO says stores will reopen Tuesday after the union representing 10,000 of its workers ratified a new deal with the liquor retailer on Sunday to end the strike that had closed its stores for two weeks.
The Liquor Control Board of Ontario confirmed the ratification in a news release Sunday, saying the strike will officially end at 12:01 a.m. Monday and stores will be ready to serve customers the next day.
LCBO workers represented by the Ontario Public Service Employees Union voted in favour of the three-year collective agreement, OPSEU said in a press release Sunday.
"We went on strike to protect good jobs and public revenues, and to win more permanent jobs with benefits and guaranteed hours," said Colleen MacLeod, chair of the OPSEU bargaining team, in the press release. "Our members stood strong. They held strong lines, they talked to their communities - and they won."
The LCBO published details of the agreement on Friday, which includes an eight per cent wage increase over three years, the conversion of about 1,000 casual employees to permanent part-time positions and no store closures over the course of the agreement.
The ratification comes after the deal was up in the air on Friday, after both OPSEU and the LCBO announced a tentative agreement had been reached but the union said the strike would continue after the employer refused to sign a return-to-work protocol.
Both sides accused each other of bargaining in bad faith, with the employer saying it would file an unfair labour practice complaint.
The LCBO, however, issued a statement on Saturday saying both sides had signed off on the protocol and reopening plans were back underway.
"As we welcome back our valued employees, we are focused on returning to our normal operations in support of our retail and wholesale customers and of Ontario's beverage alcohol industry," the LCBO said in a statement Sunday.
OPSEU has said the strike, which began on July 5 and was the first of its kind in the LCBO's history, was about protecting union jobs and provincial public revenue amid Premier Doug Ford's plan to allow convenience and grocery stores to sell ready-to-drink cocktails.
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newsletters so we can keep you informed.

Document 486 of 500

Today's letters: We need more safe cyclists and fewer bozos

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-we-need-more-safe-cyclists-fewer/docview/3082938207/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+We+need+more+safe+cyclists+and+fewer+bozos&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Shoddy cycling on Scott Street
Re: Bike advocacy group pushes back on new road-safety education plan, July 16.
Again, the bicyclists play the "victim card" to deflect the problem on to the vehicle drivers. A noted local politician once told me: "There are a-holes on both sides of the situation." So, lets call the poor bicyclists "bozos" and the good ones bicyclists.
I drove (yes, my gas-guzzler) to the grocery store today. During the drive I came across bozos. One was going down Scott Street as if in the Olympics, ignoring the perfectly good, very expensive, bike way on Scott. Another was speeding up to blow through a traffic light as it turned red. A third one used the sidewalk instead of the adjacent bike lane.
I also met six bicyclists riding carefully. There was an additional rider of note, who stopped at a red light, went up to the traffic pole and pressed the walking request button. This person also had a flashing light on the front of the bike, which deserves a bonus point.
Total score: Bicyclists 8, Bozos 3. The good outnumber the bad, but given the consequences of a bicycle accident, still not good enough.
John Fraser, Ottawa
Near misses were cyclists' fault
Re: Bike advocacy group pushes back on new road-safety education plan, July 16.
Bike Ottawa is in complete denial if they don't think the City needs a defensive cycling education program.
I am in complete shock that they find the program somehow offensive. Take a look at the endless responses to police ads to motorists about cyclist safety, and the responses are "tell that to the cyclists!"
The near misses I've had with bicycles have been very clearly due to misbehaviour on the cyclist side - either from lack of education or, worse, aggressive cyclist behaviour. Sadly, the latter are likely to eschew such courses. Yet I'm the one who risks getting charged if anything happens.
Not everything is due to bike infrastructure. Issues like lack of signalling, weaving in and out of traffic, cutting cars off with no notice, driving many cyclists abreast, cycling through stop signs or failing to yield, including entering streets. I could go on, as could many drivers.
One of the scariest moments I encountered was a cyclist going the wrong way in the car lanes on Queen Elizabeth Driveway, with me thinking I was safely in the other lane, and that cyclist suddenly turning left, at high speed and head on, crossing in front of my car with no notice. To this day I don't know how I missed him.
This week, a cyclist turned into the traffic lane at full speed from a parking lot on busy Merivale Road with not so much as a glance. It was for traffic to screech to a halt.
I would be happy with a licensing system so one could report the worst offenders.
We need to be as serious about cyclist misbehaviour - out of ignorance or arrogance - as we are regarding motorists. Denial serves no one.
L.J. Ridgeway, Ottawa
The C stands for Control
Re: Union turns up pressure in LCBO strike, Jul 10.
Discussions of the LCBO strike usually focus on jobs and "convenience" while ignoring the "C" in LCBO. The "C" does not stand for "cost" or "convenience." It stands for "control." The LCBO was not established to create good jobs or to earn cash for the provincial government. It was established for the purpose of trying to ameliorate some health and social problems that are associated with alcohol.
The LCBO was created when prohibition was abolished. Prohibition was a misguided attempt to solve real problems. Many people in our society find it difficult to control their consumption of alcohol. If they drink a little, they drink too much. This often results in intimate-partner abuse, child abuse, drunk driving and accidents. However, prohibition failed miserably. Instead of solving the alcohol problem, it created a crime problem.
When prohibition ended in Ontario, the government turned to a different method of trying to ameliorate the problems caused by inappropriate consumption of alcohol. The LCBO was designed to make alcohol available but to control its consumption in the hope of reducing its abuse. Legislators noted that profit-oriented organizations are motivated to sell alcohol to minors, people who are already drunk, and people who regularly consume an excessive amount of alcohol; they believed that a government organization would not be incited to do those things and would train its employees to try to reduce the abuse of alcohol. People who were entitled to consume alcohol were given a card to identify them. Records were kept of their consumption and it was hoped that potential over-drinking and criminal activities would be detected and reduced.
The original plan has long been forgotten. Bars, restaurants, brewers oppose the idea of restricting consumption. Retail stores see selling alcohol as a source of profits. Most important, the LCBO has become a "cash cow" for the Ontario government. Convenience and cash have replaced control in most people's minds.
Since the LCBO was created, science has advanced. Research has shown that alcohol is never beneficial for the consumer's health. We now know that when it comes to alcohol consumption, there is no safe amount. This new knowledge makes the original goal of the LCBO worth reconsideration.
It would be foolish to think that we can end drinking or even substantially reduce it. However, the government should not be in the business of encouraging alcohol consumption. When I see Doug Ford (who I am told does not drink alcohol) appearing in advertisements encouraging drinking, I am horrified. What legitimate reason is there for spending government money to encourage people to consume harmful substances?
Dave Parnas, Ottawa
What do the experts say?
It is widely known and acknowledged that societal alcohol consumption brings with it considerable health costs. In this regard therefore, in any consideration of the Government of Ontario's plan to increase Ontarians' access to alcohol beyond the LCBO, one all-important question readily comes to mind: has any Ontario institution, say the Ontario Ministry of Health, or the Canadian Medical Association, performed a health cost assessment of this plan?
Such an assessment, of course, must be made well before the Government of Ontario's plan is making it a reality.
Joost Harwig, Almonte
The perils of privatization
Re: The LCBO union is losing, July 11.
Randall Denley seems to approach the issue as a wannabe politician, supporting Doug Ford's desire to open up the alcohol market to every convenience store in Ontario. But it's not, in any way, a form of "retail competition."
In fact, in his final paragraph he suggests the government should divest itself entirely from the alcohol business. Well, alcohol is not just like "any other product."
If the sale of alcohol products is opened up as Denley and Ford are proposing, I fear that the benefits now provided by the LCBO will disappear. This will lead to our choice of beverages being made by some unknown store owner, much as big grocery stores now decide which brands we consumers can choose from. And, our overall costs will increase.
Adhere to the old adage, "If it ain't broke, don't fix it." And, the LCBO isn't broke.
Ken Bowering, Woodlawn
CASO strike ignored
I find it interesting that the Ottawa Citizen has published numerous updates on the LCBO strike when there is also an ongoing strike at the Children's Aid Society of Ottawa, which has had maybe one small mention in these pages.
What is more important? That we are inconvenienced by having to buy our wine and beer elsewhere for a time, or that vulnerable children and families are not getting the support that they desperately need?
As a former foster parent, I've seen the essential work provided by CASO's dedicated social workers and child and youth counsellors first-hand. They need our support now to make sure that Ottawa is a place where every child and family can thrive.
Jessica Clark, Ottawa
Ford has lost our votes
My husband and I voted for Doug Ford in the last two elections, but because of his blatant lack of respect for workers' rights and the negotiation process, most particularly in regard to the current LCBO workers strike, he no longer has our votes. I personally was disgusted when he brought his comments and agenda to the public via the media for his own personal gain.
The focus needs to be on education and health care. There is no issue with access to alcoholic beverages in our province. Billionaires stand to gain even more money while good jobs are being lost to those who will work for the least amount of wages in major corporations.
Kelly Mason, Stittsville
Don't appease Putin
The Prime Minister of Hungary has said that, if elected, Trump would somehow settle the conflict in Ukraine.
On Oct.1, 1938, Neville Chamberlain arrived in London proclaiming "Peace for our time." Chamberlain had reached an agreement with Hitler whereby Germany would be allowed to occupy part of Czechoslovakia. The Czechs were not consulted. Ten days later, Hitler violated the agreement by occupying more Czech territory than was agreed to. On March 15, 1939, less than six months later, Hitler occupied all of Czechoslovakia. Less than six months after that, Hitler invaded Poland.
Any agreement imposed on Ukraine, for imposed it will be, will be lived up to for as many months as Putin's excessive ego can stand and then he will find a pretext to invade again. We must stand up to that evil man and his government, I hope with, but even without U.S. support.
Bruce F. Simpson, Ottawa
An unwelcome blast from the past
I'm a political junkie. Naturally I spent the last few evenings watching the Republican National Convention. Almost entirely, with very few exceptions, the delegates were middle-aged Caucasians.
To my eyes, they appeared to be trying to recapture the Eisenhower era, a time in the 1950s when men were in charge and women stayed at home, a time when African Americans and Hispanic Americans and Asian Americans and Jews were expected to be out of sight. The world has changed in so very many ways since those times, and mostly for the better.
They were filled with joy that someone is listening to their fears. But their anger and their fear should scare the rest of us, what would they do to us if they win?
Gary L Cohen, Nepean
Hearing no evil
Recent photos of MAGA-hatted Trump worshippers at the RNC included their newest hero-worship statement: the covering of their right ears with white "bandages" fashioned out of cloth or paper or whatever.
This is disturbing, not only because it shows the maniacal grasp that this human stain has over his devotees, but also because in adorning their ears thusly, these sycophantic acolytes are covering the wrong body part.
Never mind that Trump was wounded in the body part that he utilizes perhaps the least. These misguided and misled voters should be shielding their eyes (for what they don't let in) or even perhaps their mouths (for what they keep on letting out).
Jeff Kanter, Iroquois
A matter of respect
Re: Ottawa living in a canoe says its the safest place for her, July 10.
Bruce Deachman's article underscores an objective I have striven to adhere to through my life: "Before we drop our own templates of what we think is best for people in her or others' situations, we need to better understand their stories."
This is even more critical in times like these, when persons from diverse backgrounds are criticised or shunned while "the dogs" try to shove everyone into the same boat, so to speak. And affix labels. Everyone is different. Even identical twins are different from one another. And their needs end up being different. There is no fix-all.
Assist those who need help, but please live and let live.
Hélène Masson, Russell
Bluesfest volunteers went above and beyond
Bad news reporting and strong opinions tend to make headlines. However, good stories are also noteworthy, and I wanted to share a wonderful experience I recently had.
My husbands wallet was lost while we were sightseeing in Ottawa on July 12. He barely had time to miss it when I got a call from a volunteer at Bluesfest (shoutout to Aron), saying that it had been turned in (with everything still in it) and we could pick it up on our way to the show at LeBreton Flats that evening.
I was so impressed with the city, the citizens we met during our visit, the Ottawa Bluesfest event and most of all, the wonderful volunteers who make it all come together.
Joy Douglas, Barrie

Ford lost our votes over LCBO strike

Author: Mason, Kelly

Document 488 of 500

New squabble casts doubt on tentative deal at LCBO; Area beer stores see empty shelves as customers stock up for the weekend

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-squabble-casts-doubt-on-tentative-deal-at/docview/3082873316/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+squabble+casts+doubt+on+tentative+deal+at+LCBO%3B+Area+beer+stores+see+empty+shelves+as+customers+stock+up+for+the+weekend&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As a tentative deal to end a twoweek-long LCBO strike was thrown into question Friday afternoon, store shelves at beer stores in Ottawa looked nearly bare.
Despite trumpeting a tentative agreement to end the strike just hours earlier, the union representing 10,000 LCBO workers announced that the strike would continue because the employer had refused to sign a return-towork protocol.
The LCBO fired back with a statement that it would file an unfair labour practice complaint against the Ontario Public Service Employees Union because it had introduced "significant new monetary demands" after the tentative deal was reached.
The LCBO said in a statement earlier Friday that a tentative agreement had been reached and, if it was ratified, the strike would end at 12:01 a.m. Monday, with stores set to reopen on Tuesday.
Beer Store shelves in Ottawa were lightly stocked or empty in some locations, particularly those that once held imported beers, or products typically purchased at LCBO outlets.
Across the Ottawa River in Gatineau, some SAQ shelves were also sparsely stocked, as Ontarians sought alternative sources for beer, wine and alcohol ahead of a sunny summer weekend. Between 20 and 25 SAQ retailers close to Ottawa had reported increased traffic since the LCBO strike began.
Linda Bouchard, a media representative for the Quebec agency, said spirits and ready-to-drink products had been particularly popular, and "the shelves empty quickly.
"The SAQ had to adjust its operations and supply these branches more frequently to meet demand," she said via email Friday.
OPSEU workers walked off the job at LCBO on July 5. Negotiations resumed Wednesday.
The union said the dispute was largely about Premier Doug Ford's plan to allow convenience and grocery stores to sell ready-to-drink cocktails, saying expanded sales of the beverages would threaten their job security.
The LCBO said that wasn't a matter for the bargaining table.
The last offer it made public included wage increases of seven per cent over three years, a special adjustment for certain warehouse positions, improved access to benefits for casual part-timers, conversion of about 400 casual workers to permanent full-time status and improved severance provisions.
During the strike, the Ford government forged ahead with its alcohol sales expansion plans. Ford sped up the date when grocery stores already licensed to sell beer and wine could add the premixed cocktails and coolers to their offerings.
During the strike, the LCBO was fulfilling orders online and for licensees such as bars and restaurants, but those venues said their supplies were becoming strained.
Maximilien Roy, vice-president of Restaurants Canada, said restaurants and bars in Ottawa and across Ontario were "breathing a sigh of relief" with news of the tentative agreement, but then the deal was thrown into question.
"We're crossing fingers all the orders from the LCBO to their suppliers go well in the coming weeks, so it doesn't last, the reduced selection of products," he said.
Roy said bars and restaurants had been placing orders for their wine, beer and spirits via an online distribution system, but there was far less selection than usual and products were selling out quickly. On top of that, some restaurants and bars have reported waits of up to 10 days for product deliveries, he added.
"Things are shaping up and we've seen improvements since last week," he said. "We're grateful for the Ontario government and LCBO for what they've been doing, helping small biz like restaurants and bars."
Ottawa-based restaurants and bars in particular have been busy during the strike, thanks to support from local patrons, Roy said. Postmedia News With files from The Canadian Press

Document 489 of 500

LCBO isn't broken so why privatize?

Author: Bowering, Ken

Publication info: Bowering, Ken. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-isnt-broken-so-why-privatize/docview/3082873282/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+isn%27t+broken+so+why+privatize%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=Bowering%2C+Ken&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: The LCBO union is losing, July 11. Randall Denley seems to approach the issue as a wannabe politician, supporting Doug Ford's desire to open up the alcohol market to every convenience store in Ontario. But it's not, in any way, a form of "retail competition."
In fact, in his final paragraph he suggests the government should divest itself entirely from the alcohol business. Well, alcohol is not just like "any other product."
If the sale of alcohol products is opened up as Denley and Ford are proposing, I fear that the benefits now provided by the LCBO will disappear. This will lead to our choice of beverages being made by some unknown store owner, much as big grocery stores now decide which brands we consumers can choose from. And, our overall costs will increase.
Adhere to the adage, "If it ain't broke, don't fix it." And, the LCBO isn't broke.

Subject: Alcohol; Liquor

Identifier / keyword: union; losing; randall; denley; seems; approach

Document 490 of 500

Remember the C stands for Control

Author: Parnas, Dave

Publication info: Parnas, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/remember-c-stands-control/docview/3082873053/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Remember+the+C+stands+for+Control&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=Parnas%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: Union turns up pressure in LCBO strike, July 10. Discussions of the LCBO strike usually focus on jobs and "convenience" while ignoring the "C" in LCBO. The "C" does not stand for "cost" or "convenience." It stands for "control." The LCBO was not established to create good jobs or to earn cash for the provincial government. It was established for the purpose of trying to ameliorate some health and social problems that are associated with alcohol.
The LCBO was created when Prohibition was abolished. Prohibition was a misguided attempt to solve real problems. Many people in our society find it difficult to control their consumption of alcohol. If they drink a little, they drink too much. This often results in intimate-partner abuse, child abuse, drunk driving and collisions. However, Prohibition failed miserably. Instead of solving the alcohol problem, it created a crime problem.
When Prohibition ended in Ontario, the government turned to a different method of trying to ameliorate the problems caused by inappropriate consumption of alcohol. The LCBO was designed to make alcohol available, but to control its consumption in the hope of reducing its abuse. Legislators noted that profit-oriented organizations are motivated to sell alcohol to minors, people who are already drunk and people who regularly consume an excessive amount of alcohol; they believed that a government organization would not be incited to do those things and would train its employees to try to reduce the abuse of alcohol. People who were entitled to consume alcohol were given a card to identify them. Records were kept of their consumption and it was hoped that potential over-drinking and criminal activities would be detected and reduced.
The original plan has long been forgotten. Bars, restaurants and brewers oppose the idea of restricting consumption. Retail stores see selling alcohol as a source of profits. Most important, the LCBO has become a "cash cow" for the Ontario government. Convenience and cash have replaced control in most people's minds.
Since the LCBO was created, science has advanced. Research has shown that alcohol is never beneficial for the consumer's health. We now know that when it comes to alcohol consumption, there is no safe amount. This new knowledge makes the original goal of the LCBO worth reconsideration.
It would be foolish to think that we can end drinking or even substantially reduce it. However, the government should not be in the business of encouraging alcohol consumption. When I see Doug Ford (who I am told does not drink alcohol) appearing in advertisements encouraging drinking, I am horrified. What legitimate reason is there for spending government money to encourage people to consume harmful substances?

Subject: Alcohol

Identifier / keyword: union; turns; pressure; strike; discussions; usually

Document 491 of 500

Empty shelves at Ottawa beer stores as doubt surrounds deal to end LCBO strike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/empty-shelves-at-ottawa-beer-stores-as-doubt/docview/3082838712/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Empty+shelves+at+Ottawa+beer+stores+as+doubt+surrounds+deal+to+end+LCBO+strike&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As a tentative deal to end a two-week-long LCBO strike was thrown into question Friday afternoon, store shelves at beer stores in Ottawa looked nearly bare.
Despite trumpeting a tentative agreement to end the strike just hours earlier, the union representing 10,000 LCBO workers announced that the strike would continue because the employer had refused to sign a return-to-work protocol.
The LCBO fired back with a statement that it would file an unfair labour practice complaint against the Ontario Public Service Employees Union because it had introduced "significant new monetary demands" after the tentative deal was reached.
The LCBO said in a statement earlier Friday that a tentative agreement had been reached and, if it was ratified, the strike would end at 12:01 a.m. Monday, with stores set to reopen on Tuesday.
Beer Store shelves in Ottawa were lightly stocked or empty in some locations, particularly those that once held imported beers, or products typically purchased at LCBO outlets.
Across the Ottawa River in Gatineau, some SAQ shelves were also sparsely stocked, as Ontarians sought alternative sources for beer, wine and alcohol ahead of a sunny summer weekend.
Between 20 and 25 SAQ retailers close to Ottawa had reported increased traffic since the LCBO strike began.
Linda Bouchard, a media representative for the Quebec agency, said spirits and ready-to-drink products had been particularly popular, and "the shelves empty quickly.
"The SAQ had to adjust its operations and supply these branches more frequently to meet demand," she said via email Friday.
The closed LCBO store on Isabella Street in central Ottawa on Friday..
OPSEU workers walked off the job at LCBO on July 5. Negotiations resumed Wednesday.
The union said the dispute was largely about Premier Doug Ford's plan to allow convenience and grocery stores to sell ready-to-drink cocktails, saying expanded sales of the beverages would threaten their job security.
The LCBO said that wasn't a matter for the bargaining table.
The last offer it made public included wage increases of seven per cent over three years, a special adjustment for certain warehouse positions, improved access to benefits for casual part-timers, conversion of about 400 casual workers to permanent full-time status and improved severance provisions.
During the strike, the Ford government forged ahead with its alcohol sales expansion plans. Ford sped up the date when grocery stores already licensed to sell beer and wine could add the pre-mixed cocktails and coolers to their offerings.
During the strike, the LCBO was fulfilling orders online and for licensees such as bars and restaurants, but those venues said their supplies were becoming strained.
Maximilien Roy, vice-president of Restaurants Canada, said restaurants and bars in Ottawa and across Ontario were "breathing a sigh of relief" with news of the tentative agreement, but then the deal was thrown into question.
"We're crossing fingers all the orders from the LCBO to their suppliers goes well in the coming weeks, so it doesn't last, the reduced selection of products," he said.
Roy said bars and restaurants had been placing orders for their wine, beer and spirits via an online distribution system, but there was far less selection than usual and products were selling out quickly. On top of that, some restaurants and bars have reported waits of up to 10 days for product deliveries, he added.
"Things are shaping up and we've seen improvements since last week," he said. "We're grateful for the Ontario government and LCBO for what they've been doing, helping small biz like restaurants and bars."
Ottawa-based restaurants and bars in particular have been busy during the strike, thanks to support from local patrons, Roy said.
With files from The Canadian Press

Police watchdog clears Ottawa officer who shot woman in March
UPDATE: Ottawa airport, Porter hit by fallout from Microsoft crash

Document 492 of 500

Today's letters: LCBO workers are picketing for the good of the province

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-lcbo-workers-are-picketing-good/docview/3082544160/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+LCBO+workers+are+picketing+for+the+good+of+the+province&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LCBO profits help pay for services
LCBO workers are striking for something bigger than just their paycheques. They're stepping up for all of us in Ontario.
The LCBO isn't just any store. It's a golden goose for our province, bringing in a whopping $2.58 billion in profit before taxes this year alone. That money's essential because it helps pay for the services we all need, like hospitals and schools.
Now, Premier Doug Ford wants to sell ready-to-drink cocktails at big-box stores, grocery stores and corner stores. He says it's all about making our lives easier, but it could actually mess with our province's wallet.
If this happens, the government might have to hike taxes or slash budgets for services. This could mean less money for our schools and hospitals that are already scraping by.
The LCBO strike is a heads-up about what we could lose if we allow Ford's plan to go forward.
Pamela Burns King, Cornwall
Ford's decision rewards foreign companies
With the LCBO strike bringing focus to changes in alcohol distribution in Ontario, it is worth noting that Premier Ford's rush to end the exclusive arrangement with The Beer Store a year ahead of schedule will cost taxpayers an announced $225 million - about $15 from the pocket of each and every person in Ontario.
It will be paid to the foreign owners of the Beer Store: Molson Coors (50.9 per cent or $114,525,000) Labatt Brewing (subsidiary of AB InBev: 44.9 per cent or $101,025,000) and Sleeman (Subsidiary of Sapporo: 4.2 per cent or $9,450,000). None of them appear to be listed on any Canadian stock exchange or have any Canadian connection other than owning historic brand names and a few manufacturing plants.
Gary MacDonald, Ottawa
Greater access to alcohol comes with societal costs
Recent revisions to guidelines for alcohol consumption say no level of alcohol consumption is healthy. So there will likely be significant long-term costs for making alcohol more easily accessible.
Meanwhile, the latest numbers show over 2.5 million Ontarians don't have a family doctor and an additional 600,000 have to drive great distances to see theirs.
Seems like misguided priorities to me.
Stephen Godfrey, Ottawa
Closed Driveway is a delight
As residents living next to Queen Elizabeth Driveway, we're delighted with its closure for the summer for recreational purposes. The addition this year of picnic tables and activities, such as miniature golf, is a bonus. Three generations of our extended family regularly utilize these facilities.
If it takes us a few minutes more to drive somewhere in the city because of the closure (which so far has not been our experience), it seems a small price to pay for the joy it brings us and our family, and to other residents of our community who are enjoying this wonderful recreational resource.
George and Pat McLaughlin, Ottawa

Ford's plan rewards foreign companies

LCBO profits help pay for services; Letters to the editor

Document 495 of 500

Ontario's groundbreaking journalism plan; Local media needs support at all levels

Author: Taube, Michael

Publication info: Taube, Michael. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarios-groundbreaking-journalism-plan-local/docview/3082051477/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+groundbreaking+journalism+plan%3B+Local+media+needs+support+at+all+levels&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Taube%2C+Michael&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recently, Ontario's Progressive Conservative government announced a plan to help provide financial support for newspaper publishers in the province. The best part about it: it doesn't come at any cost to the taxpayers.
Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy sent letters to several provincial Crown corporations, including the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation, Ontario Cannabis Retail Corporation and the Liquor Control Board of Ontario. These agencies were directed to allocate one-quarter of their annual advertising budget, which is reportedly over $100 million, to Ontario's news publishers by Sept. 3.
This means there will be roughly $25 million available for news organizations in Ontario, including the National Post's parent company, Postmedia Network Inc., to fund local journalism.
"These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province," reads Bethlenfalvy's letter. It also highlights the Ontario government's belief there is a "compelling opportunity to leverage the advertising spends of Ontario's major government agencies to support Ontario workers and content."
This is a creative and forward-thinking economic plan by Premier Doug Ford and the Ontario PCs. It will help ensure that a strong focus on local news content will be maintained, and significant job opportunities will be available for young journalists and established veterans alike. Most importantly, it will play a vital role in the survival of Ontario newspapers.
The reality we all have to face is that there's going to be some level of financial involvement by federal and provincial governments in Canada's news industry, both now and in the future.
The vast majority of our newspapers, magazines and news websites currently receive some type of public funding. That being said, the use of direct subsidies can't be the only funding lifeline for Canadian publications, including newspapers.
Ontario's directive to allocate part of the advertising budget of a number of provincial Crown corporations is, therefore, a step in the right direction.
This advertising budget is, unlike the introduction of direct subsidies, already earmarked and accounted for in annual government expenditures. It's an existing sum rather than an additional source of taxpayer money. Hence, the $25 million allocated for Ontario publications simply involves shifting one-quarter of the budget, while the remaining three-quarters can be used as the Crown corporations see fit.
The Ford government's policy will allow government agencies to continue to get their messages across to Ontarians, while investing in local journalism. The money is to be used for Ontario-based newspapers, news content and journalists working in the profession.
This funding directive isn't being handed out with no strings attached, either. A review will apparently be conducted on a quarterly basis. If the Ontario government feels that the main objectives of "promoting local content and culture and supporting Ontario jobs" aren't being met, it will consider withdrawing the funding altogether. Hence, it's up to Ontario newspapers to ensure that the money is being used effectively, wisely and, above all, properly.
Andrew MacLeod, Postmedia's president and chief executive officer, said, "I'm certainly extremely grateful to the leadership of Premier Ford and his team. My hope is this will serve as an example, a north star, not just for other provincial governments, but for the federal government and for major corporations."
I agree with MacLeod's sentiment wholeheartedly. There are ways for the public and private sectors to help and support Canadian journalism that don't involve direct subsidies.
Ford and the Ontario PCs have identified one financial path to success. There are many others still to be considered and utilized.
National Post

Subject: Journalism; Government agencies; Advertising; Newspaper industry; Funding; Subsidies

Business indexing term: Subject: Advertising Newspaper industry Subsidies

Company / organization: Name: National Post; NAICS: 513110

Document 496 of 500

Bennett: School smartphone bans alone won't fix teens' social media addiction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bennett-school-smartphone-bans-alone-wont-fix/docview/3080220715/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bennett%3A+School+smartphone+bans+alone+won%27t+fix+teens%27+social+media+addiction&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Social psychologist Jonathan Haidt's international bestseller, The Anxious Generation (2024), may be what is commonly known as a "game changer." It has awakened us to the rise of "phone-based childhood" and its role as a prime contributor to our contemporary child and youth mental-health crisis
What was, until recently, a battle waged by education authorities and schools to limit, control or eliminate cellphones in classrooms has been recast as one of the most urgent social issues of our time. Now U.S. Surgeon General Vivek M. Murthy has weighed in, identifying social media as addictive and proposing health risk warning labels on social media carriers.
Banning or severely restricting cellphones in class is the latest digital copycat trend in Canadian K-12 education. Seven provinces have adopted, or are in the process of adopting, policies that seek to ban or restrict mobile devices during instructional time: Ontario, Quebec, British Columbia, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Manitoba and Alberta.
Upon closer scrutiny, most of the so-called "bans" are more like "patches" to block student access during the school day, aimed mostly at only one dimension of the problem: reducing the constant stream of texts, videos and notifications that make it next-to-impossible to teach classes.
Restricting cellphones in class is simply a "quick fix" and, in isolation, may suffer the same fate as school-based curbs dating back to 2007 and the appearance of the Apple iPhone and its more affordable alternatives. In the wake of the global pandemic disruption, it's evolved into an ongoing struggle to reclaim the minds of the smartphone generation and to curtail the adverse effects on the mental well-being of children and youth.
Smartphones are essentially the new cigarettes, albeit without the frightening and immediate health risks. Changing a contemporary culture dominated by social media is a daunting challenge. It begins at birth and needs to encompass every stage of human development, most acutely affecting toddlers, tweens, teens and youth up to 24 years of age (considered prolonged adolescence and covering the first phase of post-secondary education).
Since a July 2023 UNESCO report on the harmful effects of mobile technology and the arrival of Haidt's The Anxious Generation, it is now virtually impossible to ignore, but - to-date - the proposed prescriptions do not fully address the need for a broad cross-sectoral change encompassing public health, education, and social and community services. It may well take a mobilization of change forces comparable to that committed to stamping out smoking in public spaces and cessation of tobacco use among teens.
The tipping point in the shift may well be Surgeon General Murthy's June 2024 New York Times declaration in which he identified social media addiction among teens as a mental health emergency and called upon the U.S. Congress to introduce legislation authorizing a "warning label" for social media platforms.
The surgeon general's recommended plan of action is not limited to warning labels. It would encompass federal legislation to "shield" children and youth from "online harassment, abuse and exploitation"; federal and state regulations to require social media companies to share data on health effects and be subject to independent safety audits; and the adoption of mutually supportive policies in the schools and public health domains.
Social critics were quick to pounce on the call for health risk warnings in and of themselves as a "feel good response" that may achieve little in getting teens to "quit" social media. The stunning decline in smoking among adolescents age 16 from 1999 to 2012 - dropping from 48 per cent to 12 per cent - was the result of a dramatic shift in opinion driven by a combination of factors. Initial health warnings, family and peer influence, anti-smoking promotional messages, prohibitions in public spaces, pharmaceutical cessation aids, and substitution activities together completely altered the trajectory. It's an ongoing struggle.
Curbing the detrimental and debilitating influence of social media is the new priority, but it must be achieved without cutting-off access to the internet for older, more mature students. It will require federal public health leadership and should be built upon a child development regulatory framework, embracing evidence-based and age-appropriate research in key fields.
Social media addiction is fast becoming an urgent teen public health priority. Very soon social media will be recognized by the Canadian Mental Health Association, like video game obsession, as a form of addictive child and teen behaviour The emerging "cessation" movement would be well advised to include "warning labels" as part of a broader integrated strategy engaging three systems: health, education and social services. It's far bigger than just education.
Paul W. Bennett, Ed.D., is Director, Schoolhouse Institute, Adjunct Professor of Education, Saint Mary's University, and Chair of researchED Canada. His latest research report, Weapons of Mass Distraction, was published this month by the Macdonald-Laurier Institute.
