Document 497 of 500

Cooper: Why Children's Aid Society workers deserve support on the picket line

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cooper-why-childrens-aid-society-workers-deserve/docview/3079933767/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cooper%3A+Why+Children%27s+Aid+Society+workers+deserve+support+on+the+picket+line&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I went to my first-ever picket line on Tuesday, determined to support my former colleagues at the Children's Aid Society of Ottawa who are on strike trying to stop more cuts, fix a crisis, and keep children safe.
I have worked in child welfare for the last 17 years, 11 of those in child protection with the Children's Aid Society of Ottawa. I know all too well what these workers face, day in and day out.
Child protection workers work in high-stress, often dangerous conditions trying to keep children safe, and keep families together. They spend their days trying to keep up with desperate calls for help from families in crisis, and on the road and in the homes of these families who are in deep, emotional distress and trying to survive trauma, violence, mental-health challenges and addiction.
The job very often puts the workers themselves in dangerous situations - at times facing violent, aggressive behaviour and navigating conflict, all the while making very difficult choices about how best to protect the children caught in the middle.
The Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services makes the job so much harder, drowning workers in bureaucratic red tape: the never-ending and ever-increasing box-ticking demanded by the ministry has workers recording every minute of their day, every phone call, every home visit and every meeting. None of this helps provide real, on-the-ground safety for children. And it all gets in the way of the follow-up work that does: writing multiple assessments, safety plans, case notes and lengthy referrals to request help from outside agencies, and advocating to multiple community partners to support these families' complex needs. And these partners all face the same challenges in a lack of funding to provide the services needed in our community.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford's callous approach to the entire care sector - whether it's child protection, home care, child care, or health care - is perpetuating a crisis that is putting everyone at risk. I dare him to spend just one day in the shoes of a child protection worker. I doubt he could make it through one family visit.
The ministry says it's spending millions of dollars on Children's Aid Societies but fails to explain to the public how and where this money is spent. Workers on the ground know from experience that the poor, archaic funding model does not reflect any understanding of how Children's Aid Societies actually work. The funding is not going where it needs to go: supporting the workers who are working to keep children with their families and in their communities.
Cutting funding and jobs will just make this crisis worse.
As for Ford's cheerleaders, who blame the strike for putting children at risk, you can't imagine the reality of this crisis. Ford should spend less time thinking about "buck a beer" schemes and more time learning about what it takes to keep children safe.
I suspect this government thinks it can starve these workers back to work, and if it succeeds, it won't be good for our children or society. So I encourage you to do what I did: show support and attend the picket lines, and do what Ford seems so reluctant to do: listen to the workers so you really understand what their jobs entail.
Natalie Cooper worked as a front-line child protection worker for the Children's Aid Society of Ottawa from 2012 to 2023.
Related
Pellerin: Diagolon debacle - be wary of political extremists at all levels
MacDougall: Canada's weak defence spending is losing us friends globally

Document 498 of 500

Keep ad spending local; News industry

Author: Sharpe, Peter

Publication info: Sharpe, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/keep-ad-spending-local-news-industry/docview/3079897422/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Keep+ad+spending+local%3B+News+industry&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Sharpe%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Please forgive this recitation of a long-held and hopefully not controversial belief: a strong, free press is a pillar of any strong democracy. That's certainly been my experience through my career in business, and I've had a frontrow seat to its impact in my role on the board of Postmedia, on which I've had the privilege to serve since the company's inception.
Our team, the largest group of journalists in Canada not employed by the CBC, does the same thing every day: it puts its head down and focuses on our mission to deliver ambitious, trusted and high-quality journalism.
To say the last 15 years has been transformational in our industry is to put it lightly. The business model that sustained journalism for centuries has been dramatically disrupted. In Canada, foreign tech giants like Alphabet (Google) and Meta (Facebook) currently control around 80 per cent of the online advertising market but contribute little in the way of employment or taxes. (While, in some cases, increasing access to disinformation that can actively harm our democracy.)
Disruption can happen in any industry, and businesses are responsible for sustaining their own futures. That includes Postmedia, and that's a responsibility that we take seriously. But the core of our business is journalism - and it's a business that sustains a strong democracy.
When a pillar of democracy is at stake, it's incumbent upon everyone in a position of power to ask what they can do to help and be open to new structures and paradigms. Great leaders step up.
That's why I was delighted when Ontario's Progressive Conservative government announced its plan to support journalism in the province by devoting a quarter of its advertising budget, or roughly $25 million a year, to Ontario publishers. This will not just benefit Postmedia, but all publishers that provide important coverage of local issues throughout the province.
As part of this, Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy directed several provincial Crown corporations - including Metrolinx, the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation, Ontario Cannabis Retail Corporation and the Liquor Control Board of Ontario - to have plans in place to implement this directive by the beginning of September.
This is an example of true leadership by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, and governments in other jurisdictions across the country should follow suit. But please forgive me for reciting another long-held belief: a strong country with strong institutions is also the foundation for success in business, and the growth of great corporations. That's why corporate Canada also has a crucial role to play by putting its own oar in the water to support Canadian media.
Canada's business leaders and companies, which form the nucleus of our private sector, have the ability and the financial wherewithal to help strengthen our domestic news media industry, while growing their own businesses through advertising that reaches their target audiences.
Research from News Media Canada shows that more than 80 per cent of Canadians read newspaper content each week, whether in print or online, and upwards of 60 per cent read it on a daily basis.
By diverting a greater portion of their ad budgets away from foreign companies and towards local publishers that for decades have been a critical part of Canada's diverse communities, corporate Canada has an opportunity to reach its customers while supporting a vibrant news industry and helping to ensure the long-term viability of Canada's free press.
It's a win-win for everyone involved.
Postmedia Network Peter Sharpe is chair of the board of Postmedia Network Inc.

Subject: Journalism; Leadership; News media; Advertising; Democracy; Newspaper industry; Advertising expenditures

Document 499 of 500

Crowd the extremists out of our politics; What the people of Carp did in reaction to Diagolon is what all of us need to do

Author: Pellerin, Brigitte

Publication info: Pellerin, Brigitte. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crowd-extremists-out-our-politics-what-people/docview/3079897031/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crowd+the+extremists+out+of+our+politics%3B+What+the+people+of+Carp+did+in+reaction+to+Diagolon+is+what+all+of+us+need+to+do&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=Pellerin%2C+Brigitte&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You need a lot of valuable skills to manage an organization like the Carp Agricultural Society. Nobody would expect that list to include "being able to spot political extremists from a mile away." Yet it is increasingly necessary to equip ourselves for this new reality.
As you may have heard, the Carp organization inadvertently rented a hall to Diagolon. For the hitherto blissfully unaware, this is a loose group of extreme right-wingers whose leader once "joked" about raping Pierre Poilievre's wife, which prompted the Conservative leader - despite having previously been photographed perhaps unknowingly shaking the group leader's hand - to call the lot of them "dirtbags" and "losers."
I heartily sympathize.
There are plenty of well-documented reasons to avoid shaking hands with this character, even before he laughed on camera about sexual assault. But I wouldn't expect people whose lives are spent in productive pursuits outside the sewers of Twitter to be as extremist-aware as your favourite columnist is.
"We didn't know who they were when they booked," Jean Sullivan, co-president of the Carp Agricultural Society, told the media. "It's not within our values or mission. It was an unfortunate series of events."
Nobody can blame Sullivan for having been hoodwinked, especially as the Diagolon group didn't go out of its way to disclose who they were or what their purpose was. "From my understanding, they did not tell us what the organization was and I had never heard of them before Saturday night," Sullivan added.
I'm not as ready to absolve Goldie Ghamari, MPP for Carleton, who was kicked out of Doug Ford's Conservative caucus over a meeting with U.K. far-right extremist Tommy Robinson. Politicians have staff to do research for them and warn them not to meet someone like Robinson who, as Randall Denley reminded Citizen readers earlier this week, has earned himself "various criminal convictions" for his activities.
At this point, you may be reading this and wondering, "Is it me, or is there a noticeable increase in run-ins with unsavoury extremists?" It's not you, and it's not just in Canada either. In France, voters got a nasty scare after the first round of legislative elections yielded a victory for Marine Le Pen's extremist National Rally party, with 33 per cent support. After the second round of voting this past Sunday, the result was entirely different. The far-right party was not the winner; French voters gave first place to a brand-new coalition of four left-wing parties. Voters held their noses and cast their ballots for whichever candidate had the best chance of beating the National Rally contender.
As David Broder noted in the New York Times, one of the most remarkable developments in that second round of voting was how leftwing voters in contests where the two leading candidates were President Emmanuel Macron's centrist candidate or the far-right candidate, backed the centrist or abstained from voting.
The result may prove to be an ungovernable mess, but at least in the immediate future, French voters avoided electing a far-right party. Not that left-wing extremists would have been better, in case you were wondering.
Extremism is always bad in politics, and the trick is for the rest of us who are scattered across the regular political spectrum to occupy as much space as possible so that extremists have nowhere to grow. The reaction of the people of Carp who objected strenuously and publicly to Diagolon being in their lovely community is exactly what we need to do.
Despite our disagreements on the best ways to achieve desirable goals, the vast majority of us agree on the fundamentals that make for a good and decent society. Individual rights (very much including reproductive rights), the rule of law and respect for our shared institutions of governance. And a baseline of properly financed social services such as health and education.
What we agree on is what makes this country such a desirable place to live.
It's worth protecting it against extremists of all kinds. Brigitte Pellerin (they/them) is an Ottawa writer.

Subject: Extremism; Sex crimes; Voters; Rape; Society; Elections

Ford's priorities are completely wrong

Author: Sampson, Scott A

Publication info: Sampson, Scott A. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-priorities-are-completely-wrong/docview/3079696911/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+priorities+are+completely+wrong&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Sampson%2C+Scott+A&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Increased access to diagnostic imaging in Ontario would lead to faster diagno-sis, improved and shorter treatment periods and better outcomes. This would also reduce the burden on the health-care system and the cost per patient.
While there is little doubt the blame for inefficiencies can be laid at the feet of successive governments, the current Ontario government under Doug Ford seems uninterested in anything other than tailgate parties, cheap beer and increased access to alcohol.
A conservative estimate to double the current number of machines would be a capital expenditure of $7 billion. For perspective, this is about 5.5 years of the lost revenues from the LCBO and the licence plate fees.
Of course, Ford will beg the federal government for help - which basically means it appears on your federal taxes instead of provincial. Hardly a savings.
The estimated $250 million to $300 million in lost revenues from the LCBO won't be a savings for the taxpayer either. Large corporations like the Westons will appreciate their increased profits - as they blame governments for increased retail prices.
We need leadership: people who understand that "service" isn't about getting elected (or re-elected) but what you do when you are there. Often service requires sacrifice. Ford and his people prioritize themselves.
